<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Define the vanishing gradient problem. Mention two initialization strategies to mitigate it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs during backpropagation in deep neural networks when the gradients of the loss with respect to early-layer weights become extremely small (close to zero). This happens because repeated multiplication of small derivatives (e.g., sigmoid derivative $\le 0.25$) and small weights causes the gradient product to shrink exponentially with depth. Consequently, early layers learn extremely slowly or stop learning entirely.

**Two initialization strategies:**
1. **Xavier (Glorot) Initialization:** $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 2/(n_{in}+n_{out}))$ — keeps variance stable for tanh/sigmoid.
2. **He Initialization:** $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 2/n_{in})$ — specifically designed for ReLU, compensating for the half-killed activation.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
*What is "dying ReLU"? How is it solved by Leaky ReLU?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **dying ReLU** neuron is one whose pre-activation is non-positive for every input in the training set. Since $\mathrm{ReLU}'(x) = 0$ for $x \le 0$, the gradient w.r.t. its weights becomes exactly zero and the neuron can never recover — it permanently outputs 0.

**Leaky ReLU** defines:

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} x & x > 0 \\ \alpha x & x \le 0 \end{cases}$$

with small $\alpha$ (typically $0.01$). Its derivative is $\alpha$ (not zero) on the negative side, so a small non-zero gradient flows back even for inactive neurons, allowing recovery. This eliminates the dying-ReLU pathology.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students lose marks by **listing remedies without explaining the cause**. Always derive/explain *why* the issue arises before listing fixes.
> - Don't write "use ReLU" alone — mention **He initialization** alongside, or the remedy is incomplete.
> - In numericals involving variance propagation, forgetting to square terms or ignoring the ReLU halving factor costs 2–3 marks.
> - For BatchNorm questions, students often forget that $\gamma$ and $\beta$ are learnable — examiners specifically test this.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain with mathematical justification why deep networks with sigmoid activation suffer from the vanishing gradient problem. Show that gradient magnitude is bounded by an exponentially decaying factor. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the He initialization variance $\mathrm{Var}(W) = 2/n_{in}$ from first principles, clearly stating all assumptions. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

Consider an $L$-layer feed-forward network. At layer $l$, $a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)})$, $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)}a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$, where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid.

By the chain rule, the gradient of loss w.r.t. $z^{(1)}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(L)}}\prod_{l=2}^{L}\frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial z^{(l-1)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(L)}}\prod_{l=2}^{L}\left(W^{(l)}\odot\sigma'(z^{(l)})\right)$$

Taking norms:

$$\left\Vert\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(1)}}\right\Vert \le \left\Vert\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(L)}}\right\Vert\prod_{l=2}^{L}\Vert W^{(l)}\Vert\cdot\max\sigma'(z)$$

Since $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) \le 0.25$ with maximum at $z=0$:

$$\left\Vert\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(1)}}\right\Vert \le \left\Vert\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(L)}}\right\Vert\prod_{l=2}^{L}\Vert W^{(l)}\Vert\cdot(0.25)$$

If $\Vert W^{(l)}\Vert < 4$ for any layer, the product $\le (0.25 \Vert W\Vert)^{L-1}\to 0$ as $L\to\infty$ exponentially. **Hence the gradient at the first layer vanishes exponentially with depth.**

This means early-layer weights update negligibly per step, while later layers update normally — a **slow / stalled learning** in shallow layers.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

Setup: For layer $l$ with $n_{in}$ inputs:

$$z_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n_{in}} W_{ji}\,a_i$$

**Assumptions:**
- $W_{ji}$ and $a_i$ are independent.
- $\mathbb{E}[W_{ji}] = 0$, $\mathbb{E}[a_i] = 0$.
- $W_{ji}$ i.i.d. with variance $\mathrm{Var}(W)$.
- $b = 0$.

**Step 1 — Variance of pre-activation:**

$$\mathrm{Var}(z_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_{in}}\mathrm{Var}(W_{ji}\,a_i) = n_{in}\,\mathrm{Var}(W)\,\mathrm{Var}(a)$$

**Step 2 — Effect of ReLU on variance.** For $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_z^2)$, $a = \max(0,z)$:

$$\mathrm{Var}(a) = \mathbb{E}[a^2] - (\mathbb{E}[a])^2 = \tfrac{\sigma_z^2}{2} - \tfrac{\sigma_z^2}{2\pi} = \tfrac{\sigma_z^2}{2}(1 - \tfrac{1}{\pi})$$

Using the practical approximation $\mathrm{Var}(a) \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{Var}(z)$:

$$\mathrm{Var}(a^{(l)}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,n_{in}\,\mathrm{Var}(W)\,\mathrm{Var}(a^{(l-1)})$$

**Step 3 — Set equal for stability:**
$$\tfrac{1}{2}\,n_{in}\,\mathrm{Var}(W) = 1 \Rightarrow \boxed{\mathrm{Var}(W) = \dfrac{2}{n_{in}}}$$

So weights are initialized as $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 2/n_{in})$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the Internal Covariate Shift problem and derive the Batch Normalization forward and update equations. Discuss why BN enables higher learning rates. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the various activation functions (sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, Leaky ReLU, ELU) for deep networks with respect to gradient behavior. Recommend a strategy combining activation, initialization, and normalization for a 50-layer CNN. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Internal Covariate Shift (ICS):**
During training, as parameters of earlier layers update, the distribution of inputs to subsequent layers changes. This forces deeper layers to continuously adapt to a shifting input distribution, slowing training and requiring careful initialization and small learning rates.

**Batch Normalization Forward Pass:**
For mini-batch $\mathcal{B} = \{x_1,\ldots,x_m\}$:

$$\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}x_i,\qquad \sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^{2} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i-\mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2$$

$$\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^{2}+\epsilon}}$$

$$y_i = \gamma\hat{x}_i + \beta$$

$\gamma, \beta$ are learnable parameters; $\epsilon$ avoids division by zero.

**Inference:** Use running averages $\hat{\mu} = \mathbb{E}[\mu_{\mathcal{B}}]$, $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \mathrm{Var}[\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2]$ computed during training.

**Why BN allows higher learning rates:**
- Normalization keeps activations in a stable range, preventing explosion/vanishing.
- Reduces "internal covariate shift," making the loss surface smoother.
- Acts as mild regularization through mini-batch noise, reducing the need for dropout.
- Smooth loss landscape allows larger gradient steps without divergence.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Comparison Table:**

| Activation | Range | Derivative Max | Gradient Issue | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid | (0,1) | 0.25 | Severe vanishing | Output (binary) |
| Tanh | (-1,1) | 1 | Mild vanishing | Hidden (shallow) |
| ReLU | [0,∞) | 1 | Dying ReLU | Hidden (default) |
| Leaky ReLU | (-∞,∞) | 1 or $\alpha$ | None (small $\alpha$) | Hidden (deep) |
| ELU | (-1,∞) | 1 or $\alpha e^x$ | None; zero-mean | Hidden (deep) |

**Recommended Strategy for 50-Layer CNN:**

1. **Activation:** **Leaky ReLU** (or ELU/PReLU) for all hidden layers — eliminates both vanishing and dying problems.
2. **Initialization:** **He initialization** with $\mathrm{Var}(W)=2/n_{in}$ — matches ReLU variance scaling.
3. **Normalization:** **Batch Normalization** after each convolution and before activation (or after — both work; "BN before activation" is theoretically preferred).
4. **Optimizer:** **Adam** ($\eta = 10^{-3}$, $\beta_1 = 0.9$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$) with **gradient clipping** at norm 5.
5. **Learning-rate schedule:** **Warmup (5 epochs)** followed by **cosine annealing**.
6. **Regularization:** **Dropout (p = 0.3)** in fully-connected head + **weight decay $10^{-4}$**.
7. **Skip connections (ResNet style):** $\mathbf{a}^{(l+1)} = f(\mathbf{a}^{(l)}) + \mathbf{a}^{(l)}$ every 2 layers to enable gradient flow across 50 layers.

This combination has been validated empirically on ImageNet-scale 50+ layer networks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Vanishing Gradient:** Sigmoid derivative $\le 0.25$; product over many layers $\to 0$. Cure: ReLU + He init + BN.
- **Exploding Gradient:** Large weight norms amplified through layers. Cure: Gradient clipping + small LR + BN.
- **Dying ReLU:** Pre-activation permanently $\le 0$. Cure: Leaky ReLU with $\alpha \approx 0.01$.
- **Xavier init** (Glorot): $\mathrm{Var}(W) = 2/(n_{in}+n_{out})$ — for **tanh/sigmoid**.
- **He init:** $\mathrm{Var}(W) = 2/n_{in}$ — for **ReLU** family (factor of 2 compensates for ReLU killing half).
- **Batch Normalization:** Normalizes activations to zero mean, unit variance per mini-batch; learnable $\gamma, \beta$; running averages at inference.
- **Internal Covariate Shift:** Distribution shift of layer inputs during training — BN mitigates this.
- **Saddle Points:** Exponentially more common than local minima in high dimensions; momentum and noise (SGD) help escape.
- **Gradient Clipping:** If $\Vert \mathbf{g}\Vert > T$, scale down — prevents explosion in RNNs and transformers.
- **Learning-Rate Schedules:** Warmup → Cosine/Step decay; critical for transformer-style models.
- **Engineering Pipeline:** ReLU + He + BN + Adam + Warmup-Cosine + (optional) Skip Connections is the **modern default**.
- **Variance Propagation:** $\mathrm{Var}(y) = n_{in}\mathrm{Var}(W)\mathrm{Var}(x)$ for independent zero-mean inputs.
- **Dropout:** Randomly zero activations during training; invert at test time. Regularization technique.
- **Weight Decay (L2):** Adds $\lambda\Vert\theta\Vert^2$ to loss; reduces overfitting.
- **KTU-Specific Tip:** Always write the **mathematical cause** before the **remedy** in long-answer questions — examiners allocate marks accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overfitting and Underfitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overfitting and Underfitting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of supervised learning under the **PECDT695 – Deep Learning** syllabus, the generalization behaviour of a neural network / hypothesis function $h_\theta(x)$ is characterized by the deviation between its **empirical risk** (training error) and its **expected risk** (true population error).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overfitting** is the phenomenon where a learned model $h_\theta(x)$ achieves an excessively low training error $\hat{E}_{train}$ but a substantially higher generalization error $E_{gen}$ on unseen test data, because it has memorized statistical noise and idiosyncrasies of the training set rather than learning the underlying functional mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Underfitting** is the dual phenomenon where the model $h_\theta(x)$ is too constrained (insufficient capacity, overly strong prior, or undertrained) to capture even the dominant structural patterns in the data, resulting in high error on **both** the training set and the test set.

Mathematically, for a hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$ and true target function $g(x)$, the **generalization gap** is defined as:

$$\delta_{gen} \;=\; E_{gen} - \hat{E}_{train}$$

A model is said to **overfit** when $\delta_{gen} \gg 0$ with $\hat{E}_{train} \approx 0$, and to **underfit** when both $E_{gen}$ and $\hat{E}_{train}$ remain large.

## 1.2 The Three Regimes of Learning (Intuitive Overview)

A model can be in one of three regimes with respect to the **model capacity** $C(\mathcal{H})$ (e.g., number of parameters, depth, width):

| Regime | Training Error $\hat{E}_{train}$ | Test Error $E_{test}$ | Model Capacity | Symptom |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Underfitting** | High | High | Too Low | High Bias |
| **Good Fit (Sweet Spot)** | Low | Low (≈ Training) | Optimal | Balanced |
| **Overfitting** | Very Low / ≈ 0 | High | Too High | High Variance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Capacity** $C(\mathcal{H})$ of a hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$ is its ability to fit a wide variety of functions. Capacity grows with: number of layers, neurons per layer, polynomial degree, and effective parameter count.

## 1.3 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

**Analogy 1 – The Rote-Learning Student vs. The Lazy Student:**

Imagine a classroom preparing for an exam based on a textbook containing 100 solved problems.

- **Underfitting** is like a **lazy student** who only reads the chapter title and sleeps. He cannot solve *any* of the 100 problems correctly (high training error), and naturally also fails the unseen exam questions (high test error). His model is too simple.
- **Overfitting** is like a **rote-memorizing student** who memorizes the *exact wording* of all 100 solutions, including the arithmetic mistakes, the typos, and the page numbers. He gets near-perfect score on the 100 training problems (training error ≈ 0), but when the examiner changes the numbers or phrasing slightly, he fails miserably (high test error).
- **Good Fit** is the **intelligent student** who understands the underlying principle (e.g., Newton's 2nd law) and can apply it to both the practice and the unseen problems.

**Analogy 2 – Tailoring a Suit:**

- **Underfit suit**: A rectangle sheet draped on the body — fits nobody.
- **Good suit**: Standard tailoring that matches the body shape with a small tolerance.
- **Overfit suit**: A bespoke suit that fits the *exact* posture, the *exact* breathing pattern, and the *exact* day — the moment you sit differently, it tears.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's 2024 scheme places Overfitting/Underfitting, the **Bias-Variance Decomposition**, and **Regularization** as a triad that is assessed in **almost every semester** under Module 1 (frequently appearing as a 7-mark sub-question in Part B). Mastery of the bias-variance tradeoff, learning curves, and at least two mitigation techniques is essential.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bias-Variance-Noise Decomposition (The Heart of the Topic)

For a regression problem with true target $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is i.i.d. zero-mean noise with variance $\sigma^2$, the **expected prediction error** of a learned hypothesis $\hat{f}(x)$ at a fixed query point $x$ can be decomposed as:

$$E\Big[\big(y - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\Big] = \underbrace{\big(f(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)]\big)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{E\Big[\big(\hat{f}(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)]\big)^2\Big]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

Where:
- **Bias²**: The squared error between the *expected* prediction of the model and the *true* function. High bias ⇒ underfitting.
- **Variance**: How much the model's prediction fluctuates across different training sets drawn from the same distribution. High variance ⇒ overfitting.
- **$\sigma^2$ (Irreducible Error)**: The Bayes error / inherent noise in the data; cannot be removed by any model.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The model lifecycle as a function of capacity $C(\mathcal{H})$ follows a deterministic pattern:

1. **At very low capacity** — the model class is too restrictive; it cannot represent the true function $f(x)$ ⇒ **Bias² dominates**, and variance is small. Training error is high.
2. **As capacity grows** — the model class begins to approximate $f(x)$ more closely; Bias² falls, while variance starts to grow. The total error (Bias² + Variance) reaches a **minimum** — this is the **sweet spot**.
3. **At very high capacity** — the model class can now interpolate noisy training points; **Variance explodes**, Bias² is small, total error rises again. Training error falls to ≈ 0 but test error climbs.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Engineering / ML Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generalization Error | $E_{gen} = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}_{test}}\big[\,\mathcal{L}(h_\theta(x),y)\,\big]$ | Performance on unseen data |
| 2 | Empirical / Training Error | $\hat{E}_{train} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}),y^{(i)})$ | Performance on training set |
| 3 | Generalization Gap | $\delta_{gen} = E_{gen} - \hat{E}_{train}$ | Diagnostic for overfitting |
| 4 | Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Splits total error into 3 parts |
| 5 | Bias Definition | $\text{Bias}(\hat{f}(x)) = f(x) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)]$ | Systematic error of the model |
| 6 | Variance Definition | $\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\big[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\big]$ | Sensitivity to training set |
| 7 | L2 Regularization Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{reg}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda \|\theta\|_2^2$ | Penalizes large weights |
| 8 | L1 Regularization Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{reg}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda \|\theta\|_1$ | Induces sparsity |
| 9 | Effective Capacity Bound | $C(\mathcal{H}) \propto \log \vert\mathcal{H}\vert$ (qualitative) | Larger $\mathcal{H}$ ⇒ more capacity |
| 10 | Expected Risk vs. Empirical | $E[\hat{E}_{train}] = E_{gen} - O\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{C(\mathcal{H})}{N}}\right)$ | Sample-size-dependent gap |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\vert\mathcal{H}\vert$ denotes the cardinality (size) of the hypothesis class — used here as a measure of capacity.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

Understanding overfitting vs. underfitting is the *first* diagnostic step in any deployed machine learning or deep learning pipeline (computer vision, NLP, recommender systems, time-series forecasting, autonomous driving perception). In production:

- A medical-imaging CNN that **overfits** will pass internal validation but fail on hospital-to-hospital domain shift.
- A demand-forecasting model that **underfits** will systematically miss seasonal patterns, costing millions in inventory losses.

Mastery of this concept drives decisions on **data augmentation, regularization strength, network depth, early-stopping patience, and cross-validation strategy** — all of which directly translate to business KPIs.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bias-Variance Decomposition

We start from the expected squared error at a single point $x$ and expand it algebraically.

**Step 1 — Define the loss at a point $x$:**

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D},\varepsilon}\Big[\big(y - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\Big]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the data-generating process** $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, where $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2$:

$$= \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D},\varepsilon}\Big[\big(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\Big]$$

**Step 3 — Add and subtract the mean prediction** $\bar{f}(x) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)]$ inside the square:

$$= \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D},\varepsilon}\Big[\big(f(x) - \bar{f}(x) + \bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x) + \varepsilon\big)^2\Big]$$

**Step 4 — Expand the trinomial** using $(a+b+c)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2ab + 2ac + 2bc$, where $a = f(x) - \bar{f}(x)$, $b = \bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)$, $c = \varepsilon$:

$$= \mathbb{E}\Big[\big(f(x) - \bar{f}(x)\big)^2\Big] + \mathbb{E}\Big[\big(\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\Big] + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] + 2\mathbb{E}\Big[(f - \bar{f})(\bar{f} - \hat{f})\Big] + 2\mathbb{E}\Big[(f - \bar{f})\varepsilon\Big] + 2\mathbb{E}\Big[(\bar{f} - \hat{f})\varepsilon\Big]$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the cross-terms.** Since $\bar{f}(x)$ is a constant w.r.t. the data-distribution, and $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)] = \bar{f}(x)$, the term $\mathbb{E}[(\bar{f} - \hat{f})] = 0$, killing the third cross-term. Similarly, the first cross-term vanishes:

$$2\mathbb{E}\Big[(f - \bar{f})(\bar{f} - \hat{f})\Big] = 2(f - \bar{f}) \cdot \mathbb{E}[\bar{f} - \hat{f}] = 2(f - \bar{f}) \cdot 0 = 0$$

The second cross-term vanishes because $\varepsilon$ is independent of $f(x)$ and $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$:

$$2\mathbb{E}\Big[(f - \bar{f})\varepsilon\Big] = 2(f - \bar{f}) \cdot \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$$

**Step 6 — Recognise the three remaining terms:**

$$= \underbrace{\big(f(x) - \bar{f}(x)\big)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\Big[\big(\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x)\big)^2\Big]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

**Step 7 — Compact form (final result):**

$$\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\Big[\big(y - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\Big] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This identity is the cornerstone of model selection: a perfect model ($E = 0$) is impossible because $\sigma^2 > 0$ for any noisy real-world data. The engineer's job is to allocate the *remaining* error budget between Bias² and Variance.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Each Mitigation Maps to Bias / Variance

| Mitigation Technique | Effect on Bias² | Effect on Variance | Effect on $\sigma^2$ | KTU Favourite? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase model capacity (more layers/neurons) | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | — Unchanged | Yes |
| Decrease model capacity (prune / smaller net) | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | — Unchanged | Yes |
| L2 / Weight Decay Regularization | ↑ Slight ↑ | ↓↓ Strongly ↓ | — | Yes |
| L1 Regularization | ↑ Slight ↑ | ↓ Decreases | — | Yes |
| Dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014) | ↑ Slight ↑ | ↓↓ Strongly ↓ | — | Yes |
| Data Augmentation | ↓ May ↓ | ↓↓ Strongly ↓ | — | Yes |
| Early Stopping (on validation loss) | — Roughly neutral | ↓ Decreases | — | Yes |
| Batch Normalization | ↓ Slight ↓ | ↓ Decreases | — | Yes |
| More Training Data ($N \uparrow$) | — Neutral | ↓↓ Strongly ↓ | — | Yes |
| Ensembling / Model Averaging | ↓ Decreases | ↓↓ Strongly ↓ | — | Yes |
| k-Fold Cross-Validation | — Diagnostic only | — | — | Yes |

## 3.3 The Learning Curve Diagnostic

A **learning curve** plots $\hat{E}_{train}$ and $E_{val}$ as a function of the number of training iterations (or sample size $N$).

**Diagnostic signatures:**

- **Underfit signature**: Both $\hat{E}_{train}$ and $E_{val}$ plateau at a *high* value with a *small gap* between them. The model is too simple to learn even the training set.
- **Overfit signature**: $\hat{E}_{train}$ continues to descend toward 0, while $E_{val}$ initially descends but then *diverges upward*, opening a wide gap $\delta_{gen}$.
- **Good fit signature**: $\hat{E}_{train}$ and $E_{val}$ converge to similar low values; the gap is small and stable.

**Asymptotic behaviour with sample size $N$:**

$$E_{gen}(N) \;\to\; \sigma^2 \quad \text{as } N \to \infty \text{ (fixed-capacity model)}$$

i.e., more data shrinks the variance term but cannot reduce irreducible noise.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Error Decomposition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    dataIn["Input Data: y = f of x + epsilon"]
    trainLoop["Model Training Loop: theta* = argmin Loss over theta in H"]
    meanPred["Compute Mean Prediction: fbar of x = E_D of fhat of x"]
    biasBlock["Bias^2 = square of f of x minus fbar of x"]
    varBlock["Variance = E_D of square of fhat of x minus fbar of x"]
    noiseBlock["Irreducible Noise: sigma^2 = Var of epsilon"]
    sumBox["Total Expected Error = Bias^2 + Variance + sigma^2"]
    
    dataIn --> trainLoop
    trainLoop --> meanPred
    meanPred --> biasBlock
    meanPred --> varBlock
    dataIn --> noiseBlock
    biasBlock --> sumBox
    varBlock --> sumBox
    noiseBlock --> sumBox
```
