# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECDT695]**
*Define the terms "overfitting" and "underfitting" in the context of a supervised learning model. Mention ONE typical symptom of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Overfitting** is a modelling error that occurs when a machine learning model learns the random noise and minute fluctuations in the training data to such an extent that it negatively impacts the model's performance on unseen test data. The model exhibits **high variance** and a very **low training error** but a **high generalization error** (large gap $\delta_{gen}$). Typical symptom: training accuracy ≈ 99% but test accuracy ≈ 60%.

> **Underfitting** is the opposite phenomenon in which the model is unable to capture the underlying trend of the data, usually because it is too simple (low capacity) or insufficiently trained. It exhibits **high bias**, with high error on *both* training and test sets, and the two errors remain close to each other. Typical symptom: training accuracy ≈ 55% and test accuracy ≈ 54%.

**Mark allocation:** Definition of overfitting (1) + symptom (0.5) + Definition of underfitting (1) + symptom (0.5).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECDT695]**
*State the Bias-Variance decomposition for the expected squared error of a regression model. What does the term $\sigma^2$ represent?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The expected prediction error at a point $x$ decomposes as:

$$\mathbb{E}\big[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\big] = \underbrace{\big(f(x) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)]\big)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\big[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\big]}_{\text{Variance}} + \sigma^2$$

- **Bias² (1 mark):** Squared difference between the *true* function and the *average* prediction; measures systematic error.
- **Variance (1 mark):** Variability of the model's prediction across different training sets drawn from the same distribution.
- **$\sigma^2$ (1 mark):** The **irreducible noise** (Bayes error) — the variance of the inherent stochastic component $\varepsilon$ in the data-generating process $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$. It cannot be eliminated by any model, no matter how flexible.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU 2024 ESE convention for Module 1, students are given an **internal choice** between two full-14-mark questions. Both options are presented below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** in supervised learning. With the help of a neatly labelled diagram, describe how **training error** and **test error** behave as the model capacity is increased from very low to very high. Identify the regions corresponding to underfitting and overfitting.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **four (4) techniques** to mitigate overfitting in a deep neural network. For each technique, briefly state whether it primarily reduces bias or variance.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — Bias-Variance Tradeoff and Capacity Curve [7 marks]:**

The **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** is the central diagnostic framework that decomposes the expected prediction error of a learned model into three orthogonal components: the squared bias, the variance, and the irreducible noise.

$$\text{Total Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$$

- **Bias²** captures the *systematic* error — the deviation between the *expected* model prediction and the *true* target function $f(x)$. A high-bias model is too rigid and will underfit.
- **Variance** captures the *sensitivity* of the learned model to the specific training set sampled. A high-variance model over-reacts to noise and overfits.
- **$\sigma^2$** is the floor that no model can breach — the inherent stochasticity of the data.

**Behaviour as capacity increases** (refer to the figure below):

1. At **very low capacity** (e.g., linear model on a curved dataset), the model cannot represent $f(x)$ ⇒ **Bias² is high, Variance is low**, and *both* training and test errors are high and close together ⇒ **Underfitting region**.
2. As **capacity increases**, the model starts fitting the true signal. **Bias² falls rapidly**, while **variance begins to rise slowly**. There exists a point where the sum Bias² + Variance is **minimised** — the **optimal capacity** or "sweet spot".
3. As **capacity grows further**, the model begins to interpolate the noise. **Variance rises sharply**, while Bias² continues to decrease (slowly). The training error falls toward 0, but the test error diverges upward ⇒ **Overfitting region**.

**Capacity-Error Curve Diagram (ASCII schematic, draw equivalent in exam):**

```
Error
  ^                       Variance  /
  |                              /
  |                             /
  |              Total  - - - /
  |                   \  /  /
  |                    \/  /  Bias^2
  |                    /\ /
  |                   /  V
  |                  /  Sweet Spot
  |                 /    |
  |________________/_____|__________> Model Capacity
                   Low   Optimal   High
                  (Under)  (Good) (Over)
```

**Mark allocation for (a):** Bias-Variance identity (1) + explanation of Bias² (1) + explanation of Variance (1) + Capacity curve description with regions (3) + diagram (1).

---

**(b) — Four Techniques to Mitigate Overfitting [7 marks]:**

1. **Get More Training Data / Data Augmentation** [1.5 marks]
   Increase $N$ or apply label-preserving transformations (rotation, crop, flip, Mixup, CutMix). This *primarily reduces variance* by giving the optimizer more evidence of the true underlying distribution, diluting the influence of any single noisy training sample.

2. **Regularization (L1 / L2 / Weight Decay)** [1.5 marks]
   Add a penalty term $\lambda \|\theta\|_p^p$ to the loss. L2 shrinks weights smoothly; L1 induces sparsity. Both *primarily reduce variance* by constraining the hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$ to lower-capacity sub-classes, raising bias slightly but lowering variance substantially.

3. **Dropout** [1.5 marks]
   At each training step, randomly zero-out a fraction $p$ of activations. This is equivalent to training an *ensemble* of $2^n$ thinned sub-networks that share parameters. *Primarily reduces variance*; very slight bias increase.

4. **Early Stopping** [1.5 marks]
   Monitor validation loss at every epoch; stop training when it stops improving. The implicit effect is to keep the network at a low-norm, low-complexity state, behaving like an inexpensive form of L2 regularization. *Primarily reduces variance*.

**(Alternative correct answers:** Batch Normalization, Ensembling, Reducing model capacity, k-Fold Cross-Validation, Label Smoothing, Transfer Learning.)

**Mark allocation for (b):** 4 techniques × (1.25 marks explanation + 0.25 marks bias/variance classification).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Bias-Variance decomposition** of the expected squared prediction error for a regression problem. Clearly state the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is a **learning curve**? Sketch and explain the characteristic learning curves corresponding to (i) underfitting, (ii) good fit, and (iii) overfitting. What is the role of $k$-fold cross-validation in diagnosing these regimes?

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — Derivation of Bias-Variance Decomposition [7 marks]:**

**Assumptions** (state explicitly for full marks):
- Data-generating process: $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ i.i.d.
- $\varepsilon$ is **independent** of $x$ and of the training set.
- $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$, $\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2$.
- The model is fit on a random training set $\mathcal{D}$ of size $N$ drawn i.i.d. from the joint distribution.

**Derivation** (reproduce the steps from Section 3.1 of these notes; the concise version is below):

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D},\varepsilon}\big[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\big]$$

Substitute $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$:

$$= \mathbb{E}\big[(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x))^2\big]$$

Add and subtract the mean prediction $\bar{f}(x) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)]$:

$$= \mathbb{E}\big[\big(f(x) - \bar{f}(x)\big) + \big(\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)\big) + \varepsilon\big]^2$$

Expand and apply the expectation operator term-by-term. Cross-terms vanish because:
- $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)] = 0$ (by definition of $\bar{f}$)
- $\mathbb{E}_\varepsilon[\varepsilon] = 0$

The remaining three terms yield the final decomposition:

$$\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}\big[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\big] = \underbrace{(f(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\big[(\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2\big]}_{\text{Variance}} + \sigma^2\;}$$

**Mark allocation for (a):** Assumptions (2) + Insert mean (1) + Expand (1) + Cross-terms vanish (1) + Final result (2).

---

**(b) — Learning Curves and Cross-Validation [7 marks]:**

A **learning curve** is a diagnostic plot of the model's **training error** and **validation (or test) error** as a function of training iterations (epochs) or training-set size $N$. It is one of the most widely used *empirical* tools in KTU board-exam problems to distinguish between the three regimes.

**Three characteristic signatures:**

**(i) Underfitting [1 mark]:** Both training and validation errors remain high, are close to each other, and plateau quickly. The gap is small. → *Action*: increase capacity, add features, reduce regularization.

**(ii) Good Fit [1 mark]:** Training and validation errors both descend and converge to a low value; the gap between them is small and stable. → *Action*: deploy.

**(iii) Overfitting [1 mark]:** Training error descends toward 0, but validation error first decreases, then begins to *rise* — the gap $\delta_{gen}$ widens. → *Action*: regularize, augment data, use early stopping, dropout.

**ASCII learning-curve schematic (replicate in exam):**

```
Error
  ^   | (i) Underfit  (ii) Good  (iii) Overfit
  |   | _________      \_          Train ___
  |   |          \      \         /        \
  |   | Val  ___  \      \   Val /          \   <- Val diverges up
  |   |       \   \      \ /                \
  |   |        \   \      X                  
  |   |         \   \    / \                 
  |   |__________\___\__/___\___________________> Epochs
        (i)         (ii)        (iii)
```

**Role of k-Fold Cross-Validation [3 marks]:**

In k-Fold CV, the data is split into $k$ equal folds. The model is trained $k$ times, each time holding out a *different* fold for validation and training on the remaining $k-1$ folds.

- It provides a **low-variance estimate** of the generalization error by averaging over $k$ hold-out splits.
- It detects **high variance** (overfitting) because the $k$ validation scores will show high spread if the model is over-sensitive to the training partition.
- It allows the use of *all* data for both training and validation, which is critical when $N$ is small.
- Combined with learning curves, CV gives a **complete diagnostic**: the learning curve shows *when* the model starts overfitting, and CV quantifies *how severe* the overfitting is.

**Mark allocation for (b):** Definition (0.5) + 3 curve explanations (3) + Schematic (0.5) + CV role (3).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating "bias" with "bias term" of a neuron.** In the bias-variance context, "bias" is a *statistical* concept ($\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - f$), not the intercept of a linear unit. Mixing these two usages loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Omitting the assumption $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$** in the derivation. The cross-terms vanish *only* under this assumption; without stating it, the derivation is incomplete and you lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "variance = 0" for deterministic models** — variance is defined w.r.t. the *distribution over training sets*, not w.r.t. a single deterministic prediction. The expectation $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\cdot]$ is essential.
> 4. **Drawing the capacity curve flipped** (showing both errors decreasing). The correct signature is that *training error monotonically decreases* with capacity, while *test error is U-shaped*.
> 5. **Forgetting to label the "sweet spot"** in the capacity diagram. KTU examiners explicitly check for the minimum of the total-error curve.
> 6. **Listing dropout, regularization, augmentation, early stopping** without specifying which (Bias² or Variance) they affect. This is asked frequently as a sub-part — answering it without the classification loses 0.5–1 mark per technique.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Overfitting** = low training error, high test error, large generalization gap $\delta_{gen}$, high **variance**.
- [x] **Underfitting** = high training error, high test error, small gap, high **bias²**.
- [x] **Good fit** = both errors low and close to each other.
- [x] **Total Error** = Bias² + Variance + $\sigma^2$ (irreducible noise / Bayes error).
- [x] **$\sigma^2$ is irreducible** — no model can ever beat it.
- [x] **Capacity curve**: As capacity ↑, Bias² ↓ monotonically, Variance ↑ monotonically, Total error is **U-shaped** with a sweet spot.
- [x] **Bias** is *systematic* error; **Variance** is *sensitivity* to training data.
- [x] **More training data** → reduces variance, no effect on bias or $\sigma^2$.
- [x] **Regularization (L1/L2/Dropout)** → primarily reduces variance; slight bias increase.
- [x] **Increasing model capacity** → reduces bias, increases variance.
- [x] **Early stopping** → primarily reduces variance (acts like cheap L2).
- [x] **Data augmentation** → primarily reduces variance.
- [x] **k-Fold Cross-Validation** → low-variance estimator of $E_{gen}$; also a diagnostic tool.
- [x] **Learning curves** plot $E_{train}$ and $E_{val}$ vs. epochs; signature shapes diagnose the regime.
- [x] **In the derivation**, always state: (i) $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, (ii) $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$, (iii) $\varepsilon \perp \!\!\! \perp$ training set, (iv) add-and-subtract the mean $\bar{f}(x)$, (v) show cross-terms vanish.
- [x] **No model is perfect** — the floor is $\sigma^2$.
- [x] **KTU favourite 14-mark structure**: (a) Diagram + tradeoff + regime identification (7 m), (b) Four mitigations classified by bias/variance (7 m).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hyperparameters and Validation Sets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hyperparameters and Validation Sets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Hyperparameters?

In the context of deep learning, **hyperparameters** are the **configuration variables that govern the entire training process itself**, as opposed to the *parameters* (weights and biases) that the network learns from data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Hyperparameters are **external configuration settings** whose values cannot be directly estimated from the training data and must be set **prior** to initiating the learning process. They control the *learning algorithm's behavior* — not what the model learns, but *how* it learns.

### Parameters vs Hyperparameters — The Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Parameters | Hyperparameters |
|---|---|---|
| **Who sets it?** | The model (via optimization) | The engineer / practitioner |
| **Where do values come from?** | Learned from training data | Set manually or via search algorithms |
| **Examples** | Weights $W$, biases $b$ | Learning rate $\eta$, batch size $m$, epochs $E$ |
| **Updates during training?** | Yes (every iteration) | No (fixed before training starts) |
| **Count** | Often millions/billions | Usually 10–50 in a typical DL pipeline |

### 1.2 What is a Validation Set?

A **validation set** is a curated subset of data, **distinct from both the training set and the test set**, used to:
1. Tune hyperparameters
2. Perform model selection
3. Detect overfitting early
4. Decide when to stop training (early stopping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECDT695 Module 1):** The *validation set* is the bridge between training and testing. It acts as a **proxy for the test set** during hyperparameter tuning — but it must NEVER be used to update model weights.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🎯

**Think of preparing for the KTU University Exam:**

- **Parameters (Weights)** = Your *actual knowledge* acquired by solving problems (learned through practice/study).
- **Hyperparameters** = Your *study strategy*: how many hours per day (learning rate), how many chapters per day (batch size), total months of preparation (epochs), whether to use flashcards or mind maps (optimizer choice).
- **Training Set** = The textbook exercises you solve repeatedly.
- **Validation Set** = The *previous year question papers* — you use them to test your strategy and tune it, but you don't memorize them.
- **Test Set** = The actual exam day paper — you've never seen these questions before; this is the *true measure* of learning.

> **The trap students fall into**: Memorizing previous year papers (validation set) is like *overfitting to the validation set*. You ace the "known" questions but fail on truly novel ones.

## 1.4 Why Hyperparameters and Validation Sets Matter in Deep Learning

Modern deep networks have **enormous hypothesis spaces**. Without disciplined hyperparameter control:

- The network may **underfit** (too small / undertrained)
- The network may **overfit** (memorizes training data, fails on unseen data)
- Training may **diverge** (loss explodes to NaN)
- Convergence may be **prohibitively slow** (weeks of GPU time wasted)

A well-designed validation protocol is therefore **non-negotiable** in any production-grade deep learning system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Hyperparameters in Deep Learning

Hyperparameters can be categorized into three broad families:

### Family A — Optimizer Hyperparameters
These control *how* gradient updates are applied.

1. **Learning Rate ($\eta$)** — arguably the **single most important hyperparameter**.
2. **Momentum ($\beta$)** — typically $\beta \in [0.9, 0.999]$.
3. **Optimizer choice** — SGD, Adam, RMSprop, AdaGrad, AdamW.
4. **Weight Decay ($\lambda$)** — L2 regularization coefficient.
5. **Gradient Clipping Threshold** — for RNNs and transformers.

### Family B — Model Architecture Hyperparameters
These control the *capacity* of the network.

1. **Number of layers ($L$)** — depth.
2. **Number of hidden units per layer ($n_l$)** — width.
3. **Activation function** — ReLU, LeakyReLU, GELU, Swish, Tanh, Sigmoid.
4. **Dropout rate ($p$)** — fraction of units randomly zeroed.
5. **Skip / Residual connections** — architectural choice.

### Family C — Data & Training-Loop Hyperparameters
These control the *input pipeline*.

1. **Batch size ($m$)**
2. **Number of epochs ($E$)**
3. **Learning rate schedule** — step decay, cosine annealing, warm restarts.
4. **Data augmentation parameters**
5. **Early stopping patience ($P$)**

## 2.2 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff (Foundation of Validation)

The total expected prediction error of a learned model can be decomposed as:

$$
\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}
$$

- **High Bias (Underfitting):** Model is too simple; misses patterns. Fix: bigger model, train longer.
- **High Variance (Overfitting):** Model memorizes training data; fails to generalize. Fix: more data, regularization, dropout, smaller model.
- **Validation set** is the diagnostic instrument that *quantifies* this tradeoff.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The validation loss $J_{\text{val}}$ is the **unbiased estimator of the generalization error** that we are allowed to peek at during model development. The test loss $J_{\text{test}}$ must remain a *one-shot* final measurement.

## 2.3 Data Splitting Strategies

### 2.3.1 Simple Hold-Out Validation

The dataset $\mathcal{D}$ of size $N$ is partitioned:

$$
N = N_{\text{train}} + N_{\text{val}} + N_{\text{test}}
$$

Typical ratios: **70/15/15** or **80/10/10** depending on $N$.

**Limitation:** High variance in the validation score; depends on which samples land in the validation split.

### 2.3.2 k-Fold Cross-Validation

Split $\mathcal{D}$ into $k$ equal folds. Train $k$ times, each time holding out a different fold for validation.

$$
\text{CV Score} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} J_{\text{val}}^{(i)}
$$

**Variance Reduction Formula:**

$$
\text{Var}(\text{CV Score}) \approx \frac{1}{k} \text{Var}(J_{\text{val}}^{(i)})
$$

So increasing $k$ reduces the variance of the score estimate. **Standard choice:** $k = 5$ or $k = 10$.

> [!NOTE]
> In **deep learning**, k-fold CV is often *impractical* because each fold requires a full retraining of a massive network. Practitioners usually prefer **single hold-out** or **stratified hold-out**.

### 2.3.3 Stratified Splitting

Maintains the class distribution across train/val/test. Essential for imbalanced datasets.

$$
p_{\text{class}}(y=c) \approx \text{constant across all splits}
$$

## 2.4 Early Stopping — The "Free" Regularizer

**Algorithm Outline:**
1. After every epoch $t$, record validation loss $J_{\text{val}}^{(t)}$.
2. Maintain a running best: $J_{\text{val}}^{*} = \min_t J_{\text{val}}^{(t)}$.
3. If no improvement for $P$ consecutive epochs (patience), stop.
4. Restore the model weights from epoch $t^{*} = \arg\min_t J_{\text{val}}^{(t)}$.

**Mathematical Justification:** Early stopping is equivalent to **L2 regularization** under specific assumptions (Goodfellow et al., *Deep Learning*, MIT Press, 2016 — equation 7.20). The number of training iterations acts as an *implicit* regularizer.

## 2.5 Hyperparameter Search Strategies

### 2.5.1 Grid Search
Exhaustively try all combinations in a predefined grid.

$$
\text{Combinations} = \prod_{i=1}^{d} \vert \mathcal{H}_i \vert
$$

where $d$ is the number of hyperparameters and $\vert \mathcal{H}_i \vert$ is the grid size for hyperparameter $i$.

**Drawback:** Curse of dimensionality. Wastes compute on unimportant dimensions.

### 2.5.2 Random Search
Sample each hyperparameter independently from a prior distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bergstra & Bengio (2012)** proved that **Random Search is asymptotically more efficient than Grid Search** when some hyperparameters are much more important than others. For $d$ hyperparameters where only $d_1 \ll d$ actually matter, random search finds a good configuration in exponentially fewer trials.

### 2.5.3 Bayesian Optimization
Builds a *probabilistic surrogate model* (usually a Gaussian Process) over the hyperparameter-to-validation-loss mapping.

$$
p(J_{\text{val}} \mid \mathcal{H}) \approx \mathcal{GP}(\mu(\mathcal{H}), k(\mathcal{H}, \mathcal{H}'))
$$

Then uses an **acquisition function** (e.g., Expected Improvement) to choose the next trial:

$$
\text{EI}(\mathcal{H}) = \mathbb{E}[\max(0, J_{\text{val}}^{*} - J_{\text{val}}(\mathcal{H}))]
$$

### 2.5.4 Successive Halving & Hyperband

Allocates more compute to promising configurations. Hyperband combines random search with successive halving — provably near-optimal under mild assumptions.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Meaning | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SGD Update | $W \leftarrow W - \eta \nabla_W J$ | $\eta \in [10^{-6}, 10^{-1}]$ |
| 2 | Momentum Update | $v \leftarrow \beta v + \nabla_W J$; $W \leftarrow W - \eta v$ | $\beta \approx 0.9$ |
| 3 | Adam Update | Combines momentum + RMSProp; adapts per-parameter $\eta$ | $\beta_1=0.9$, $\beta_2=0.999$ |
| 4 | L2 Regularization | $J_{\text{reg}} = J + \lambda \Vert W \Vert_2^2$ | $\lambda \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-2}]$ |
| 5 | Dropout (inverted) | At training: zero out with prob $p$; rescale by $1/(1-p)$ | $p \in [0.1, 0.5]$ |
| 6 | Hold-out ratios | $N_{\text{train}} : N_{\text{val}} : N_{\text{test}}$ | $70:15:15$ or $80:10:10$ |
| 7 | k-Fold CV | $\text{Score} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} J_{\text{val}}^{(i)}$ | $k = 5$ or $10$ |
| 8 | Early Stopping | Stop if $J_{\text{val}}$ hasn't improved for $P$ epochs | $P = 5$ to $20$ |
| 9 | Grid combinations | $\prod_{i=1}^{d} \vert \mathcal{H}_i \vert$ | Avoid if $d > 4$ |
| 10 | Bias-Variance | $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Diagnostic |
| 11 | Effective LR (Batch Size) | $\eta_{\text{eff}} = \eta \cdot \frac{m_{\text{new}}}{m_{\text{ref}}}$ | Linear scaling rule |
| 12 | One-Cycle Policy | $\eta: \eta_{\min} \to \eta_{\max} \to \eta_{\min}$ | Smith (2018) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Hyperparameter Tuning |
|---|---|
| **Computer Vision** | Tuning CNN for medical imaging — dropout, augmentation |
| **NLP** | Transformer learning rate warm-up, attention dropout |
| **Speech** | RNN regularization, sequence padding hyperparameters |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | PPO clip ratio, discount factor $\gamma$, entropy bonus |
| **AutoML / Production** | Bayesian opt. + Hyperband for efficient cloud GPU search |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Early Stopping ≈ L2 Regularization

Consider a simple quadratic loss around the optimum $W^{*}$, with the SGD update rule (no momentum):

$$
W^{(t+1)} = W^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_W J(W^{(t)})
$$

**Step 1:** Approximate the loss with a Taylor expansion to second order:

$$
J(W) \approx J(W^{*}) + \frac{1}{2}(W - W^{*})^T H (W - W^{*})
$$

where $H$ is the Hessian matrix at $W^{*}$.

**Step 2:** Gradient of this quadratic approximation:

$$
\nabla_W J = H(W - W^{*})
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into the SGD update:

$$
W^{(t+1)} = W^{(t)} - \eta H (W^{(t)} - W^{*})
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange by defining the deviation from optimum $\delta^{(t)} = W^{(t)} - W^{*}$:

$$
\delta^{(t+1)} = (I - \eta H) \delta^{(t)}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve this linear recurrence. In the eigenbasis of $H$, with eigenvalues $\lambda_i$:

$$
\delta_i^{(t)} = (1 - \eta \lambda_i)^t \, \delta_i^{(0)}
$$

**Step 6:** Apply the small-$\eta\lambda_i$ Taylor approximation $(1 - \eta\lambda_i)^t \approx e^{-\eta\lambda_i t}$:

$$
\delta_i^{(t)} \approx \delta_i^{(0)} \, e^{-\eta \lambda_i t}
$$

**Step 7:** Compare with L2-regularized optimum. For the regularized loss $J_{\text{reg}}(W) = J(W) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\Vert W \Vert_2^2$, the optimum $\tilde{W}$ satisfies:

$$
\nabla J(\tilde{W}) + \lambda \tilde{W} = 0
$$

Using the quadratic approximation:

$$
H(\tilde{W} - W^{*}) + \lambda \tilde{W} = 0
$$

**Step 8:** Solve for $\tilde{W}$ in the eigenbasis (assuming small effect on $W^{*}$):

$$
\tilde{\delta}_i = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda_i + \lambda} W^{*}
$$

**Step 9:** Equating the early-stopped solution at step $t$ with the L2-regularized optimum, we obtain the **correspondence**:

$$
\eta \lambda_i t \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{\lambda}{\lambda_i}
$$

Solving for the implicit L2 coefficient:

$$
\lambda \;\leftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{\eta t}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\text{early-stop}} = \frac{1}{\eta t_{\text{stop}}}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Stopping at iteration $t$ is *equivalent* to applying L2 regularization with coefficient $\lambda \approx 1/(\eta t)$. **Fewer iterations ↔ stronger regularization.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Variance of k-Fold Cross-Validation Score

**Step 1:** Each fold $i$ yields a validation loss $X_i = J_{\text{val}}^{(i)}$. Assume $X_i$ are i.i.d. with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.

**Step 2:** The k-fold CV estimator is the sample mean:

$$
\bar{X}_k = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} X_i
$$

**Step 3:** By standard results for the variance of a sample mean:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{X}_k) = \frac{\sigma^2}{k}
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with single hold-out (effectively $k = 1$):

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{X}_1) = \sigma^2
$$

**Step 5:** Variance reduction factor:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\text{Var}(\bar{X}_k)}{\text{Var}(\bar{X}_1)} = \frac{1}{k}}
$$

**Step 6:** Standard error (useful for confidence intervals):

$$
\text{SE} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{k}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k(k-1)} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (X_i - \bar{X}_k)^2}
$$

**Step 7:** A 95% confidence interval for the true generalization loss (using normal approximation):

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} = \bar{X}_k \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{k}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Effective Learning Rate Under Batch Size Scaling

**Motivation:** When doubling the batch size, should we double the learning rate?

**Step 1:** SGD update on a mini-batch of size $m$:

$$
W^{(t+1)} = W^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_W J^{(m)}(W^{(t)})
$$

where $J^{(m)}$ is the average loss over the $m$ samples.

**Step 2:** The mini-batch gradient is an unbiased estimator of the true gradient:

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_W J^{(m)}\right] = \nabla_W J
$$

**Step 3:** Its variance scales inversely with batch size:

$$
\text{Var}\left[\nabla_W J^{(m)}\right] = \frac{\sigma_g^2}{m}
$$

where $\sigma_g^2$ is the per-sample gradient variance.

**Step 4:** To keep the *expected per-step progress* $\Vert \eta \nabla J^{(m)}\Vert$ constant when changing $m \to m'$:

$$
\eta \Vert \nabla J \Vert = \eta' \sqrt{\frac{m}{m'}} \Vert \nabla J \Vert
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the scaled learning rate:

$$
\boxed{\eta' = \eta \sqrt{\frac{m'}{m}}}
$$

**Step 6:** Empirically (Goyal et al., 2017), the **linear scaling rule** works well for moderate batch sizes:

$$
\eta' \approx \eta \cdot \frac{m'}{m}
$$

with a warm-up phase to stabilize early training.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing a Hyperparameter Strategy

| Scenario | Recommended Strategy | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Small dataset ($N < 10^4$), few hyperparams ($d \leq 3$) | **Grid Search + k-Fold CV** | Exhaustive; dataset is cheap to train on |
| Large dataset, $d > 5$ | **Random Search** | Bergstra & Bengio: asymptotically optimal |
| Expensive training (each trial = hours/days) | **Bayesian Optimization** | Minimizes total compute |
| Many cheap trials, clear win/lose | **Hyperband / Successive Halving** | Aggressive early killing of bad configs |
| Deep learning with massive data | **Single hold-out + Random Search** | k-fold too expensive |
| Imbalanced classification | **Stratified k-Fold** | Preserves class ratio per fold |
| Time-series data | **Forward-Chaining CV** | Respects temporal causality |
| Transfer learning | **Hold-out on target task** | Source task hyperparams rarely transfer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Data Splitting & Validation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset D of size N] --> B[Shuffle and Stratify]
    B --> C{Train Test Split}
    C --> D[Training Set 70 percent]
    C --> E[Test Set 15 percent HOLD OUT FINAL]
    D --> F[Tune Validation Split]
    F --> G[Training Subset 80 percent of Train]
    F --> H[Validation Set 20 percent of Train]
    G --> I[Train Model Candidate]
    H --> J[Compute Validation Loss]
    J --> K{Better than Best So Far}
    K -- Yes --> L[Save Model Weights]
    K -- No --> M[Increment Patience Counter]
    M --> N{Patience Exceeded}
    N -- No --> I
    N -- Yes --> O[Stop Training Early]
    L --> I
    O --> P[Select Best Hyperparameters]
    P --> Q[Retrain on Full Train Set]
    Q --> R[Final Evaluation on Test Set]
    R --> S[Report Generalization Error]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hyperparameter Tuning Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hyperparameter Tuning Starts] --> B{Compute Budget Available}
    B -- Small --> C[Manual Search or Grid Search]
    B -- Medium --> D[Random Search]
    B -- Large --> E[Bayesian Optimization]
    B -- Very Large --> F[Population Based Training PBT]
    C --> G[Evaluate via Validation Loss]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Better than Current Best}
    H -- Yes --> I[Update Best Config]
    H -- No --> J[Discard or Continue]
    I --> K[Allocate More Resources]
    J --> L[Prune or Halve]
    K --> M[Final Best Hyperparameters]
    L --> M
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Bias-Variance Diagnostic via Validation Curves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Train Model] --> B[Plot Train Loss vs Epoch]
    A --> C[Plot Validation Loss vs Epoch]
    B --> D{Train Loss Still High}
    C --> E{Val Loss Diverges from Train}
    D -- Yes --> F[UNDERFITTING: Increase Capacity]
    D -- No --> G[Check Other]
    E -- Yes --> H[OVERFITTING: Add Regularization]
    E -- No --> I[GOOD FIT: Stop Here]
    F --> J[Tune Hyperparameters]
    H --> J
    G --> J
    J --> A
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: k-Fold Cross-Validation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Full Dataset D] --> B[Partition into k Folds]
    B --> C[Initialize CV Score = 0]
    C --> D[For fold i = 1 to k]
    D --> E[Hold Out Fold i as Validation]
    E --> F[Train on Remaining k minus 1 Folds]
    F --> G[Compute Validation Score S_i]
    G --> H[Add S_i to CV Score]
    H --> I{fold i less than k}
    I -- Yes --> D
    I -- No --> J[Average CV Score]
    J --> K[Compute Standard Error]
    K --> L[Report Final CV Score plus minus SE]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Distinguish between model parameters and hyperparameters in a deep neural network. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Parameters** are internal variables whose values are *learned automatically* during training from the data. They are updated via backpropagation and gradient descent.  
  *Examples:* weights $W^{[l]}$ and biases $b^{[l]}$ of layer $l$. **(1 mark)**

- **Hyperparameters** are external configuration values that the engineer must set *before* training begins. They control the learning algorithm itself and are typically tuned via a validation set.  
  *Examples:* learning rate $\eta$, batch size $m$, number of epochs $E$, dropout rate $p$. **(1 mark)**

- **Key distinguishing property:** parameters are *outputs* of training; hyperparameters are *inputs* to training. **(1 mark)**

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: What is the role of a validation set? Why must the test set not be used for hyperparameter tuning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **validation set** is used to (i) estimate the generalization error during model development, (ii) compare different hyperparameter configurations, and (iii) implement early stopping. **(1.5 marks)**

- If the test set is repeatedly used to tune hyperparameters, information about it leaks into the model selection process. The reported test error then becomes *optimistically biased* — it is no longer an unbiased estimator of generalization performance on truly unseen data. **(1 mark)**

- Therefore, the test set must be kept in a "vault" and used **only once** at the very end. **(0.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every module question offers an internal choice. Below are **two complete alternatives** (Question A OR Question B). Pick any one in the exam.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – PECDT695 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various hyperparameter tuning strategies used in deep learning. Compare their relative merits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the k-fold cross-validation procedure. For $k = 5$, show mathematically why it provides a lower-variance estimate of generalization error than a single hold-out split. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The principal hyperparameter tuning strategies are:

1. **Manual Search (a.k.a. babysitting)** [1 mark]
   - Engineer manually adjusts hyperparameters based on experience and validation loss curves.
   - Works well when the model trains quickly and the engineer has strong intuition.

2. **Grid Search** [1 mark]
   - Define a discrete grid for each hyperparameter; exhaustively try all $\prod_i \vert \mathcal{H}_i \vert$ combinations.
   - **Pros:** Reproducible, exhaustive within grid.
   - **Cons:** Suffers curse of dimensionality; wastes compute on unimportant dimensions.

3. **Random Search** [1 mark]
   - Sample each hyperparameter independently from a prior distribution (often log-uniform for learning rate).
   - **Bergstra & Bengio (2012) theorem:** Random search is asymptotically more efficient than grid search when only a subset of hyperparameters strongly influences performance.
   - **Pros:** Better coverage of high-dimensional spaces.
   - **Cons:** No learning from past trials.

4. **Bayesian Optimization** [1.5 marks]
   - Builds a **probabilistic surrogate** (typically a Gaussian Process) over $p(J_{\text{val}} \mid \mathcal{H})$.
   - Uses an **acquisition function** (Expected Improvement, UCB) to choose the next promising trial.
   - **Pros:** Sample-efficient; learns from past evaluations.
   - **Cons:** Surrogate modeling adds overhead; scales poorly to > 20 dimensions.

5. **Hyperband / Successive Halving** [1.5 marks]
   - Aggressively allocates compute: run many configs with small budgets, kill the worst half, double budgets for survivors.
   - **Hyperband** wraps successive halving in a meta-algorithm that adapts the initial budget.
   - **Pros:** Excellent compute efficiency for deep learning.
   - **Cons:** Aggressive early-stopping may discard configs that need long warm-ups (e.g., transformers).

6. **Population Based Training (PBT)** [1 mark]
   - Maintains a population of models; periodically exploits (copy weights from better models) and explores (perturb hyperparameters).
   - **Pros:** Discovers schedules, not just point values.
   - **Cons:** Requires parallel training infrastructure.

**Comparative summary table:**

| Strategy | Sample Efficiency | Scalability to High $d$ | Handles Schedules | Implementation Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Manual | Low (human time) | Medium | Yes | Low |
| Grid | Low | Poor | No | Low |
| Random | Medium | Good | No | Low |
| Bayesian | High | Medium | No | Medium |
| Hyperband | High | Good | Partial | Medium |
| PBT | High | Good | Yes | High |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**k-Fold Cross-Validation Procedure** [3 marks]

Given dataset $\mathcal{D}$ of size $N$:

1. Partition $\mathcal{D}$ randomly into $k$ disjoint folds $\mathcal{F}_1, \mathcal{F}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{F}_k$, each of size $\approx N/k$.
2. For $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$:
   - Train model on $\bigcup_{j \neq i} \mathcal{F}_j$.
   - Evaluate validation loss $J_{\text{val}}^{(i)}$ on held-out fold $\mathcal{F}_i$.
3. Compute the CV score: $\bar{J} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} J_{\text{val}}^{(i)}$.
4. Optionally compute standard error: $\text{SE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k(k-1)} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (J_{\text{val}}^{(i)} - \bar{J})^2}$.

**Mathematical Proof of Variance Reduction** [4 marks]

Let $X_i = J_{\text{val}}^{(i)}$ denote the validation loss on fold $i$. Assume:

- $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_k$ are approximately **i.i.d.** with $\mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu$ (true generalization loss) and $\text{Var}(X_i) = \sigma^2$.

The CV estimator is:

$$
\bar{X}_k = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} X_i
$$

By the property of sample means:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{X}_k) = \frac{\sigma^2}{k}
$$

A **single hold-out** split with $N_{\text{val}}$ samples yields an estimator with variance $\sigma^2$ (proportional to $1/N_{\text{val}}$). If the hold-out uses $N/k$ samples, its variance is roughly:

$$
\text{Var}(\text{Hold-out}) = \frac{k \sigma^2}{N}
$$

The **variance reduction ratio** is:

$$
\frac{\text{Var}(\bar{X}_k)}{\text{Var}(\text{Hold-out}_i)} = \frac{\sigma^2 / k}{\sigma^2} = \frac{1}{k}
$$

For $k = 5$, this is a **5× reduction** in estimator variance. Equivalently, k-fold CV uses the data $k$ times more efficiently than a single split of the same validation-set size.

**Quantitative Example** (for completeness): If $\sigma = 0.02$ and $k = 5$:

$$
\text{SE}_{k\text{-fold}} = \frac{0.02}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.0089
$$

vs.

$$
\text{SE}_{\text{single}} = 0.02
$$

Hence the 95% CI for the k-fold estimate is roughly **$\pm 0.018$** versus **$\pm 0.039$** for a single hold-out.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – PECDT695 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** With neat sketches, explain the bias-variance tradeoff. How does the validation set help diagnose underfitting vs overfitting? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain Early Stopping as a form of regularization. Derive the relationship between the optimal stopping iteration and the equivalent L2 regularization strength. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**The Bias-Variance Tradeoff** [4 marks]

For a regression problem with true target $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, the expected prediction error at a point $x$ of a learned model $\hat{f}(x)$ decomposes as:

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \underbrace{\left(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[\left(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2\right]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Noise}}
$$

- **Bias²** measures how far the *average prediction* is from the true function. High bias ⟹ model is too rigid (underfitting).
- **Variance** measures how much the prediction *fluctuates* across different training sets. High variance ⟹ model is too sensitive (overfitting).
- **Noise** $\sigma^2$ is irreducible.

**Graphical Sketch (mental model):**

```
Error │
      │ \                            /
      │  \      Total Error        /
      │   \                    /
      │    \      ___        /
      │     \   /     \    /
      │ Bias²\ /       \  / Variance
      │       V         \/
      │ Model Complexity →
```

**Role of Validation Set in Diagnosis** [3 marks]

After each epoch $t$, compute both $J_{\text{train}}^{(t)}$ and $J_{\text{val}}^{(t)}$. The diagnostic patterns are:

| Scenario | $J_{\text{train}}$ | $J_{\text{val}}$ | Diagnosis | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Both high, similar | High | High (≈ train) | **Underfitting** | Bigger model, longer training, lower regularization |
| Train low, val diverging | Low | High (≫ train) | **Overfitting** | More data, dropout, weight decay, early stopping |
| Both low, similar | Low | Low (≈ train) | **Good fit** | Stop training |

The validation set is therefore the **diagnostic instrument** that quantifies the generalization gap $J_{\text{val}} - J_{\text{train}}$, which is the empirical proxy for variance.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Early Stopping as Implicit Regularization** [3 marks]

Early stopping halts training when validation loss stops improving. Empirically, models trained longer continue to reduce training loss but generalize worse — a clear signature of overfitting. By saving the weights from the iteration with the lowest $J_{\text{val}}$, early stopping selects a model with controlled capacity.

**Derivation** [4 marks]

Following the derivation in Section 3.1:

1. Quadratic approximation of loss around optimum: $J(W) \approx J(W^*) + \frac{1}{2}(W - W^*)^T H (W - W^*)$.
2. SGD update in the eigenbasis of $H$: $\delta_i^{(t)} = (1 - \eta \lambda_i)^t \delta_i^{(0)}$.
3. L2-regularized optimum deviation: $\tilde{\delta}_i = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda_i + \lambda} W^{*}_i \approx \frac{\lambda}{\lambda_i} W^{*}_i$.
4. Equating (for early-stopped at iteration $t$ vs. L2 with coefficient $\lambda$):

$$
e^{-\eta \lambda_i t} \approx \frac{\lambda}{\lambda_i + \lambda}
$$

5. For small $\eta \lambda_i t$ and $\lambda \ll \lambda_i$:

$$
\eta \lambda_i t \approx \frac{\lambda}{\lambda_i} \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda \approx \eta \lambda_i^2 t
$$

Averaging across eigenvalues with characteristic magnitude $\bar{\lambda}$:

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{\text{eff}} \approx \frac{1}{\eta \, t_{\text{stop}}}}
$$

**Engineering Takeaway:**

- **Smaller learning rate $\eta$** ⟹ slower approach to optimum ⟹ *more* implicit regularization at fixed $t_{\text{stop}}$.
- **Shorter training $t_{\text{stop}}$** ⟹ stronger regularization.
- Early stopping is therefore a **computationally cheap** alternative to explicit weight decay, especially valuable when the optimal regularization strength is unknown.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:**
>
> 1. **Conflating validation and test sets.** Many students write "we use the test set to tune hyperparameters." This is a *fundamental protocol error* and can cost 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Confusing parameters with hyperparameters.** Always clarify *who sets* the value — the algorithm (parameters) or the engineer (hyperparameters).
> 3. **Forgetting to mention "stratified" splitting** for classification problems with class imbalance. Examiners look for this nuance.
> 4. **In derivations**, skipping algebraic steps. KTU expects every transition to be justified. "Therefore, the result follows" without work = 0 marks for that sub-step.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., i.i.d. assumption for k-fold variance derivation).
> 6. **Writing "more data always helps."** Be specific: more data *reduces variance* but cannot reduce bias or noise.
> 7. **For Early Stopping derivations**, failing to mention the Taylor / quadratic approximation assumption explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU Exam):**

- [ ] **Parameters** are learned; **Hyperparameters** are set before training.
- [ ] **Three-way split**: Train (learns weights), Validation (tunes hyperparameters), Test (final unbiased evaluation).
- [ ] **Validation set is a proxy for the test set** during model development.
- [ ] **Test set must be used exactly once** — never for tuning.
- [ ] **k-Fold CV score** has variance $\sigma^2 / k$; standard error is $\sigma / \sqrt{k}$.
- [ ] **Random Search > Grid Search** in high dimensions (Bergstra & Bengio 2012).
- [ ] **Bayesian Optimization** uses Gaussian Process surrogate + Expected Improvement acquisition.
- [ ] **Early Stopping ≈ L2 Regularization** with $\lambda_{\text{eff}} \approx 1 / (\eta \, t_{\text{stop}})$.
- [ ] **Learning rate $\eta$** is the single most critical hyperparameter.
- [ ] **Batch size scaling**: $\eta' \approx \eta \cdot (m'/m)$ (linear rule, with warm-up).
- [ ] **Overfitting** → training loss low, validation loss high → use dropout, weight decay, early stopping, more data.
- [ ] **Underfitting** → both losses high → use bigger model, train longer, reduce regularization.
- [ ] **Stratified split** preserves class distribution; mandatory for imbalanced data.
- [ ] **Patience $P$** in early stopping: number of epochs without improvement before stopping.
- [ ] **Patience too small** ⟹ stops before convergence; **patience too large** ⟹ wastes compute.
- [ ] **Hyperparameter families**: Optimizer, Architecture, Data/Training-loop.
- [ ] **In deep learning**, single hold-out is preferred over k-fold due to computational cost.
- [ ] **Reproducibility**: always fix random seeds before reporting validation scores.
- [ ] **Data leakage** (using test info during training/validation) is the cardinal sin of ML evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimators - Bias and Variance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimators - Bias and Variance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Estimators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Estimators**
> An **estimator** is a statistical rule or function that maps an observed sample of data to a numerical value (estimate) of an unknown population parameter. The random variable representing this rule is called the **estimator** ($\hat{\theta}$), while its realized numerical value for a given sample is called the **estimate**.

Let $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(n)}\}$ be an i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) sample drawn from an unknown probability distribution $P_{data}$. An estimator is any measurable function:

$$\hat{\theta} : \mathcal{D} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

For example, the **sample mean** is an estimator for the population mean $\mu$:

$$\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x^{(i)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In deep learning, estimators are foundational because we are perpetually estimating unknown functions (e.g., the true data-generating distribution) using finite training data. The Bias–Variance decomposition provides the analytical lens to diagnose generalization errors.

## 1.2 Bias — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias of an Estimator** (KTU 2024 Module 1 — Statistical Foundations of Learning)
> The bias of an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ for the true parameter $\theta$ is the systematic error defined as the difference between the **expected value of the estimator** and the **true parameter value**:
> $$\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$$

An estimator is called:
- **Unbiased**: if $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = 0$, i.e., $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$
- **Asymptotically Unbiased**: if $\lim_{n \to \infty} \text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = 0$
- **Biased**: if $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) \neq 0$

## 1.3 Variance — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variance of an Estimator**
> The variance of an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ quantifies the average squared deviation of the estimator from its own expected value, capturing the sensitivity of the estimator to fluctuations in the training sample:
> $$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[\left(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{\theta}]\right)^{2}\right]$$

A **low-variance** estimator gives similar estimates for different samples, while a **high-variance** estimator produces wildly different estimates for different samples.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Dartboard Target**
> Imagine you are throwing darts at a bullseye, where the bullseye represents the **true parameter $\theta$** and each throw represents a different training sample yielding an estimate $\hat{\theta}$:
> - **High Bias, Low Variance**: All darts cluster tightly, but in the wrong corner — the estimator is *consistent but systematically wrong*.
> - **Low Bias, High Variance**: Darts scatter all around the bullseye — the estimator is *on average correct, but unstable*.
> - **Low Bias, Low Variance**: Darts cluster tightly around the bullseye — *the ideal estimator*.
> - **High Bias, High Variance**: Darts scatter wildly in the wrong region — *the worst case*.

In deep learning, **high bias** typically manifests as **underfitting** (the model is too rigid to capture the underlying pattern), while **high variance** manifests as **overfitting** (the model memorizes noise rather than learning generalizable structure).

---

## 1.5 The Bias–Variance Tradeoff (Prelude)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Principle**
> For any estimator $\hat{\theta}$, the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** decomposes as:
> $$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^{2}\right] = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + \left(\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})\right)^{2}$$
> This identity is the **Bias–Variance Decomposition**, which formally captures the inverse relationship: reducing one source of error often increases the other.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Estimator

An estimator has three crucial properties that determine its quality as a learner:

1. **Bias**: Systematic deviation from the true parameter (inaccuracy).
2. **Variance**: Sensitivity to the particular sample drawn (instability).
3. **Consistency**: Convergence to the true parameter as $n \to \infty$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Bias–Variance

**Step 1 — Sample Realization**
For a given dataset $\mathcal{D}$, compute the point estimate $\hat{\theta}(\mathcal{D})$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Sampling Distribution**
Imagine drawing many independent datasets $\mathcal{D}_1, \mathcal{D}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{D}_M$ from $P_{data}$. Each yields an estimate $\hat{\theta}_m = \hat{\theta}(\mathcal{D}_m)$. The empirical distribution of these values is the **sampling distribution**.

**Step 3 — Compute Expected Value**
$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] = \int \hat{\theta}(\mathcal{D}) \, p(\mathcal{D}) \, d\mathcal{D}$$
If $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$, the estimator is unbiased — on average, across all possible samples, it hits the bullseye.

**Step 4 — Compute Variance**
The spread around $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]$ measures how much individual samples deviate. A small spread means the estimator is *robust* to data perturbations.

**Step 5 — Tradeoff Analysis**
Increasing model capacity (more parameters, deeper networks) typically **decreases bias** but **increases variance**. Regularization, dropout, and early stopping all work by deliberately re-introducing some bias to reduce variance.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Range / Property | Engineering Significance |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Estimator | $\hat{\theta} = g(x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(n)})$ | A measurable function of data | Maps data → parameter estimate |
| 2 | Bias | $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | Systematic error |
| 3 | Variance | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}])^{2}\right]$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Sampling fluctuation |
| 4 | Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + \text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})^{2}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Total error |
| 5 | Standard Error | $\text{SE}(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta})}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Magnitude of typical deviation |
| 6 | Sample Mean Estimator | $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Unbiased for $\mu$ | Variance $= \sigma^{2}/n$ |
| 7 | Sample Variance Estimator (Bessel) | $S^{2} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^{2}$ | Unbiased for $\sigma^{2}$ | Uses $n-1$ (Bessel's correction) |
| 8 | MLE Variance (Cramer–Rao Lower Bound) | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}) \geq \frac{1}{nI(\theta)}$ | $\geq 0$ | Theoretical minimum variance |
| 9 | Consistency | $\lim_{n \to \infty} \hat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ | Asymptotic | Converges in probability |
| 10 | Efficiency | $\text{eff}(\hat{\theta}) = \frac{\text{CRLB}}{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta})}$ | $(0, 1]$ | 1 = most efficient |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Device (for KTU viva)**: "**B**ias hits the **B**ullseye on average, **V**ariance is the **V**ibration around the average."

---

## 2.3 Worked Examples of Bias and Variance

### Example A — Sample Mean
Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ i.i.d.
- **Estimator**: $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$
- **Bias**: $\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}] = \mu \Rightarrow \text{Bias} = 0$ (unbiased)
- **Variance**: $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}$ (decreases with more data)

### Example B — MLE of Variance (Biased)
The maximum likelihood estimator of $\sigma^{2}$ is:
$$\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^{2}$$
- **Bias**: $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}}] = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^{2}$, so $\text{Bias} = -\frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}$ (biased downward)
- **Correction (Bessel's)**: $S^{2} = \frac{n}{n-1}\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^{2}$ is unbiased.

### Example C — Regression Error Decomposition
For a regression model $\hat{f}(x)$ trying to estimate $f(x)$:
$$\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^{2}\right] = \underbrace{\sigma^{2}}_{\text{Irreducible}} + \text{Bias}^{2}(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x))$$

This is the most important identity in supervised learning model diagnostics.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Computer Vision** | Diagnosing CNN overfitting vs underfitting using validation curves |
| **NLP** | Bias–variance tradeoff in language model fine-tuning |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | Choosing between model-based (low variance, high bias) and model-free (high variance, low bias) estimators |
| **Signal Processing** | Bias-variance tradeoff in Kalman filters |
| **Bayesian Deep Learning** | Using posterior distributions to directly quantify estimator uncertainty |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Bias–Variance Decomposition (MSE)

We begin with the **Mean Squared Error** of an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ with respect to the true parameter $\theta$:

$$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^{2}\right]$$

### Step 1 — Algebraic Manipulation (Add and Subtract $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]$)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) &= \mathbb{E}\left[\left(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta\right)^{2}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

This adds and subtracts the mean estimator $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]$ inside the squared term — a standard algebraic identity trick.

### Step 2 — Expand the Squared Binomial

$$\begin{aligned}
(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^{2} &= (\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}])^{2} + (\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^{2} \\
&\quad + 2(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}])(\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)
\end{aligned}$$

Three terms emerge: the squared deviation from the mean, the squared bias, and a cross-term.

### Step 3 — Take Expectation of Each Term

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) &= \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}])^{2}\right] + \mathbb{E}\left[(\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^{2}\right] \\
&\quad + 2\,\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}])(\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Each Expectation

The first term is the **variance** definition:
$$\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}])^{2}\right] = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$$

The second term is a constant raised to its square, so:
$$\mathbb{E}\left[(\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^{2}\right] = (\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^{2} = \text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})^{2}$$

The third term vanishes because:
$$\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}])(\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)\right] = (\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)\underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]\right]}_{=\,0} = 0$$

### Step 5 — Assemble the Final Identity

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + \text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity holds for **any** estimator of any parameter under any loss function that is squared error. It is the central theoretical justification for regularization, dropout, ensembling, and early stopping in deep learning.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sample Mean is Unbiased

For $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ i.i.d. with $\mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}] &= \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[X_i] = \frac{1}{n} \cdot n\mu = \mu
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$\text{Bias}(\bar{X}) = \mathbb{E}[\bar{X}] - \mu = 0$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Variance of the Sample Mean

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\bar{X}) &= \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \frac{1}{n^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i) \\
&= \frac{1}{n^{2}} \cdot n\sigma^{2} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}
\end{aligned}$$

The variance shrinks at rate $1/n$ — a foundational justification for the **power of more data** in machine learning.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: MLE Variance Estimator is Biased

For i.i.d. $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ with true variance $\sigma^{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\left[\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}}\right] &= \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^{2}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i^{2} - 2X_i\bar{X} + \bar{X}^{2})\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\left(\sum \mathbb{E}[X_i^{2}] - 2\bar{X}\sum \mathbb{E}[X_i] + n\,\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}^{2}]\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Using $\mathbb{E}[X_i^{2}] = \sigma^{2} + \mu^{2}$ and $\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}^{2}] = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n} + \mu^{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\left[\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}}\right] &= \frac{1}{n}\left(n(\sigma^{2} + \mu^{2}) - 2n\mu^{2} + n\left(\frac{\sigma^{2}}{n} + \mu^{2}\right)\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\left(n\sigma^{2} + n\mu^{2} - 2n\mu^{2} + \sigma^{2} + n\mu^{2}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\left((n-1)\sigma^{2}\right) = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

So:
$$\text{Bias}(\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}}) = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^{2} - \sigma^{2} = -\frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical example used in KTU board exams to test whether students understand the **direction and magnitude of bias** — the MLE **systematically underestimates** variance because it uses $\bar{X}$ (a random quantity) in place of the true mean $\mu$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Bessel-Corrected Estimator is Unbiased

$$S^{2} = \frac{n}{n-1}\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[S^{2}] &= \frac{n}{n-1}\mathbb{E}[\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}}] = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^{2} = \sigma^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore: $\text{Bias}(S^{2}) = 0$. The factor $\frac{n}{n-1}$ is called **Bessel's correction**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Estimators

| Estimator | Target | Bias | Variance | Unbiased? |
|-----------|--------|------|----------|-----------|
| Sample Mean $\bar{X}$ | $\mu$ | $0$ | $\sigma^{2}/n$ | ✓ |
| MLE Variance $\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}}$ | $\sigma^{2}$ | $-\sigma^{2}/n$ | $2\sigma^{4}(n-1)/n^{2}$ | ✗ |
| Bessel $S^{2}$ | $\sigma^{2}$ | $0$ | $2\sigma^{4}/(n-1)$ | ✓ |
| MLE Mean $\bar{X}$ | $\mu$ | $0$ | $\sigma^{2}/n$ | ✓ |
| MLE of Bernoulli $p$ | $p$ | $0$ | $p(1-p)/n$ | ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Diagram — Bias and Variance via Dartboard

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the dartboard analogy and the four canonical estimator regimes:

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["True Parameter Theta"]:::target
    nodeB["High Bias Low Variance"]:::warn
    nodeC["Low Bias High Variance"]:::warn
    nodeD["Low Bias Low Variance"]:::good
    nodeE["High Bias High Variance"]:::bad

    nodeF["Systematic error but stable"]:::label
    nodeG["Accurate on average but unstable"]:::label
    nodeH["Ideal estimator"]:::label
    nodeI["Worst case estimator"]:::label

    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeI

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeA --- nodeC
    nodeA --- nodeD
    nodeA --- nodeE

    classDef target fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#2b2b2b,stroke:#ff3333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef label fill:#eef2ff,stroke:#3333cc,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Bias–Variance Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["Observed Data D"]:::input
    node2["Estimator Function g D"]:::process
    node3["Point Estimate theta hat"]:::output
    node4["Expectation over D"]:::compute
    node5["Bias Component"]:::bias
    node6["Variance Component"]:::var
    node7["MSE Total Error"]:::total

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node4 --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define the bias of an estimator. When is an estimator said to be unbiased?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **bias** of an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ for a true parameter $\theta$ is defined as:
> $$\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$$
> An estimator is said to be **unbiased** if its bias equals zero, i.e., $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$. This means the average of the estimates obtained from repeated sampling equals the true parameter value.
> *(1 mark: defining bias | 1 mark: formula | 1 mark: unbiasedness condition)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]**
> **Q2. State the Bias–Variance Decomposition of the Mean Squared Error.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> For any estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a parameter $\theta$:
> $$\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^{2}\right] = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + \left(\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})\right)^{2}$$
> The **left-hand side** is the Mean Squared Error (MSE), which decomposes into two additive components: **variance** (sensitivity to the sample) and **squared bias** (systematic deviation from the true parameter). The cross-term vanishes due to linearity of expectation.
> *(1 mark: stating identity | 1 mark: defining each term | 1 mark: significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Full Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Model Question Paper Style]**
> **Q3A. (a)** Derive the Bias–Variance decomposition of the Mean Squared Error for an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of parameter $\theta$. Explain the practical significance in machine learning. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider i.i.d. samples $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$. Show that $\hat{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^{2}$ is a biased estimator of $\sigma^{2}$, and find the bias. Explain Bessel's correction. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Q3A(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the MSE definition:
$$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Introduce $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]$ via add-and-subtract trick:
$$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}\left[\left((\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]) + (\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Expand the square, take expectation, and show the cross-term vanishes:
$$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}])^{2}\right] + (\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^{2} + 2(\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]]}_{0}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Identify each component:
$$\boxed{\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + \text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})^{2}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Practical significance — bias represents underfitting, variance represents overfitting; tradeoff guides model selection, regularization, and capacity tuning.

### Model Solution — Q3A(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the MLE estimator and expand:
$$\hat{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^{2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(X_i^{2} - 2X_i\bar{X} + \bar{X}^{2}\right)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply expectation:
$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{\sigma}^{2}] = \frac{1}{n}\left(\sum \mathbb{E}[X_i^{2}] - 2\bar{X}\sum \mathbb{E}[X_i] + n\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}^{2}]\right)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Substitute $\mathbb{E}[X_i^{2}] = \sigma^{2} + \mu^{2}$ and $\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}^{2}] = \sigma^{2}/n + \mu^{2}$:
$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{\sigma}^{2}] = \frac{1}{n}\left(n(\sigma^{2}+\mu^{2}) - 2n\mu^{2} + \sigma^{2} + n\mu^{2}\right) = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^{2}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude:
$$\text{Bias}(\hat{\sigma}^{2}) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{\sigma}^{2}] - \sigma^{2} = -\frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Bessel's correction — multiply by $\frac{n}{n-1}$ to obtain unbiased $S^{2} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^{2}$.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 — Model Question Paper Style]**
> **Q3B. (a)** Define estimator, bias, and variance. For a sample mean $\bar{X}$ of $n$ i.i.d. random variables with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^{2}$, compute the bias and variance, and discuss how the variance changes as $n$ increases. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the bias–variance tradeoff in machine learning. Discuss how techniques like regularization and cross-validation help manage this tradeoff. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Q3B(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Definitions — estimator $\hat{\theta}$, bias $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$, variance $\mathbb{E}[(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}])^{2}]$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}]$:
$$\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu \Rightarrow \text{Bias}(\bar{X}) = 0$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $\text{Var}(\bar{X})$ using independence:
$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{1}{n^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\text{Var}(X_i) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Interpretation — variance shrinks linearly in $1/n$; quadrupling the sample halves the variance.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclusion — sample mean is unbiased and consistent with rate $\mathcal{O}(1/n)$.

### Model Solution — Q3B(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Draw / describe the **U-shaped MSE curve** as model complexity increases. Bias decreases monotonically; variance increases monotonically; MSE has a minimum at optimal complexity.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Relate to underfitting (high bias, low variance) vs overfitting (low bias, high variance). The bullseye diagram from Section 4.1 is the visual aid.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Regularization (L1/L2, dropout) — adds a small bias to drastically reduce variance, shifting MSE minimum to lower region.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Cross-validation — empirically estimates generalization error to locate the optimal complexity for the data.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Ensemble methods (bagging) reduce variance; boosting reduces bias — concrete remedies grounded in the decomposition.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the squaring of bias** — Students often write MSE = Variance + Bias, omitting the square. Bias must be squared because the identity is built from $(\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^{2}$. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Mixing up "estimator" vs "estimate"** — Estimator is the random variable (function of data); estimate is the realized value. Examiners test this distinction. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Skipping the cross-term elimination** — In the derivation, students often jump from the expansion to the final identity without showing $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]] = 0$. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Forgetting $n-1$ (Bessel's correction)** — In the variance question, writing $\frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ and not addressing why it's biased costs the Bessel-corrected part. **(−2 marks)**
> 5. **Misinterpreting bias direction** — MLE of $\sigma^2$ is biased *downward* (underestimates variance). Students often say "overestimates" — wrong. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Omitting units / domains** — Variance is non-negative; bias is in the same units as $\theta$; squared bias is in squared units. **(−0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Estimators, Bias & Variance (Module 1)**

- **Estimator** $\hat{\theta}$ = function of data; **estimate** = realized numeric value.
- **Bias** $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$; **zero bias = unbiased**.
- **Variance** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}[(\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}])^{2}]$; measures sample-to-sample fluctuation.
- **MSE Identity**: $\text{MSE} = \text{Variance} + \text{Bias}^{2}$ — *always squared bias*.
- Cross-term in derivation vanishes because $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]] = 0$.
- **Sample mean** is unbiased for $\mu$; variance $= \sigma^{2}/n$ (shrinks as $1/n$).
- **MLE variance** uses $\frac{1}{n}$ → biased downward by $-\sigma^{2}/n$.
- **Bessel's correction** uses $\frac{1}{n-1}$ → unbiased estimator of $\sigma^{2}$.
- **Dartboard analogy**: Bias = where the cluster is centered; Variance = spread of the cluster.
- **Bias → Underfitting**; **Variance → Overfitting** in deep learning models.
- Increasing model complexity → ↓ bias, ↑ variance; total MSE has a U-shape.
- **Regularization** (L1/L2/dropout) trades a small bias for large variance reduction.
- **Ensembling / bagging** reduces variance; **boosting** reduces bias.
- **Cramer–Rao Lower Bound**: $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{nI(\theta)}$ sets the *minimum achievable variance*.
- **Consistency**: $\hat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ as $n \to \infty$.
- **Efficiency** $\in (0,1]$: ratio of CRLB to actual variance; 1 = optimal.
- **KTU exam traps**: squaring bias, Bessel's $n-1$, unbiasedness of sample mean, direction of MLE bias.
- **Mnemonic**: "**Bias B**ullseye, **Variance V**ibration, **MSE = V + B²**".
- **In code-free subjects**: NO Python — express derivations purely in mathematical notation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Deep Learning
topics.content:
# Introduction to Deep Learning ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Deep Learning (DL)** is a specialized subfield of **Machine Learning (ML)** that employs **artificial neural networks (ANNs)** with multiple hidden layers (deep architectures) to automatically learn hierarchical representations of data. Unlike traditional machine learning, deep learning does not rely on hand-crafted feature engineering; instead, it discovers intricate structural patterns directly from raw, high-dimensional inputs such as images, audio, text, and time-series signals. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (PECDT695):** Deep Learning is a class of machine learning algorithms that uses multiple layers of non-linear processing units for feature extraction, transformation, and representation learning, where each successive layer uses the output of the previous layer as input. Mathematically, a deep learning model can be represented as a **composite function** $f(x; \theta)$ that maps an input $x$ to an output $y$ through a chain of $L$ transformations: $$y = f^{(L)}(f^{(L-1)}(\cdots f^{(2)}(f^{(1)}(x))\cdots))$$ where each $f^{(l)}$ represents a layer's transformation parameterized by weights $\theta^{(l)}$. ## 1.2 The AI → ML → DL Hierarchy | Field | Full Form | Scope | Key Characteristic | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **AI** | Artificial Intelligence | Broadest — any technique enabling machines to mimic human intelligence | Rule-based + learned systems | | **ML** | Machine Learning | Subset of AI — systems that learn from data | Statistical pattern recognition | | **DL** | Deep Learning | Subset of ML — uses deep neural networks | Automatic hierarchical feature extraction | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to clearly articulate the relationship **AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL** and understand that DL is distinguished by its use of **deep architectures (multiple hidden layers)** and **representation learning**. ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — "How a Child Learns to Recognize a Dog" Imagine teaching a toddler to recognize a **dog**: - **Traditional Machine Learning Approach:** You must explicitly define features — *four legs, tail, fur, barks, snout shape*. You hand-engineer these descriptors and feed them to a classifier like SVM. The system is only as good as your feature engineering. - **Deep Learning Approach:** You simply show the child thousands of photos labeled *"dog"* and *"not dog."* The child's brain (analogous to a deep neural network) **automatically** builds a hierarchy of concepts: - **Layer 1:** Edges and color gradients - **Layer 2:** Textures (fur, whiskers) - **Layer 3:** Parts (ears, nose, tail) - **Layer 4:** Full object (dog) > [!TIP] > **Intuition Capture:** Deep Learning = **letting the model build its own feature hierarchy from raw data**, mimicking how biological brains (specifically the visual cortex) process information in layers. ## 1.4 Why "Deep"? The Notion of Depth The term **"deep"** refers to the number of **hidden layers** in the network. KTU's working definition: $$\text{Depth} = L - 1$$ where $L$ is the total number of layers (excluding input). Shallow networks have 1–2 hidden layers; deep networks typically have $L \geq 3$ hidden layers, with modern architectures reaching hundreds or thousands (e.g., GPT, ResNet-152). # Theoretical Foundations & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Historical Motivation — Why Deep Learning Emerged Traditional machine learning hit a **performance plateau** in the early 2010s due to the following limitations: - **Curse of Dimensionality:** High-dimensional data (images with millions of pixels) caused classical algorithms to fail. - **Manual Feature Engineering:** Required domain experts to handcraft features — expensive and non-scalable. - **Shallow Representations:** Algorithms like SVMs and shallow MLPs could not capture complex hierarchical patterns. - **Data Silos:** With the rise of the internet, massive datasets became available, but shallow models could not exploit them fully. > [!NOTE] > The **"big bang" of deep learning** occurred around **2012**, when AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al.) dramatically reduced the ImageNet error rate from 26% to 15.3%, using GPU-accelerated CNNs. ## 2.2 Core Components of a Deep Learning System A deep learning system consists of **four essential pillars**: 1. **Data:** Large-scale labeled/unlabeled datasets (e.g., ImageNet with 14M images). 2. **Model Architecture:** A deep neural network (CNN, RNN, Transformer, etc.) with learnable parameters. 3. **Loss Function:** Quantifies the discrepancy between predicted and actual outputs. 4. **Optimization Algorithm:** Iteratively updates parameters to minimize the loss (e.g., SGD, Adam). ## 2.3 The Mathematical Building Block — A Single Neuron The **artificial neuron** (perceptron) is the fundamental computational unit. It performs two operations: ### Step 1: Linear Combination (Pre-activation) $$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$ where: - $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ is the input vector -
