$$\theta_{t+1} = 0.6 \theta_t + 2.0$$

**Step 3: Iteration $t = 0 \to t = 1$.**

$$\theta_1 = 0.6 \cdot \theta_0 + 2.0 = 0.6 \cdot 1 + 2.0 = 0.6 + 2.0 = 2.6$$

$$J(\theta_1) = (2.6 - 5)^2 = (-2.4)^2 = 5.76$$

**Step 4: Iteration $t = 1 \to t = 2$.**

$$\theta_2 = 0.6 \cdot \theta_1 + 2.0 = 0.6 \cdot 2.6 + 2.0 = 1.56 + 2.0 = 3.56$$

$$J(\theta_2) = (3.56 - 5)^2 = (-1.44)^2 = 2.0736$$

**Step 5: Verify the trend.**

| Iteration | $\theta_t$ | $J(\theta_t)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 1.00 | 16.00 |
| 1 | 2.60 | 5.76 |
| 2 | 3.56 | 2.07 |

The parameters are approaching the true minimum $\theta^* = 5$ (where $J = 0$) with each iteration, and the loss is monotonically decreasing — confirming correct GD behavior.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the update rule.** The direction is *opposite* to the gradient; writing $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \eta \nabla J$ is a critical error (sign flip).
> 2. **Confusing the loss function $J$ with the per-sample loss $L$.** Always clarify whether the gradient is over the full dataset (BGD) or a single sample (SGD).
> 3. **Skipping the Taylor expansion derivation.** In 7-mark questions, examiners expect the mathematical justification of *why* the negative gradient direction is used, not just the formula.
> 4. **Numerical calculation errors in iteration problems.** Always show all intermediate steps; partial credit requires visible computation.
> 5. **Not mentioning convergence criteria.** A complete answer must include *when* GD stops (gradient norm, loss plateau, max iterations).
> 6. **Ignoring practical challenges.** In 14-mark answers, purely theoretical treatment without discussing learning rate tuning, local minima, or saddle points will lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **GD Update Rule (memorize verbatim):** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$
- **Three Variants:** BGD (all data, stable, slow), SGD (1 sample, noisy, fast), MBGD (mini-batch, balanced, standard).
- **Learning Rate ($\eta$):** Controls step size; too small = slow, too large = diverge; typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$.
- **Convergence Criteria:** (i) $\Vert \nabla J \Vert < \epsilon$, (ii) $\vert J_{t+1} - J_t \vert < \epsilon$, (iii) max iterations.
- **Mathematical Proof:** First-order Taylor expansion proves that $-\eta \Vert \nabla J \Vert^2 \leq 0$, guaranteeing loss decrease.
- **Local Minima vs Saddle Points:** Non-convex landscapes contain both; GD stalls at both, but momentum helps escape.
- **Condition Number $\kappa(A)$:** Governs GD convergence rate; ill-conditioned problems converge slowly.
- **Adaptive Methods:** Adam, RMSProp, AdaGrad dynamically scale $\eta$ per parameter — the modern evolution of GD.
- **GD + Backpropagation:** GD provides the update rule; backpropagation provides the gradient — they are inseparable in deep learning.
- **Practical Standard:** MBGD with batch sizes 32, 64, 128, or 256 is the de facto choice in production deep learning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stochastic GD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stochastic GD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is a cornerstone of the *Optimization in Deep Learning* sub-unit in Module 2. Expect derivations of the update rule, comparison with Batch/Mini-Batch GD, and 14-mark analytical problems on convergence trade-offs.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** is an iterative first-order optimization algorithm used to minimize an *objective / loss function* $J(\theta)$ — typically the expected risk $E_{(x,y)\sim \mathcal{D}}[\mathcal{L}(f(x;\theta), y)]$ — by updating the model parameters $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^d$ using the **gradient computed from a single training example** (or a small mini-batch) randomly sampled from the dataset at each iteration.

Mathematically, the generic parameter update at iteration $t$ is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}\big(f(x^{(i_t)}; \theta_t), y^{(i_t)}\big)$$

where $\eta_t$ is the **learning rate** (a hyperparameter), and $i_t$ is a random index drawn uniformly from $\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}$ at each step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Batch GD:** In **Batch Gradient Descent**, the gradient is computed using the *entire* training set: $\nabla J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta)$. In **SGD**, the inner sum is replaced by a single sample, making the gradient a *noisy, unbiased estimator* of the true gradient.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blindfolded Hiker"

Imagine you are a **blindfolded hiker** standing on a foggy mountain, trying to reach the valley floor (the *minimum* of the loss function):

- **Batch GD** is like surveying the *entire mountain* (all training samples) to compute the true steepest descent direction before taking even *one step*. Highly accurate, but extremely slow.
- **Stochastic GD** is like feeling the slope of the ground *just under your left foot* (one random sample) and stepping in that direction. The path is **noisy and zig-zag-like**, but you take *thousands of small, fast steps*, often reaching the valley much faster in wall-clock time.
- **Mini-Batch GD** is the compromise — you feel a small patch of ground (e.g., 32 or 64 samples) before stepping.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the exam:** *"Batch = Big & Slow, Stochastic = Shuffle & Step, Mini-Batch = Best of Both."*

## 1.3 Why SGD is the Workhorse of Deep Learning

| Property | Role in Deep Learning |
|---|---|
| **Low per-iteration cost** $O(d)$ | Enables training on millions of parameters |
| **Unbiased gradient estimator** $E[\hat{g}] = \nabla J(\theta)$ | Guarantees convergence on average |
| **Inherent stochastic noise** | Acts as a *regularizer*, helping escape sharp local minima and saddle points |
| **Online learning capability** | Can learn from streaming data one sample at a time |
| **Scalability** | Core to backpropagation in neural networks |

## 1.4 Standard KTU Metric Symbols (Bookmark These)

- $N$ — total number of training samples (**bold** = standard in KTU solutions)
- $\theta$ — parameter vector (weights & biases)
- $\eta$ or $\alpha$ — learning rate, typically $\eta \in (0, 1]$
- $\nabla_\theta J(\theta)$ — true gradient of the loss
- $\hat{g}_t$ — stochastic (noisy) gradient estimate at step $t$
- $E$ — number of training epochs (**bold** = required notation)
- $B$ — mini-batch size (default **bold** for clarity)
- $t$ — iteration counter
- $J(\theta)$ — objective/loss function
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Paradigms of Gradient Descent (Comparison Matrix)

Let the per-sample loss be $\mathcal{L}_i(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(f(x^{(i)}; \theta), y^{(i)})$ and the empirical risk be:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}_i(\theta)$$

| Variant | Gradient Used per Update | Update Rule | Per-Step Cost | Noise Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | Full dataset average | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta_t)$ | $O(N \cdot d)$ | **Zero** (deterministic) |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | Single sample $i_t$ | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)$ | $O(d)$ | **High** |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | Subset $\mathcal{B}_t$ of size $B$ | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \frac{1}{B}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta_t)$ | $O(B \cdot d)$ | **Moderate** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often award marks for explicitly stating that **SGD is equivalent to Mini-Batch GD with $B = 1$**.

## 2.2 The Complete SGD Algorithm (Pseudocode Logic Flow)

The following structured steps represent the canonical SGD algorithm as taught in KTU Module 2:

1. **Initialize** parameters $\theta_0 \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (randomly, e.g., He or Xavier initialization).
2. **Choose** a learning rate schedule $\{\eta_t\}_{t=0}^{T-1}$ (constant, step-decay, exponential, or cosine).
3. **For** epoch $e = 1, 2, \ldots, E$ **do**:
    - **Shuffle** the training set: randomly permute indices $\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}$.
    - **For** each sample $i$ in shuffled order **do**:
        - Compute the stochastic gradient: $\hat{g}_t = \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_i(\theta_t)$.
        - Update parameters: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \hat{g}_t$.
4. **Return** final parameters $\theta_T$.

## 2.3 The "Why" — Intuition Behind Each Step

- **Shuffling** prevents cyclic bias and ensures the *expected* gradient equals the true gradient: $E[\hat{g}_t] = \nabla J(\theta_t)$.
- **Decaying learning rate** $\eta_t \to 0$ (e.g., $\eta_t = \eta_0 / (1 + t/\tau)$) is *required* for almost-sure convergence under standard assumptions (Robbins-Monro conditions).
- **Single-sample updates** yield $N$ parameter updates per epoch, whereas Batch GD gives only 1.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robbins-Monro Convergence Conditions** (frequently asked in 14-mark questions):
> $$\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \eta_t = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 < \infty$$
> These ensure SGD converges *almost surely* to a local minimum for *convex* objectives and to a stationary point for *non-convex* deep learning losses.

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)$ | Core SGD update rule | 3-mark / 7-mark derivations |
| 2 | $E[\hat{g}_t] = \nabla J(\theta_t)$ | Unbiasedness of stochastic gradient | 3-mark proof questions |
| 3 | $\text{Var}[\hat{g}_t] \leq \sigma^2$ (finite variance) | Bounded second-moment assumption | Convergence proofs |
| 4 | $E[J(\theta_t)] - J(\theta^*) = O(1/\sqrt{t})$ | Convergence rate (convex, smooth) | Theoretical analysis |
| 5 | $\eta_t = \eta_0 (1 + \gamma t)^{-1}$ | Standard $1/t$ decay schedule | Practical scenarios |
| 6 | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \hat{g}_t + \beta(\theta_t - \theta_{t-1})$ | SGD with **momentum** (Polyak, $\beta \in [0.9, 0.99]$) | Advanced variants |
| 7 | $g \leftarrow \beta g + (1-\beta)\hat{g}_t$ | Momentum buffer update | Algorithm tracing |
| 8 | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \frac{\hat{g}_t}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_t} + \epsilon}$ | **RMSProp / Adam-style** adaptive scaling | Comparison questions |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** KTU evaluators **deduct marks** if you write SGD's update rule without the *subscript $i_t$* or sample index — it is the defining feature of stochasticity. Always write: $\nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)$ where $i_t \sim \text{Uniform}\{1, \ldots, N\}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer Vision:** SGD (with momentum) trains ResNet, VGG, and YOLO detectors.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Word2Vec, BERT pre-training rely on SGD over millions of tokens.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Policy gradient methods (REINFORCE, PPO) use SGD to update policy networks.
- **Industrial Recommendation Systems:** SGD enables real-time model updates on streaming user data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The SGD Update Rule from First Principles

We start from the *empirical risk minimization* objective for a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}_i(\theta)$$

**Step 1 — Define the full-batch gradient.** The true gradient of the empirical risk is:

$$\nabla J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the computational bottleneck.** Computing this sum requires a full pass over $N$ samples *every iteration*. For $N = 10^6$ and $T = 10^4$ iterations, this is $10^{10}$ gradient evaluations — infeasible for deep networks.

**Step 3 — Replace the full sum with a single-sample Monte Carlo estimate.** Sample one index $i_t$ uniformly at random from $\{1, \ldots, N\}$. Define the stochastic gradient:

$$\hat{g}_t \;=\; \nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)$$

**Step 4 — Verify unbiasedness (Key Proof for KTU).** Compute the expectation over the random index $i_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{i_t}\!\left[\hat{g}_t\right] &= E_{i_t}\!\left[\nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)\right] \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \cdot \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta_t) \quad \text{(law of total expectation, uniform sampling)} \\
&= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta_t) \\
&= \nabla J(\theta_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $E[\hat{g}_t] = \nabla J(\theta_t)$ — the stochastic gradient is an **unbiased estimator** of the true gradient. $\blacksquare$

**Step 5 — Apply the gradient descent step.** Using the first-order Taylor expansion of $J$ around $\theta_t$, we move *downhill* by a small step of size $\eta_t$:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \hat{g}_t = \theta_t - \eta_t \cdot \nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)$$

This is the **Stochastic Gradient Descent update rule**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Convergence Rate of SGD (Convex Case)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU ESE. Memorize the assumptions and the final rate.

**Assumptions (Standard in KTU textbooks — Bott, Courville, Bengio / Goodfellow et al.):**
1. $J(\theta)$ is **convex** and differentiable.
2. $J(\theta)$ is **$L$-smooth**: $\vert\vert \nabla J(\theta) - \nabla J(\theta') \vert\vert \leq L \vert\vert \theta - \theta' \vert\vert$.
3. The stochastic gradient has **bounded variance**: $E[\vert\vert \hat{g}_t \vert\vert^2] \leq G^2$ for some constant $G$.
4. The optimal value is bounded: $J(\theta) \geq J(\theta^*) = J^*$.

**Step 1 — Smoothness implies a quadratic upper bound.** From $L$-smoothness:

$$J(\theta_{t+1}) \leq J(\theta_t) + \nabla J(\theta_t)^T(\theta_{t+1} - \theta_t) + \frac{L}{2}\vert\vert \theta_{t+1} - \theta_t \vert\vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the SGD update.** Note $\theta_{t+1} - \theta_t = -\eta_t \hat{g}_t$, so $\vert\vert \theta_{t+1} - \theta_t \vert\vert^2 = \eta_t^2 \vert\vert \hat{g}_t \vert\vert^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(\theta_{t+1}) &\leq J(\theta_t) - \eta_t \nabla J(\theta_t)^T \hat{g}_t + \frac{L \eta_t^2}{2}\vert\vert \hat{g}_t \vert\vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Take expectation conditioned on $\theta_t$.** Using $E[\hat{g}_t \vert \theta_t] = \nabla J(\theta_t)$ and $E[\vert\vert \hat{g}_t \vert\vert^2 \vert \theta_t] \leq G^2$:

$$E[J(\theta_{t+1}) \vert \theta_t] \leq J(\theta_t) - \eta_t \vert\vert \nabla J(\theta_t) \vert\vert^2 + \frac{L \eta_t^2 G^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Use convexity.** For convex $J$, $\vert\vert \nabla J(\theta_t) \vert\vert^2 \geq 2(J(\theta_t) - J^*)$. Substituting:

$$E[J(\theta_{t+1}) \vert \theta_t] \leq J(\theta_t) - 2\eta_t(J(\theta_t) - J^*) + \frac{L \eta_t^2 G^2}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Choose optimal constant learning rate** $\eta_t = \eta = \sqrt{2(J(\theta_0) - J^*) / (L G^2 T)}$ and telescope the inequality over $T$ steps. The resulting **sublinear convergence rate** is:

$$E[J(\theta_T)] - J^* \;=\; O\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}\right)$$

This is **slower** than Batch GD's $O(1/T)$ rate in iteration-count, but **faster in wall-clock time** because each SGD iteration is $N$ times cheaper. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SGD with Momentum (Exponential Moving Average of Gradients)

**Motivating "Why":** SGD's noisy updates oscillate in directions of high curvature. **Momentum** smooths these oscillations by accumulating a velocity vector.

**Velocity update:**

$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1-\beta)\hat{g}_t \quad \text{where } \beta \in [0, 1)$$

**Parameter update:**

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t v_{t+1}$$

**Unrolling the recursion** to see the effective gradient is an *exponentially weighted moving average*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{t+1} &= (1-\beta)\hat{g}_t + \beta v_t \\
&= (1-\beta)\hat{g}_t + \beta\big[(1-\beta)\hat{g}_{t-1} + \beta v_{t-1}\big] \\
&= (1-\beta)\hat{g}_t + \beta(1-\beta)\hat{g}_{t-1} + \beta^2 v_{t-1} \\
&= (1-\beta)\sum_{\tau=0}^{t} \beta^{t-\tau} \hat{g}_\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

Each past gradient $\hat{g}_\tau$ is weighted by $\beta^{t-\tau}$, so older gradients contribute less. With $\beta = 0.9$, the **effective horizon** is approximately $1/(1-\beta) = 10$ steps. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Comparison: Why SGD Beats Batch GD in Practice (Intuitive Loss Landscape Analysis)

| Aspect | Batch GD | SGD |
|---|---|---|
| **Loss landscape navigation** | Stops at nearest local minimum (often sharp) | Escapes sharp minima due to noise; prefers **flat minima** which generalize better |
| **Plateau / saddle points** | Slow to escape (gradient $\approx 0$) | Noise gives random kicks → escapes faster |
| **Memory footprint** | Stores full gradient ($N$ terms) | Stores single sample gradient |
| **Parallelism** | Vectorized over $N$ | Easily parallelized over mini-batches |
| **Convergence (theoretical)** | $O(1/T)$ iterations | $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ iterations |
| **Convergence (practical wall-clock)** | Slower | Faster |

> [!NOTE]
> **Flat Minima Hypothesis (KTU Advanced):** Keskar et al. (2017) showed that large-batch SGD converges to *sharp* minima that generalize poorly, while small-batch / pure SGD prefers *flat* minima with better test accuracy. This is why modern deep learning rarely uses Batch GD.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SGD Optimization Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Initialize theta_0 and learning rate eta"] --> B["Shuffle training dataset indices"]
    B --> C["Sample one training example with index i_t"]
    C --> D["Compute stochastic gradient g_t = grad L_i_t with respect to theta_t"]
    D --> E["Apply update rule: theta_new = theta_t minus eta times g_t"]
    E --> F{"Convergence criterion met? Loss below epsilon or max iterations reached"}
    F -- "No" --> G{"All epochs completed?"}
    G -- "No" --> B
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Output final parameters theta_T"]
    F -- "Yes" --> H
```

## 4.2 Loss Landscape Trajectory Comparison (SGD vs Batch GD)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Loss landscape with multiple minima"] --> B["Batch GD path: Direct descent to nearest sharp minimum"]
    A --> C["SGD path: Noisy zigzag trajectory toward flat minimum"]
    B --> D["Sharp minimum: Poor generalization on test data"]
    C --> E["Flat minimum: Better generalization on test data"]
    D --> F["Test accuracy: Lower"]
    E --> G["Test accuracy: Higher"]
```

## 4.3 SGD with Momentum — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Input Layer"
        I["Training sample x_i_t and label y_i_t"]
    end

    subgraph "Gradient Computation Block"
        G["Compute stochastic gradient g_t from current parameters theta_t"]
    end

    subgraph "Momentum Buffer Block"
        M["Velocity v_t held in memory from previous step"]
        N["Update v_new = beta times v_t plus 1 minus beta times g_t"]
    end

    subgraph "Parameter Update Block"
        U["Compute theta_new = theta_t minus eta times v_new"]
    end

    subgraph "Feedback Loop"
        F["Pass theta_new back as theta_t for next iteration"]
    end

    I --> G
    G --> N
    M --> N
    N --> U
    U --> F
    F --> G
```

## 4.4 Mini-Batch Size Trade-off Schematic (KTU Favourite Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Choose mini-batch size B"] --> B{"B equals 1?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Pure SGD: Maximum noise, minimum memory, fast per step"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"B equals N?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Batch GD: Zero noise, maximum memory, slow per step"]
    D -- "No" --> F["Mini-Batch GD: Balanced noise and memory, hardware-friendly on GPU"]
    C --> G["Typical use: Online learning, streaming data"]
    E --> H["Typical use: Small datasets, theoretical analysis"]
    F --> I["Typical use: Deep learning training with B = 32, 64, 128, 256"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to *"draw the SGD algorithm flow"*, you must always include: (1) dataset shuffling, (2) random sampling of $i_t$, (3) gradient computation, (4) parameter update with learning rate $\eta_t$, and (5) convergence check. Missing the shuffling step costs 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Mark Drain #1:** Writing "Gradient Descent" instead of "Stochastic Gradient Descent" loses the 2-mark distinction mark. Always mention the *random sample index $i_t$*.
> - **Mark Drain #2:** Forgetting the Robbins-Monro conditions ($\sum \eta_t = \infty$ and $\sum \eta_t^2 < \infty$) in a 14-mark convergence question costs 4 marks.
> - **Mark Drain #3:** Confusing "iteration" and "epoch". In SGD, *one epoch* contains $N$ *iterations* (one per sample). Examiners test this distinction.
> - **Mark Drain #4:** Writing the update rule without a *negative sign* ($+ \eta \hat{g}_t$ instead of $-\eta \hat{g}_t$) — this makes SGD *diverge*. Easy to lose 2 marks.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`
**Differentiate between Batch Gradient Descent and Stochastic Gradient Descent in terms of gradient computation, computational cost per iteration, and convergence noise.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Batch GD | Stochastic GD |
|---|---|---|
| **Gradient computation** | Uses *all* $N$ training samples: $\nabla J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta)$ | Uses *one* randomly sampled example: $\hat{g}_t = \nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)$ |
| **Per-iteration cost** | $O(N \cdot d)$ — expensive for large $N$ | $O(d)$ — cheap and constant in $N$ |
| **Convergence noise** | Deterministic, smooth trajectory | Noisy, zig-zag trajectory; unbiased estimator $E[\hat{g}_t] = \nabla J(\theta_t)$ |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per row)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]`
**State the Robbins-Monro conditions for the convergence of SGD and explain why both conditions are necessary.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The Robbins-Monro conditions on the learning rate schedule $\{\eta_t\}_{t=0}^{\infty}$ are:

$$\text{(i) } \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \eta_t = \infty \qquad \text{(ii) } \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 < \infty$$

- **Condition (i)** ensures the step sizes are *large enough* in total to reach the minimum from any starting point.
- **Condition (ii)** ensures the step sizes *decay fast enough* so that the stochastic noise vanishes, enabling convergence.

A standard schedule satisfying both is $\eta_t = c / (t_0 + t)$ for constants $c > 0$, $t_0 > 0$. *(3 marks: 1 mark each condition + 1 mark for schedule example)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### `Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a) Derive the Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) update rule starting from the empirical risk minimization objective. Prove that the stochastic gradient is an unbiased estimator of the true gradient. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Empirical Risk Objective.** For a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, the empirical risk is:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}_i(\theta)$$

**Step 2 — True Gradient.** Differentiating with respect to $\theta$:

$$\nabla J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Stochastic Approximation.** Sample index $i_t$ uniformly at random. Define:

$$\hat{g}_t = \nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)$$

**Step 4 — Unbiasedness Proof.** By the law of total expectation with uniform sampling probability $P(i_t = i) = 1/N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{i_t}[\hat{g}_t] &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} P(i_t = i) \cdot \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta_t) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta_t) \\
&= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta_t) = \nabla J(\theta_t) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Update Rule.** Applying the first-order Taylor approximation and taking a descent step:

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks objective setup, 2 marks unbiasedness proof, 2 marks update rule derivation, 1 mark for proper indexing and justification)*

---

**(b) Explain the Robbins-Monro conditions for SGD convergence. Show that the schedule $\eta_t = c/(t_0 + t)$ satisfies both conditions, and comment on the practical implications for deep learning training. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Robbins-Monro Conditions.** To guarantee almost-sure convergence, the learning rate schedule must satisfy:

$$\text{(i) } \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \eta_t = \infty \qquad \text{(ii) } \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 < \infty$$

**Step 2 — Verify Condition (i) for $\eta_t = c/(t_0 + t)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \eta_t &= \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \frac{c}{t_0 + t} \\
&= c \sum_{k=t_0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k} \quad \text{(harmonic series)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The harmonic series diverges, so $\sum \eta_t = \infty$. $\checkmark$

**Step 3 — Verify Condition (ii).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 &= \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \frac{c^2}{(t_0 + t)^2} \\
&= c^2 \sum_{k=t_0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k^2} \quad \text{(p-series with } p = 2 > 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Basel-related p-series converges for $p = 2$, so $\sum \eta_t^2 < \infty$. $\checkmark$

**Step 4 — Practical Implications.**
- Modern deep learning uses **constant** or **step-decayed** learning rates that *violate* the strict Robbins-Monro conditions but work well in practice.
- Schedules like **cosine annealing** ($\eta_t = \eta_{\min} + \frac{1}{2}(\eta_{\max} - \eta_{\min})(1 + \cos(\pi t / T))$) and **warm restarts** are empirical best practices.
- In practice, we *stop* training after a finite number of epochs rather than waiting for theoretical convergence.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for stating conditions, 3 marks for verifying both summations, 2 marks for practical implications)*

---

### `Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style, Alternative Choice]`

**(a) Compare Batch GD, Mini-Batch GD, and Stochastic GD with respect to: (i) gradient computation, (ii) per-iteration cost, (iii) noise level, (iv) convergence in iterations, and (v) suitability for large-scale deep learning. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Let $N$ = total samples, $B$ = mini-batch size (with $1 \leq B \leq N$), $d$ = parameter dimension.

| Aspect | Batch GD ($B = N$) | Mini-Batch GD ($1 < B < N$) | Stochastic GD ($B = 1$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Gradient** | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta)$ | $\frac{1}{B}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_t} \nabla \mathcal{L}_i(\theta)$ | $\nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)$ |
| **(ii) Cost per step** | $O(N \cdot d)$ | $O(B \cdot d)$ | $O(d)$ |
| **(iii) Noise** | Zero (deterministic) | Moderate (decreases as $\sqrt{B}$) | High |
| **(iv) Iteration rate** | $O(1/T)$ (fast) | $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ | $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ |
| **(v) Best for** | Small datasets, convex problems | **Deep learning, GPUs** | Online/streaming data |

**Key Insight:** Mini-batch GD with $B \in \{32, 64, 128, 256\}$ is the *de facto* standard in deep learning because it exploits GPU parallelism, balances noise vs. stability, and achieves near-Batch gradient quality at a fraction of the cost.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per row + 1 mark for the practical insight on $B$ choice)*

---

**(b) Derive the update rule for SGD with **momentum**. Show that the velocity vector is an exponentially weighted moving average of past stochastic gradients. Explain how momentum helps overcome poor conditioning of the loss landscape. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Velocity Update.** Introduce a velocity variable $v_t$ initialized as $v_0 = 0$:

$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1 - \beta)\hat{g}_t, \quad \beta \in [0, 1)$$

**Step 2 — Parameter Update.**

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t v_{t+1}$$

**Step 3 — Unroll the Recursion.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{t+1} &= (1-\beta)\hat{g}_t + \beta v_t \\
&= (1-\beta)\hat{g}_t + \beta\big[(1-\beta)\hat{g}_{t-1} + \beta v_{t-1}\big] \\
&= (1-\beta)\hat{g}_t + \beta(1-\beta)\hat{g}_{t-1} + \beta^2 v_{t-1} \\
&= (1-\beta)\sum_{\tau=0}^{t} \beta^{t-\tau} \hat{g}_\tau \quad \text{(by induction)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

The weights $(1-\beta)\beta^{t-\tau}$ sum to 1 (geometric series), so $v_{t+1}$ is a valid **exponentially weighted moving average** of past stochastic gradients.

**Step 4 — Intuition for Poor Conditioning.**
- In a loss landscape with very different curvatures along different axes (high *condition number*), vanilla SGD oscillates across the steep direction while crawling along the shallow direction.
- Momentum **accumulates velocity** in consistent directions (shallow axis) and **damps oscillations** in inconsistent directions (steep axis), effectively acting as a low-pass filter on the gradient noise.
- The effective horizon is $1/(1-\beta)$; with $\beta = 0.9$, the gradient history spans ~10 steps.

*(7 marks: 1 mark velocity update, 1 mark parameter update, 3 marks recursion unrolling, 2 marks conditioning explanation)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Punch-List for SGD (Module 2 — KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- **Core Update Rule:** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t \nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)$, with $i_t$ sampled uniformly at random.
- **Unbiasedness:** $E_{i_t}[\nabla \mathcal{L}_{i_t}(\theta_t)] = \nabla J(\theta_t)$ — always include this proof in 7-mark questions.
- **Variance Reduction:** Variance of stochastic gradient $\leq \sigma^2$ (finite, but non-zero) — distinguishes SGD from Batch GD.
- **Three Variants:** Batch ($B = N$), Mini-Batch ($1 < B < N$), SGD ($B = 1$) — must know costs and noise levels.
- **Robbins-Monro Conditions:** $\sum \eta_t = \infty$ AND $\sum \eta_t^2 < \infty$ — *both* needed for theoretical convergence.
- **Convergence Rate (Convex, Smooth):** $E[J(\theta_T)] - J^* = O(1/\sqrt{T})$ — slower than Batch GD's $O(1/T)$ per iteration, but faster in wall-clock time.
- **Epoch vs Iteration:** 1 epoch = $N$ iterations (for SGD); 1 epoch = 1 iteration (for Batch GD).
- **Momentum Variant:** $v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1-\beta)\hat{g}_t$; $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta_t v_{t+1}$; helps in ill-conditioned landscapes.
- **Practical Default:** $B = 32$ or $64$, $\eta_0 = 0.01$ or $0.001$, $\beta = 0.9$ (momentum) — typical deep learning recipe.
- **Flat vs Sharp Minima:** SGD's noise biases optimization toward flat minima, which generalize better (Keskar et al.).
- **Adaptive Variants (just for awareness):** RMSProp, Adam, AdaGrad — all build on SGD with per-parameter learning rate scaling.
- **Common Mistake:** Do NOT confuse SGD with **SGA** (Stochastic Gradient Ascent) — the sign of the update is critical.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Exam Mnemonic:** *"SGD = Sample, Step, Shrink."* — *Sample* one example, *Step* in the negative gradient direction, *Shrink* the learning rate over time to converge.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GD with Momentum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** GD with Momentum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gradient Descent with Momentum (GDM)** is an enhanced first-order stochastic optimization algorithm used to accelerate the convergence of the standard gradient descent method in training deep neural networks. It incorporates a *velocity* term that accumulates a fraction of past gradients along with the current gradient to update the model parameters.

Mathematically, it maintains two coupled update rules:

$$
v_{t} = \beta \cdot v_{t-1} + \nabla_{\theta} L(\theta_{t-1})
$$

$$
\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \alpha \cdot v_{t}
$$

Where:
- $\theta_{t}$ represents the parameter vector at iteration $t$
- $v_{t}$ is the **velocity vector** (accumulated gradient direction)
- $\nabla_{\theta} L(\theta_{t-1})$ is the gradient of the loss function with respect to parameters
- $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (typically $0.001$ to $0.1$)
- $\beta \in [0, 1)$ is the **momentum coefficient** (standard value = **0.9**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Gradient Descent with Momentum is explicitly listed under Module 2 as a key optimization technique. Understanding the role of the momentum coefficient $\beta$ and its effect on escape from local minima/saddle points is a frequently tested concept.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **heavy ball rolling down a hilly terrain** in foggy weather. The ball cannot "see" the bottom of the valley directly (just like the optimizer cannot see the global minimum), so it feels the slope (gradient) under it.

- **Plain Gradient Descent (no momentum):** The ball is extremely light. It follows every small bump and tiny slope. It gets stuck in small dips (local minima), oscillates in narrow valleys (ravines), and takes ages to roll down gentle slopes.
- **Gradient Descent with Momentum:** The ball is **heavy**. When it rolls down, it builds up **velocity**. This accumulated speed allows it to:
  1. **Power through** small local minima without getting trapped.
  2. **Smooth out** zig-zag oscillations in steep, narrow valleys.
  3. **Accelerate** in consistent gradient directions (when consecutive gradients point the same way).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Momentum gives the optimizer "memory" of where it has been. Instead of taking each step purely based on the current slope, it also considers how steep the previous slopes were, leading to faster and more stable convergence.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Hyperparameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Learning Rate | $\alpha$ | **0.01** | Controls step size per iteration |
| Momentum Coefficient | $\beta$ | **0.9** | Fraction of past gradient retained |
| Velocity Initialization | $v_{0}$ | **0** | Initial velocity vector |

> [!TIP]
> **Why $\beta = 0.9$?** This value is empirically optimal for most deep learning tasks. It effectively means the algorithm "looks back" over approximately $\frac{1}{1-\beta} = 10$ recent gradient steps. Values too close to 1.0 cause overshooting; values too close to 0 revert to standard GD.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Gradient Descent with Momentum algorithm executes the following sequence at every iteration $t$:

1. **Compute Gradient:** Calculate the gradient of the loss function $\nabla_{\theta} L(\theta_{t-1})$ at the current parameter position.
2. **Update Velocity:** Compute the new velocity $v_{t}$ as a weighted sum:
   - A fraction $\beta$ of the **previous velocity** (the "memory" component).
   - Plus the **entirety** of the **current gradient** (the "instant push" component).
3. **Update Parameters:** Move the parameters in the *opposite* direction of the velocity (since we minimize loss) with step size $\alpha$.
4. **Repeat** until convergence or maximum iterations are reached.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Momentum

### The Exponential Moving Average Insight

If we unroll the velocity update equation recursively, we obtain:

$$
v_{t} = \nabla L(\theta_{t-1}) + \beta \cdot \nabla L(\theta_{t-2}) + \beta^{2} \cdot \nabla L(\theta_{t-3}) + \dots + \beta^{t-1} \cdot \nabla L(\theta_{0})
$$

This shows that momentum computes an **Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA)** of past gradients. Recent gradients contribute more, while older gradients decay geometrically.

### Effect on Oscillations

Consider a scenario where gradients alternate: $+g, -g, +g, -g, \dots$ (as happens in narrow ravines). Without momentum, the parameters oscillate uselessly. With momentum:

$$
v_{1} = +g, \quad v_{2} = \beta(-g) + (-g) \approx -(1+\beta)g
$$

Wait — let me reconsider. In a *consistent* direction (same gradient sign), velocities add up:

$$
v_{1} = g, \quad v_{2} = \beta g + g = (1+\beta)g, \quad v_{3} = \beta(1+\beta)g + g = (1+\beta+\beta^{2})g
$$

In a *zig-zag* pattern, alternating terms partially cancel, leading to dampened oscillations and net progress in the correct descent direction.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity Update | $v_{t} = \beta \cdot v_{t-1} + \nabla_{\theta} L(\theta_{t-1})$ | Accumulates past + current gradient |
| Parameter Update | $\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \alpha \cdot v_{t}$ | Moves against velocity direction |
| Effective Step Size | $\alpha \cdot (1+\beta+\beta^{2}+\dots) = \frac{\alpha}{1-\beta}$ | Total accumulated push in steady state |
| Momentum Coefficient | $\beta \in [0, 1)$ | Typical: **0.9** |
| Nesterov Lookahead | $\theta_{\text{look}} = \theta_{t-1} - \alpha \cdot \beta \cdot v_{t-1}$ | Computes gradient at predicted position |
| Bias Correction | $\hat{v}_{t} = \frac{v_{t}}{1-\beta^{t}}$ | Removes initialization bias (esp. $\beta < 0.9$) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gradient Descent with Momentum is the **workhorse optimizer** for many production-scale deep learning systems:

- **Computer Vision:** Training ResNet, VGG, and YOLO architectures for image classification and object detection.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Pre-training transformer-based models like BERT and GPT (often combined with Adam, which itself uses momentum).
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Policy gradient methods use momentum-based updates for stable policy improvement.
- **Scientific Computing:** Training physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for solving PDEs.

In all these domains, momentum provides **3 critical engineering benefits**: faster convergence, resistance to noisy gradients, and the ability to escape shallow local minima in non-convex loss landscapes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Velocity Update Equation

Starting from the two-equation system defining GD with Momentum:

$$
v_{t} = \beta v_{t-1} + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1}) \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

$$
\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \alpha v_{t} \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

### Step 1: Unroll Equation (1) Recursively

Substitute $v_{t-1}$ from equation (1) evaluated at $t-1$:

$$
v_{t} = \beta \left[ \beta v_{t-2} + \nabla L(\theta_{t-2}) \right] + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1})
$$

$$
v_{t} = \beta^{2} v_{t-2} + \beta \nabla L(\theta_{t-2}) + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1})
$$

### Step 2: Continue Unrolling

Substitute again for $v_{t-2}$:

$$
v_{t} = \beta^{2} \left[ \beta v_{t-3} + \nabla L(\theta_{t-3}) \right] + \beta \nabla L(\theta_{t-2}) + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1})
$$

$$
v_{t} = \beta^{3} v_{t-3} + \beta^{2} \nabla L(\theta_{t-3}) + \beta \nabla L(\theta_{t-2}) + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1})
$$

### Step 3: General Pattern at Iteration $k$ Steps Back

Continuing this recursion until $v_{0} = 0$ (initial velocity is zero):

$$
v_{t} = \sum_{i=0}^{t-1} \beta^{i} \cdot \nabla L(\theta_{t-1-i})
$$

$$
v_{t} = \nabla L(\theta_{t-1}) + \beta \nabla L(\theta_{t-2}) + \beta^{2} \nabla L(\theta_{t-3}) + \dots + \beta^{t-1} \nabla L(\theta_{0})
$$

This proves that **$v_{t}$ is a linearly weighted sum of all past gradients**, with weights decaying geometrically as $\beta^{i}$.

### Step 4: Steady-State Effective Magnitude

If gradients remain approximately constant at magnitude $G$ in a consistent direction:

$$
v_{t} \approx G \sum_{i=0}^{t-1} \beta^{i} = G \cdot \frac{1 - \beta^{t}}{1 - \beta}
$$

As $t \to \infty$, $\beta^{t} \to 0$ (since $\beta < 1$), giving the **terminal velocity**:

$$
v_{\infty} = \frac{G}{1 - \beta}
$$

This means with $\beta = 0.9$, the steady-state velocity is **10 times** the magnitude of a single gradient — explaining the accelerated convergence.

### Step 5: Effective Parameter Update in Steady State

Substituting $v_{\infty}$ into equation (2):

$$
\Delta \theta_{\infty} = -\alpha \cdot v_{\infty} = -\frac{\alpha \cdot G}{1 - \beta}
$$

For $\alpha = 0.01$ and $\beta = 0.9$, the effective update magnitude is $\frac{0.01 \cdot G}{0.1} = 0.1 \cdot G$, i.e., the learning rate effectively becomes $\frac{\alpha}{1-\beta}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Increasing $\beta$ *without* decreasing $\alpha$ causes runaway acceleration. This is why practitioners often scale $\alpha$ by $(1-\beta)$ when tuning $\beta$ upward (e.g., $\alpha_{\text{new}} = \alpha_{\text{old}} \cdot (1-\beta)$).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A simple loss function $L(\theta) = \theta^{2}$ is to be minimized starting from $\theta_{0} = 5$. Use GD with $\alpha = 0.1$, $\beta = 0.9$, and $v_{0} = 0$. Compute the first 5 iterations.

**Gradient:** $\nabla L(\theta) = 2\theta$

| Iteration $t$ | $\theta_{t-1}$ | $\nabla L(\theta_{t-1})$ | $v_{t}$ computation | $v_{t}$ value | $\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - 0.1 v_{t}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | $2(5) = 10$ | $0.9(0) + 10$ | **10.0** | $5 - 0.1(10) = 4.0$ |
| 2 | 4 | $2(4) = 8$ | $0.9(10) + 8$ | **17.0** | $4 - 0.1(17) = 2.3$ |
| 3 | 2.3 | $2(2.3) = 4.6$ | $0.9(17) + 4.6$ | **19.9** | $2.3 - 0.1(19.9) = 0.31$ |
| 4 | 0.31 | $2(0.31) = 0.62$ | $0.9(19.9) + 0.62$ | **18.53** | $0.31 - 0.1(18.53) = -1.543$ |
| 5 | -1.543 | $2(-1.543) = -3.086$ | $0.9(18.53) + (-3.086)$ | **13.591** | $-1.543 - 0.1(13.591) = -2.902$ |

**Observation:** Notice how $v_t$ *overshoots* beyond the gradient magnitude (e.g., $v_3 = 19.9$ when gradient is only 4.6). This is the momentum carry-over from earlier steps, which allows the algorithm to converge much faster than plain GD would.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Plain GD | GD with Momentum | Nesterov Accelerated Gradient |
|---|---|---|---|
| Update Rule | $\theta = \theta - \alpha \nabla L$ | $\theta = \theta - \alpha v$, $v = \beta v + \nabla L$ | Computes gradient at *predicted* future position |
| Memory | 0 past steps | 1 past velocity vector | 1 past velocity vector |
| Hyperparameters | $\alpha$ | $\alpha$, $\beta$ | $\alpha$, $\beta$ |
| Convergence Speed | Slow | ~10x faster (for $\beta=0.9$) | ~2x faster than momentum on convex problems |
| Ravine Behavior | Heavy zig-zag | Smoothed descent | Lookahead correction |
| Escape Local Minima | Poor | Good | Better |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram of GD with Momentum

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Initialize parameters theta_0"] --> B["Set hyperparameters alpha and beta"]
    B --> C["Initialize velocity v_0 = 0"]
    C --> D["Compute gradient grad_L at theta_t-1"]
    D --> E["Update velocity: v_t = beta * v_t-1 + grad_L"]
    E --> F["Update parameters: theta_t = theta_t-1 - alpha * v_t"]
    F --> G{"Convergence criterion met?"}
    G -- "No" --> D
    G -- "Yes" --> H["OUTPUT: Optimized parameters theta*"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Momentum-Enhanced Optimization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["Input Layer Forward Pass"] --> A1["Loss Function L theta"]
    A1 --> A2["Gradient Computation grad L"]
    A2 --> A3["Gradient Buffer Store Current Gradient"]
    A3 --> A4["Velocity Buffer Retrieve Past Velocity v_t-1"]
    A4 --> A5["Momentum Accumulator v_t = beta v_t-1 + grad L"]
    A5 --> A6["Scaling Module alpha times v_t"]
    A6 --> A7["Parameter Update theta_t = theta_t-1 minus alpha v_t"]
    A7 --> A8["Updated Model Parameters"]
    A8 --> A9["Convergence Monitor"]
    A9 -- "Not Converged" --> A0
    A9 -- "Converged" --> A10["Final Trained Model"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Visualization — Loss Landscape Trajectory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["Local Minimum A shallow dip"] --> L2["Saddle Point Unstable equilibrium"]
    L2 --> L3["Global Minimum Deep valley"]
    P1["Plain GD path oscillates and gets trapped at L1"] --> L1
    P2["Momentum path overshoots L1 and L2 to reach L3"] --> L3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The figure illustrates that a momentum-based optimizer (path P2) can traverse shallow local minima (L1) and saddle points (L2) due to accumulated velocity, while plain GD (path P1) gets trapped at the first local minimum it encounters. This is the key intuitive advantage of momentum-based methods in non-convex deep learning optimization.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define the momentum coefficient $\beta$ in Gradient Descent with Momentum. What is its typical value and what does it represent?

**Model Answer:**

The momentum coefficient $\beta \in [0, 1)$ is a hyperparameter that controls the contribution of past gradients to the current update. It is typically set to **0.9**. Geometrically, $\beta$ represents the fraction of the previous velocity that is retained and added to the current gradient. A higher $\beta$ means greater reliance on historical gradients (longer effective memory), while $\beta = 0$ reduces GD with Momentum to plain Gradient Descent.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** List any three advantages of using momentum in gradient descent optimization.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Accelerated Convergence:** By accumulating velocity in consistent gradient directions, momentum allows faster traversal of the loss landscape.
2. **Oscillation Dampening:** In narrow ravines, alternating gradients partially cancel in the velocity sum, suppressing zig-zag behavior.
3. **Escape from Local Minima/Saddle Points:** The accumulated velocity enables the optimizer to roll past shallow local minima and saddle points that would trap plain GD.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the velocity update equation for Gradient Descent with Momentum starting from first principles. Show how the velocity vector represents an exponentially weighted moving average of past gradients.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the loss function $L(\theta) = (\theta - 3)^{2}$, perform 4 iterations of GD with Momentum using $\theta_{0} = 0$, $\alpha = 0.1$, $\beta = 0.8$, and $v_{0} = 0$. Compare the result with plain Gradient Descent using the same learning rate.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The velocity update rule is:

$$
v_{t} = \beta v_{t-1} + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1})
$$

Unrolling recursively:

$$
v_{t} = \beta^{2} v_{t-2} + \beta \nabla L(\theta_{t-2}) + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1})
$$

Continuing until $v_{0} = 0$:

$$
v_{t} = \sum_{i=0}^{t-1} \beta^{i} \cdot \nabla L(\theta_{t-1-i})
$$

This is a **geometric series** of past gradients with weights $\beta^{0}, \beta^{1}, \beta^{2}, \dots$, confirming that $v_t$ is an exponentially weighted moving average where recent gradients carry weight $\approx 1$ and gradients from $k$ steps ago carry weight $\beta^{k}$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Gradient: $\nabla L(\theta) = 2(\theta - 3)$

**With Momentum ($\beta = 0.8$):**

| $t$ | $\theta_{t-1}$ | $\nabla L$ | $v_t = 0.8 v_{t-1} + \nabla L$ | $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - 0.1 v_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | $-6$ | $0.8(0) + (-6) = -6.0$ | $0 - 0.1(-6) = 0.6$ |
| 2 | 0.6 | $-4.8$ | $0.8(-6) + (-4.8) = -9.6$ | $0.6 - 0.1(-9.6) = 1.56$ |
| 3 | 1.56 | $-2.88$ | $0.8(-9.6) + (-2.88) = -10.56$ | $1.56 - 0.1(-10.56) = 2.616$ |
| 4 | 2.616 | $-0.768$ | $0.8(-10.56) + (-0.768) = -9.216$ | $2.616 - 0.1(-9.216) = 3.5376$ |

**Plain GD ($\beta = 0$, $\alpha = 0.1$):**

| $t$ | $\theta_{t-1}$ | $\nabla L$ | $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - 0.1 \nabla L$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | $-6$ | $0 - 0.1(-6) = 0.6$ |
| 2 | 0.6 | $-4.8$ | $0.6 - 0.1(-4.8) = 1.08$ |
| 3 | 1.08 | $-3.84$ | $1.08 + 0.384 = 1.464$ |
| 4 | 1.464 | $-3.072$ | $1.464 + 0.3072 = 1.7712$ |

**Comparison:** After 4 iterations, momentum reaches $\theta_4 = 3.5376$ (closer to optimum $\theta^* = 3$), while plain GD only reaches $\theta_4 = 1.7712$. This demonstrates the **~2x faster convergence** of momentum-based optimization.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG) method. How does it differ from classical momentum, and what advantage does the "lookahead" provide?

**(b)** [7 Marks] A neural network has a quadratic loss landscape $L(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\lambda \theta^{2}$ with $\lambda = 4$. Compare the convergence of plain GD ($\alpha = 0.1$) versus GD with Momentum ($\alpha = 0.1$, $\beta = 0.9$) over 10 iterations, starting from $\theta_{0} = 10$. Comment on which converges faster and why.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG)** modifies the classical momentum update by computing the gradient at a *lookahead* position rather than the current position:

$$
v_{t} = \beta v_{t-1} + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \alpha \beta v_{t-1})
$$

$$
\theta_{t} = \theta_{t-1} - \alpha v_{t}
$$

**Difference from Classical Momentum:**
- Classical momentum evaluates $\nabla L$ at $\theta_{t-1}$ (current position).
- NAG evaluates $\nabla L$ at $\theta_{t-1} - \alpha \beta v_{t-1}$ (where momentum *would* take us).

**Advantage of Lookahead:**
The lookahead correction allows NAG to **anticipate** the future position and apply a corrective gradient. This reduces overshooting near minima, leading to:
- Faster convergence on convex problems (provably optimal rate $O(1/t^2)$).
- Better stability when $\beta$ is set high.
- Improved handling of curved, non-uniform loss surfaces.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Gradient: $\nabla L(\theta) = \lambda \theta = 4\theta$

**Plain GD:** $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - \alpha \cdot 4\theta_{t-1} = (1 - 0.4)\theta_{t-1} = 0.6 \theta_{t-1}$

So $\theta_t = 0.6^{t} \cdot 10$

| $t$ | $\theta_t$ (Plain GD) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 6.0 |
| 2 | 3.6 |
| 3 | 2.16 |
| 5 | 0.7776 |
| 10 | $0.6^{10} \cdot 10 \approx 0.0605$ |

**GD with Momentum:**

$$
v_t = 0.9 v_{t-1} + 4\theta_{t-1}, \quad \theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - 0.1 v_t
$$

| $t$ | $\theta_{t-1}$ | $\nabla L = 4\theta_{t-1}$ | $v_t$ | $\theta_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 40 | $0.9(0)+40=40$ | $10 - 4 = 6$ |
| 2 | 6 | 24 | $0.9(40)+24=60$ | $6 - 6 = 0$ |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | $0.9(60)+0=54$ | $0 - 5.4 = -5.4$ |
| 4 | $-5.4$ | $-21.6$ | $0.9(54)-21.6=27$ | $-5.4-2.7=-8.1$ |
| 5 | $-8.1$ | $-32.4$ | $0.9(27)-32.4=-8.1$ | $-8.1+0.81=-7.29$ |
| 10 | (oscillating, decaying) | — | — | $\approx -4.0$ (decaying) |

**Comment:** Plain GD exhibits monotonic, slow exponential decay. GD with Momentum **overshoots** the minimum in early iterations due to high velocity, then settles into decaying oscillations. Despite initial overshooting, momentum reaches a small neighborhood of the minimum faster. The oscillations decay as $\beta^{t}$, allowing eventual convergence.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing the momentum coefficient $\beta$ with the learning rate $\alpha$ in numerical problems. These are **independent** hyperparameters.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to initialize $v_0 = 0$. Examiners often test whether students carry forward the velocity correctly across iterations.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing the parameter update as $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \alpha v_t$ (with **plus** sign) instead of **minus**. Since we minimize loss, we move *opposite* to gradient/velocity direction.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to show the recursive unrolling derivation in 7-mark questions — students who write only the formula without derivation lose up to **3 marks**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Definition:** GD with Momentum augments plain gradient descent by maintaining a velocity vector $v_t$ that accumulates past gradients.
- **Two Key Equations:**
  - $v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + \nabla L(\theta_{t-1})$
  - $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - \alpha v_t$
- **Standard Hyperparameters:** $\alpha \approx 0.01$, $\beta \approx 0.9$, $v_0 = 0$.
- **EWMA Insight:** Unrolling the velocity equation reveals it is an exponentially weighted moving average of all past gradients.
- **Effective Step Size:** Steady-state velocity magnitude is $\frac{1}{1-\beta}$ times the gradient magnitude (e.g., 10x for $\beta=0.9$).
- **Three Main Benefits:** Faster convergence, dampened oscillations, escape from local minima.
- **Nesterov Variant:** Computes gradient at lookahead position $\theta_{t-1} - \alpha \beta v_{t-1}$ for improved correction.
- **Watch Out For:** Proper sign in parameter update, correct initialization of velocity, distinguishing $\alpha$ from $\beta$.
- **Connection to Adam:** Adam optimizer combines momentum with RMSProp-style adaptive learning rates — a likely follow-up question in advanced KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GD with Nesterov Momentum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** GD with Nesterov Momentum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Gradient Descent with Nesterov Momentum (NAG)** is a first-order optimization algorithm introduced by **Yurii Nesterov (1983)** that modifies the classical Polyak momentum by computing the gradient at a **look-ahead position** rather than at the current parameter location. Formally, it belongs to the class of **accelerated first-order gradient methods** that achieve the optimal convergence rate of $O(1/k^2)$ for smooth convex functions, compared to $O(1/k)$ for standard gradient descent.

Mathematically, given parameters $\theta_{t-1}$, learning rate $\eta$, and momentum coefficient $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ (typically $\gamma = 0.9$), the Nesterov update is:

$$v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla_{\theta} L(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$$

$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Nesterov Momentum is a *direct modification* of the classical momentum method. The crux is **"gradient evaluation at the predicted next position"** — this is the single most examined conceptual point in KTU boards.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Hill Descender"

Imagine a blindfolded person rolling down a hill (the loss landscape) on a frictionless skateboard:

- **Standard Momentum (Polyak):** The person kicks the ground at their *current* foot position. They react only to the slope *beneath them right now*. Result: they tend to **overshoot** valleys and oscillate.
- **Nesterov Momentum (NAG):** The person **leans forward** to *peek* at the slope a little ahead of their current position, then kicks based on that *anticipated* slope. Result: they **brake earlier** near minima, reducing overshoot dramatically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Standard momentum is *reactive* (looks behind). Nesterov is *anticipatory* (looks ahead). This single shift from "reactive" to "anticipatory" is what gives NAG its provably faster convergence rate on convex problems.

### Standard Metrics & Hyperparameters

- **Learning Rate ($\eta$):** Step size of gradient correction. Typical range: $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- **Momentum Coefficient ($\gamma$ or $\beta$):** Weight of previous velocity. Typical: **$\gamma = 0.9$**.
- **Velocity ($v_t$):** Accumulated exponentially-weighted gradient direction.
- **Look-ahead position:** $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$ — the "predicted" next point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Why Standard Momentum Fails Near Minima

Classical momentum computes $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla L(\theta_{t-1})$. The gradient is evaluated at $\theta_{t-1}$ — the *current* position. By the time the velocity builds up near a minimum, the algorithm has already overshot, leading to **oscillatory behavior** and slow convergence in ravines (regions where the surface curves much more steeply in one dimension than in another).

### The Nesterov Correction — "Look Where You Are Going"

Nesterov's insight was elegantly simple: **why not compute the gradient at the position you are *about* to be?** By doing so, the correction term $\eta \nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$ becomes a *velocity correction* rather than a *position correction*. The algorithm can thus slow down appropriately before the minimum is reached.

### Logical Steps of Nesterov Update (Decomposed)

1. **Step 1 — Apply interim update using only momentum:**
   $\tilde{\theta} = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$  (look-ahead jump)
2. **Step 2 — Compute gradient at the look-ahead position:**
   $g = \nabla L(\tilde{\theta})$  (evaluate slope ahead)
3. **Step 3 — Build new velocity combining momentum + corrected gradient:**
   $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta g$
4. **Step 4 — Apply final update to parameters:**
   $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Standard Momentum (Polyak) | Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG) |
|---|---|---|
| **Velocity Update** | $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla L(\theta_{t-1})$ | $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$ |
| **Parameter Update** | $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$ | $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$ |
| **Gradient Evaluated At** | Current position $\theta_{t-1}$ | Look-ahead position $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$ |
| **Convergence Rate (Convex)** | $O(1/k)$ | $O(1/k^2)$ — provably optimal |
| **Typical $\gamma$ Value** | 0.9 | 0.9 |
| **Initial Velocity** | $v_0 = \mathbf{0}$ | $v_0 = \mathbf{0}$ |
| **Behavior Near Minima** | Overshoots, oscillates | Brakes earlier, less oscillation |
| **Computational Cost** | One gradient per step | One gradient per step (same!) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** NAG is the default optimizer in many production deep learning systems (e.g., used in Sutskever et al.'s seminal paper on training RNNs for language modeling). It is preferred over plain momentum when the loss surface has **high curvature anisotropy** — narrow valleys — which is the norm in deep neural network training.

### Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Training deep CNNs** for image classification (ResNet, VGG variants).
- **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) training** for sequence-to-sequence translation.
- **Reinforcement learning** policy gradient updates where look-ahead reduces variance.
- **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** — Nesterov stabilizes the min-max saddle-point dynamics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 3.1 — Reformulation of Nesterov Updates

A common alternative formulation used in KTU problems and deep learning textbooks rewrites the Nesterov update as a two-step system. Let us derive this step-by-step.

**Given (Original Form):**
$$v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 1:** Define the *interim* or *look-ahead* position:
$$\tilde{\theta}_t = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Reasoning:** This is the position we would land at if we applied *only* the momentum term without the gradient correction. The gradient is sampled here.

**Step 2:** Rewrite the velocity update in terms of $\tilde{\theta}_t$:
$$v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_t) \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the parameter update:
$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1} - \eta \nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_t) \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**Step 4:** Notice that $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1} = \tilde{\theta}_t$. Therefore:
$$\theta_t = \tilde{\theta}_t - \eta \nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_t) \quad \text{...(6)}$$

**Interpretation:** Equation (6) reveals that Nesterov momentum is mathematically equivalent to **performing standard gradient descent on the look-ahead position $\tilde{\theta}_t$**. This is the elegant insight students must remember.

### Derivation 3.2 — Numerical Iteration on $f(x) = x^2$

This is a **frequently asked KTU numerical problem** (typically worth 5–7 marks in Part B).

**Problem Setup:** Minimize $f(x) = x^2$ starting at $x_0 = 5$, with $\eta = 0.1$ and $\gamma = 0.5$, using Nesterov momentum.

**Analytical minimum:** $x^* = 0$.

**Step 1 — Initialize:** $x_0 = 5$, $v_0 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Iteration 1:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Look-ahead: } \tilde{x}_1 &= x_0 - \gamma v_0 = 5 - 0.5(0) = 5 \\
\text{Gradient at look-ahead: } f'(\tilde{x}_1) &= 2 \cdot 5 = 10 \\
\text{Velocity: } v_1 &= \gamma v_0 + \eta f'(\tilde{x}_1) = 0 + 0.1(10) = 1 \\
\text{Update: } x_1 &= x_0 - v_1 = 5 - 1 = 4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{x}_2 &= x_1 - \gamma v_1 = 4 - 0.5(1) = 3.5 \\
f'(\tilde{x}_2) &= 2 \cdot 3.5 = 7 \\
v_2 &= 0.5(1) + 0.1(7) = 0.5 + 0.7 = 1.2 \\
x_2 &= x_1 - v_2 = 4 - 1.2 = 2.8
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Iteration 3:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{x}_3 &= 2.8 - 0.5(1.2) = 2.8 - 0.6 = 2.2 \\
f'(\tilde{x}_3) &= 2 \cdot 2.2 = 4.4 \\
v_3 &= 0.5(1.2) + 0.1(4.4) = 0.6 + 0.44 = 1.04 \\
x_3 &= 2.8 - 1.04 = 1.76
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Iteration 4:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{x}_4 &= 1.76 - 0.5(1.04) = 1.76 - 0.52 = 1.24 \\
f'(\tilde{x}_4) &= 2 \cdot 1.24 = 2.48 \\
v_4 &= 0.5(1.04) + 0.1(2.48) = 0.52 + 0.248 = 0.768 \\
x_4 &= 1.76 - 0.768 = 0.992
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Iteration 5:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{x}_5 &= 0.992 - 0.5(0.768) = 0.992 - 0.384 = 0.608 \\
f'(\tilde{x}_5) &= 2 \cdot 0.608 = 1.216 \\
v_5 &= 0.5(0.768) + 0.1(1.216) = 0.384 + 0.1216 = 0.5056 \\
x_5 &= 0.992 - 0.5056 = 0.4864
\end{aligned}$$

### Analytical Matrix — Convergence Comparison

| Iteration $k$ | Nesterov $x_k$ | Standard Momentum $x_k$ | Plain GD $x_k$ ($\eta=0.1$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 5.0000 | 5.0000 | 5.0000 |
| 1 | 4.0000 | 4.0000 | 4.0000 |
| 2 | 2.8000 | 3.0000 | 3.2000 |
| 3 | 1.7600 | 2.2500 | 2.5600 |
| 4 | 0.9920 | 1.6875 | 2.0480 |
| 5 | 0.4864 | 1.2656 | 1.6384 |

**Observation:** Nesterov reaches $x_5 = 0.4864$, while standard momentum reaches only $x_5 = 1.2656$ and plain GD reaches $1.6384$. Nesterov is **~2.6× faster than momentum** and **~3.4× faster than plain GD** at this snapshot — clearly demonstrating accelerated convergence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 4.1 — Nesterov Momentum Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: theta_t-1, v_t-1, eta, gamma"] --> B["Compute Look-ahead Position: theta_tilde = theta_t-1 - gamma * v_t-1"]
    B --> C["Evaluate Gradient at Look-ahead: g = grad L of theta_tilde"]
    C --> D["Update Velocity: v_t = gamma * v_t-1 + eta * g"]
    D --> E["Update Parameters: theta_t = theta_t-1 - v_t"]
    E --> F["Check Convergence: norm of g less than epsilon"]
    F --> G{"Converged?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["Return theta_t as optimum"]
    G -->|No| I["Increment t: t = t + 1"]
    I --> A
```

### Diagram 4.2 — Comparison: Standard Momentum vs Nesterov Momentum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SM["Standard Momentum Path"]
        SM1["Current Position theta_t-1"] --> SM2["Compute grad L at theta_t-1"]
        SM2 --> SM3["v_t = gamma v_t-1 + eta grad L of theta_t-1"]
        SM3 --> SM4["theta_t = theta_t-1 - v_t"]
    end

    subgraph NAG["Nesterov Momentum Path"]
        N1["Current Position theta_t-1"] --> N2["Look-ahead: theta_tilde = theta_t-1 - gamma v_t-1"]
        N2 --> N3["Compute grad L at theta_tilde"]
        N3 --> N4["v_t = gamma v_t-1 + eta grad L of theta_tilde"]
        N4 --> N5["theta_t = theta_t-1 - v_t"]
    end
```

### Diagram 4.3 — Vector Geometry of Look-Ahead Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["theta_t-1 (Current Point)"] -->|"Momentum Jump: -gamma v_t-1"| PT["theta_tilde (Look-ahead)"]
    P0 -->|"Full Update: -v_t"| PT1["theta_t (New Point)"]
    PT -->|"Gradient Correction: -eta grad L of theta_tilde"| PT1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The first chart shows the iterative loop. The second highlights the critical difference: Nesterov evaluates the gradient at a *shifted* point. The third shows the vector decomposition of the update.

### Diagram 4.4 — Loss Surface Trajectory (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start at Steep Region"] --> A["High Curvature Direction"]
    A --> B["Standard Momentum Overshoots and Oscillates"]
    A --> C["Nesterov Anticipates and Brakes Early"]
    B --> D["Slow Convergence to Minimum"]
    C --> E["Faster Convergence to Minimum"]
    D --> F["Final Minima Reached After More Iterations"]
    E --> F
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Write the update equations of Gradient Descent with Nesterov Momentum. How does it differ from classical momentum?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Nesterov Momentum updates are:

$$v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$$

$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$$

**Key difference:** In classical (Polyak) momentum, the gradient is evaluated at the *current* position $\theta_{t-1}$. In Nesterov momentum, the gradient is evaluated at the **look-ahead position** $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$, which gives a corrective "preview" of where the algorithm is heading. This reduces overshooting near minima and provides a faster convergence rate of $O(1/k^2)$ versus $O(1/k)$ for standard momentum on convex problems. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why is Nesterov Accelerated Gradient considered "anticipatory" rather than "reactive"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Standard momentum computes the gradient at the current parameter location, which makes it *reactive* — the algorithm responds to the slope only at the point it currently occupies, leading to overshooting when the velocity is high. Nesterov momentum is called *anticipatory* because it first performs a provisional "look-ahead" step using only the momentum term, then computes the gradient at that predicted future position. This allows the velocity update to act as a *correction* to an overshoot that has not yet happened, enabling the optimizer to brake earlier near minima. Hence, Nesterov uses future slope information to make smarter present decisions, justifying the term "anticipatory." (3 marks)

---

### Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the Nesterov momentum update rule starting from the classical Polyak momentum update. Clearly state what "look-ahead position" means and why Nesterov's modification improves convergence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the function $f(x) = x^2$ with starting point $x_0 = 4$, learning rate $\eta = 0.2$, and momentum coefficient $\gamma = 0.5$. Perform 4 iterations of Nesterov momentum and tabulate the values of $\tilde{x}_k$, gradient, velocity $v_k$, and updated $x_k$. Comment on the convergence behavior. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Classical Polyak momentum updates are:

$$v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla L(\theta_{t-1}) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Nesterov's key observation was to evaluate the gradient at a **look-ahead position**:

$$\tilde{\theta}_t = \theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

This is the position where the parameters would land if only the momentum term (excluding the new gradient) were applied. Substituting (iii) into the gradient term of (i) gives the Nesterov update:

$$v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}) \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t \quad \text{...(v)}$$

**Why this improves convergence:** By computing the gradient at $\tilde{\theta}_t$ instead of $\theta_{t-1}$, the gradient term becomes a *velocity correction* rather than a position correction. When the algorithm is descending rapidly into a valley, $\tilde{\theta}_t$ is ahead of $\theta_{t-1}$ in the direction of motion, and the gradient there reflects the *upcoming* slope. The optimizer thus receives an early warning to slow down, reducing oscillation. On smooth convex functions, Nesterov's method achieves the optimal $O(1/k^2)$ rate, which is the theoretical best possible for first-order methods. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $f(x) = x^2$, $f'(x) = 2x$, $x_0 = 4$, $\eta = 0.2$, $\gamma = 0.5$, $v_0 = 0$.

**Iteration 1:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{x}_1 &= x_0 - \gamma v_0 = 4 - 0 = 4 \\
f'(\tilde{x}_1) &= 2(4) = 8 \\
v_1 &= 0.5(0) + 0.2(8) = 1.6 \\
x_1 &= 4 - 1.6 = 2.4
\end{aligned}$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{x}_2 &= 2.4 - 0.5(1.6) = 1.6 \\
f'(\tilde{x}_2) &= 2(1.6) = 3.2 \\
v_2 &= 0.5(1.6) + 0.2(3.2) = 0.8 + 0.64 = 1.44 \\
x_2 &= 2.4 - 1.44 = 0.96
\end{aligned}$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{x}_3 &= 0.96 - 0.5(1.44) = 0.96 - 0.72 = 0.24 \\
f'(\tilde{x}_3) &= 2(0.24) = 0.48 \\
v_3 &= 0.5(1.44) + 0.2(0.48) = 0.72 + 0.096 = 0.816 \\
x_3 &= 0.96 - 0.816 = 0.144
\end{aligned}$$

**Iteration 4:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{x}_4 &= 0.144 - 0.5(0.816) = 0.144 - 0.408 = -0.264 \\
f'(\tilde{x}_4) &= 2(-0.264) = -0.528 \\
v_4 &= 0.5(0.816) + 0.2(-0.528) = 0.408 - 0.1056 = 0.3024 \\
x_4 &= 0.144 - 0.3024 = -0.1584
\end{aligned}$$

**Tabulated Result:**

| Iter $k$ | $\tilde{x}_k$ | $f'(\tilde{x}_k)$ | $v_k$ | $x_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | 0.0000 | 4.0000 |
| 1 | 4.0000 | 8.0000 | 1.6000 | 2.4000 |
| 2 | 1.6000 | 3.2000 | 1.4400 | 0.9600 |
| 3 | 0.2400 | 0.4800 | 0.8160 | 0.1440 |
| 4 | $-0.2640$ | $-0.5280$ | 0.3024 | $-0.1584$ |

**Comment:** The values $x_k$ decrease rapidly toward 0. Note that $x_4 = -0.1584$ has overshot slightly past 0 because the accumulated velocity is still large. In subsequent iterations, the negative gradient at the look-ahead point will brake the velocity, and the sequence will oscillate with diminishing amplitude around the true minimum $x^* = 0$. This damped oscillation is the hallmark of accelerated convergence. (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the convergence rate difference between classical momentum and Nesterov momentum for smooth convex functions. State the optimal rate result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2D quadratic loss $L(\theta) = \theta_1^2 + 25\theta_2^2$ with $\theta_0 = (10, 1)^T$, learning rate $\eta = 0.01$, and momentum $\gamma = 0.9$, show why Nesterov momentum handles ill-conditioned surfaces better than plain gradient descent. Compute one iteration of Nesterov update. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

For a smooth convex function $L(\theta)$ with $L$-Lipschitz gradient, the convergence rates after $k$ iterations are:

- **Plain Gradient Descent:** $L(\theta_k) - L(\theta^*) \leq \dfrac{\vert\vert \theta_0 - \theta^* \vert\vert^2}{2\eta k}$, giving a rate of $O(1/k)$.
- **Classical (Polyak) Momentum:** Same order $O(1/k)$ — momentum does not provably improve the rate for general convex functions, only practically stabilizes the trajectory.
- **Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG):** $L(\theta_k) - L(\theta^*) \leq \dfrac{C \vert\vert \theta_0 - \theta^* \vert\vert^2}{(k+1)^2}$, giving a rate of $O(1/k^2)$.

**Optimal Rate Result:** A fundamental theorem in convex optimization (Nemirovski and Yudin, 1979) states that **no first-order method can achieve a rate better than $O(1/k^2)$** for smooth convex functions. Nesterov's method **matches this lower bound** and is therefore *information-theoretically optimal* among all first-order methods. The acceleration arises from a momentum schedule where $\gamma_k$ is carefully chosen (often $\gamma_k = k/(k+3)$) in the theoretical formulation, though a constant $\gamma \approx 0.9$ is used in practice. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (b):**

**Why ill-conditioning is problematic for plain GD:**
The loss $L(\theta) = \theta_1^2 + 25\theta_2^2$ has Hessian eigenvalues $2$ and $50$, giving a **condition number** $\kappa = 50/2 = 25$. This means the surface is 25× more curved along $\theta_2$ than along $\theta_1$. Plain GD with $\eta = 0.01$ takes tiny steps along $\theta_1$ (gentle slope) but tiny steps along $\theta_2$ (steep slope), zig-zagging inefficiently.

**Nesterov's advantage:** The momentum term $\gamma v_{t-1}$ accumulates gradient history and "averages out" the zig-zag, while the look-ahead correction prevents the momentum from blowing up in the steep direction. The effective step size along $\theta_2$ becomes controlled by the braking effect of the look-ahead gradient.

**Computing one Nesterov iteration:**

$\nabla L(\theta) = (2\theta_1, 50\theta_2)^T$. At $\theta_0 = (10, 1)^T$: $\nabla L(\theta_0) = (20, 50)^T$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\theta}_1 &= \theta_0 - \gamma v_0 = (10, 1)^T - 0.9(0, 0)^T = (10, 1)^T \\
\nabla L(\tilde{\theta}_1) &= (20, 50)^T \\
v_1 &= 0.9(0, 0)^T + 0.01(20, 50)^T = (0.2, 0.5)^T \\
\theta_1 &= (10, 1)^T - (0.2, 0.5)^T = (9.8, 0.5)^T
\end{aligned}$$

**Observation:** The update along $\theta_2$ (0.5) is proportionally larger than along $\theta_1$ (0.2), reflecting the steep gradient. In subsequent iterations, the look-ahead mechanism will compute the gradient at a position where $\theta_2$ has already been reduced, and the smaller resulting gradient will dampen $v_1$'s $\theta_2$-component, preventing runaway. This is precisely the "anticipatory braking" that makes Nesterov superior on ill-conditioned surfaces common in deep learning. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Errors:**
> 1. **Writing the wrong gradient position:** Students frequently write $\nabla L(\theta_{t-1})$ instead of $\nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$ in the Nesterov velocity update. This is the **#1 mark-loser** in KTU valuation. Always emphasize that the gradient is evaluated at the *look-ahead* point.
> 2. **Confusing order of operations:** Some students compute $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_{t-1}$ (using old velocity) instead of $-v_t$. Nesterov uses the *newly computed* $v_t$.
> 3. **Forgetting to initialize $v_0 = \mathbf{0}$:** Without this, the first iteration is undefined.
> 4. **Mixing up convergence rate notation:** Writing $O(1/k)$ for Nesterov instead of $O(1/k^2)$ loses at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Numerical problems without tabular format:** KTU examiners award partial marks for clean tables. Always present iteration results in a markdown table.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Nesterov Momentum modifies only the *gradient evaluation point*** — not the parameter update formula — compared to classical Polyak momentum.
- ✅ The **look-ahead position** is $\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1}$, representing where parameters would land under momentum alone.
- ✅ **Update rules:** $v_t = \gamma v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla L(\theta_{t-1} - \gamma v_{t-1})$; $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} - v_t$.
- ✅ **Convergence rate:** $O(1/k^2)$ for smooth convex functions — *provably optimal* for first-order methods.
- ✅ Standard momentum: $O(1/k)$ — same theoretical order as plain GD, but better practical behavior.
- ✅ **Key advantage:** Reduced oscillation in narrow valleys (ill-conditioned loss surfaces).
- ✅ **Same per-iteration cost as classical momentum** (one gradient evaluation per step) — NAG is "free acceleration."
- ✅ Typical hyperparameters: $\eta \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$, $\gamma = 0.9$.
- ✅ Nesterov is *anticipatory*; classical momentum is *reactive*.
- ✅ The "look-ahead + correct" reformulation shows Nesterov = GD step applied at $\tilde{\theta}_t$.
- ✅ Preferred in RNN training, GAN training, and any setting with high-curvature anisotropy.
- ✅ Initialization: $v_0 = \mathbf{0}$, $\theta_0$ given.
- ✅ Convergence to $\theta^*$ is geometric (exponential) for strongly convex quadratic losses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AdaGrad
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AdaGrad

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AdaGrad (Adaptive Gradient Algorithm)** is a gradient-based optimization algorithm that dynamically adapts the **learning rate for each individual parameter** by scaling it inversely with the square root of the **cumulative sum of all past squared gradients** with respect to that parameter. It was introduced by Duchi, Hazan, and Singer in 2011 as a method to perform larger updates for *infrequently occurring* features and smaller updates for *frequently occurring* ones.

Mathematically, AdaGrad modifies the standard Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) update by introducing a per-parameter adaptive learning rate, making it especially powerful in dealing with **sparse data** and **ill-conditioned objective functions** commonly encountered in deep learning.

### 1.1.1 The Vanilla SGD vs. AdaGrad Contrast

| Aspect | Vanilla SGD | AdaGrad |
|---|---|---|
| Learning Rate | Global, fixed for all parameters | Per-parameter, time-decaying |
| Sparse Features | Under-trained | Effectively trained |
| Hyperparameter Tuning | Heavy tuning of $\eta$ | Minimal tuning required |
| Convergence Behaviour | Uniform convergence | Aggressive decay over time |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Uneven Hiking Trail"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you are hiking down a mountain in thick fog, trying to find the valley. In standard SGD, you use **one fixed step size** for every direction. If the mountain is extremely steep in the north-south direction but very gentle in the east-west direction, you'll either overshoot the steep part or crawl endlessly along the flat part. **AdaGrad is like smart hiking boots that remember where the trail was steep before**: in directions where the slope has historically been steep (large accumulated gradients), the boots automatically take **shorter steps**; in directions where the slope has been gentle (small accumulated gradients), the boots take **longer steps**. Over time, the boots "adapt" to the terrain of *each individual direction*.

This is precisely what AdaGrad does in high-dimensional weight space — it treats each weight (direction) of the neural network as its own unique hiking trail.

---

## 1.3 Syllabus Highlights & Key Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key entities in AdaGrad:**
> - $\eta$ (eta) = **Global initial learning rate** (a hyperparameter, typically $0.01$ or $0.001$).
> - $\epsilon$ (epsilon) = **Smoothing constant** (a tiny value like $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-8}}$) added to the denominator to prevent division by zero.
> - $G_t$ = **Diagonal matrix of accumulated squared gradients** up to time step $t$.
> - $r_t$ = **Vector form** of accumulated squared gradients (sum of squares).
> - $g_t$ = **Gradient at time step $t$**.
