> - **Parameter Tying** = a *hard constraint* (two independent variables are forced to be equal, often across different sub-networks).
> - **Parameter Sharing** = a *structural choice* (a single variable is reused in multiple places of the computation graph).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition — The "Universal Stamp" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a postal stamping machine that uses a **single rubber stamp** to validate every letter that passes through a counter, regardless of whether the letter is destined for Kerala, Delhi, or London. The machine does not learn a new stamp for each city; it reuses one. The stamp is one "parameter" applied at many "positions."
>
> - **Parameter Sharing** in a Convolutional Neural Network works exactly this way: one filter (the stamp) slides across the entire image (the letters) producing consistent feature detection everywhere.
> - **Parameter Tying** is like forcing the *incoming stamp* and the *outgoing stamp* of a post office to be physically the same rubber die — even if they are conceptually two separate slots in the workflow.

## 1.3 Why It Matters in Deep Learning

| Aspect | Untied (Standard) Network | Tied / Shared Network |
|---|---|---|
| Free parameters | $2 \cdot N$ | $N$ (≈ **50% reduction**) |
| Memory footprint | High | Reduced |
| Generalization | Prone to overfitting on small data | Strong inductive bias |
| Translation invariance | No | **Yes** (e.g., CNNs) |

The standard metrics that matter are:
- **Number of free parameters** (model capacity)
- **Effective degrees of freedom**
- **Generalization gap** $\vert$ Train acc. $-$ Test acc. $\vert$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Stepwise

1. **Identify symmetry / equivalence** in the problem: Does the same transformation apply at multiple positions, time steps, or modalities? (e.g., translation in images, recurrence in sequences, identity in metric learning).
2. **Declare a single parameter tensor** $\mathbf{W}$ that will be reused.
3. **Replace independent weights** $\mathbf{W}_1, \mathbf{W}_2, \ldots$ in the architecture with references to the single $\mathbf{W}$.
4. **Forward pass** uses the same $\mathbf{W}$ multiple times — the graph is now *weight-tied*.
5. **Backward pass**: gradients from all tied positions are **summed** (because the same $\mathbf{W}$ contributed to multiple losses):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \sum_{k} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}}$$

6. **Update** with the accumulated gradient. This single update affects every site where $\mathbf{W}$ was used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind each step**
> - Steps 1–3 encode prior knowledge (translation, recurrence, metric similarity) into architecture.
> - Step 5 is the *regularizing mechanism* — gradient averaging acts as an implicit ensemble, smoothing updates.
> - Step 6 yields fewer effective parameters $\Rightarrow$ reduced hypothesis space $\Rightarrow$ better generalization on limited data.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme board examinations on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tying constraint | $\mathbf{W}_A = \mathbf{W}_B$ | Forces equality between two matrices |
| 2 | Tied autoencoder weight count | $N_{\text{tied}} = d_h \cdot (d_x + d_y)$ | $d_x =$ input dim, $d_y =$ output dim, $d_h =$ hidden dim |
| 3 | Untied autoencoder weight count | $N_{\text{untied}} = d_h \cdot d_x + d_y \cdot d_h$ | Independent encoder & decoder |
| 4 | Sharing gradient | $\nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_k$ | Sum of gradients from each sharing site |
| 5 | CNN parameter count (one filter) | $f_h \cdot f_w \cdot c_{\text{in}}$ | Independent of spatial size $H \times W$ |
| 6 | RNN parameter count (one layer) | $d_h^2 + d_h \cdot d_x + d_h \cdot d_y$ | Same $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ at every step |
| 7 | Regularization term (soft tying) | $\mathcal{R} = \lambda \Vert \mathbf{W}_A - \mathbf{W}_B \Vert_F^2$ | Frobenius norm penalty |
| 8 | Total tied loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{tied}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}} + \lambda \Vert \mathbf{W}_A - \mathbf{W}_B \Vert_F^2$ | Used when hard tying is infeasible |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Siamese Networks** for signature verification, face recognition, and one-shot learning tie the two twin sub-networks so embeddings live in the **same metric space**.
- **Convolutional Networks** in medical imaging, autonomous driving, and satellite image analysis share filters to achieve translation equivariance.
- **Recurrent Networks** in speech recognition and machine translation share weights across time, enabling generalization to **unseen sequence lengths**.
- **Tied Autoencoders** in NLP word embeddings (e.g., the input embedding matrix of a language model is often tied to the output projection matrix) dramatically reduce model size.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Parameter Count for a Tied vs. Untied Autoencoder

**Setup.** Consider an autoencoder with:
- Input dimension $d_x = 4$
- Hidden (code) dimension $d_h = 2$
- Reconstruction dimension $d_y = 4$

The encoder weight matrix is $\mathbf{W}_{\text{enc}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$ and the decoder weight matrix is $\mathbf{W}_{\text{dec}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y \times d_h}$.

### Case A — Untied (Standard) Autoencoder

Number of parameters in encoder:

$$N_{\text{enc}} = d_h \cdot d_x = 2 \cdot 4 = 8$$

Number of parameters in decoder:

$$N_{\text{dec}} = d_y \cdot d_h = 4 \cdot 2 = 8$$

Total untied parameters:

$$N_{\text{untied}} = N_{\text{enc}} + N_{\text{dec}} = 8 + 8 = \mathbf{16}$$

### Case B — Tied Autoencoder

We impose the hard constraint:

$$\mathbf{W}_{\text{dec}} = \mathbf{W}_{\text{enc}}^{\top}$$

This means the decoder is the *transpose* of the encoder; no new parameters are introduced. Number of free parameters:

$$N_{\text{tied}} = d_h \cdot d_x = 2 \cdot 4 = \mathbf{8}$$

The decoder output is computed as:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}} = f_{\text{dec}}(\mathbf{W}_{\text{enc}}^{\top} \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_{\text{dec}})$$

### Parameter Reduction Ratio

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{N_{\text{untied}} - N_{\text{tied}}}{N_{\text{untied}}} = \frac{16 - 8}{16} = 0.5 = \mathbf{50\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General formula:** For a symmetric autoencoder where $d_x = d_y = d$, the untied parameter count is $2 \cdot d \cdot d_h$, while the tied count is $d \cdot d_h$. The reduction ratio is therefore exactly $\mathbf{0.5}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient Accumulation Under Parameter Sharing

**Setup.** Consider a tiny RNN-like unrolling for $T = 3$ time steps with the same recurrent weight $\mathbf{W}$ used at each step. The total loss is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$$

**Step 1.** At time $t = 1$, the hidden state is:

$$\mathbf{h}_1 = \sigma(\mathbf{W} \mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{b})$$

**Step 2.** At time $t = 2$, $\mathbf{W}$ is reused:

$$\mathbf{h}_2 = \sigma(\mathbf{W} \mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{W} \mathbf{h}_1 + \mathbf{b})$$

**Step 3.** At time $t = 3$:

$$\mathbf{h}_3 = \sigma(\mathbf{W} \mathbf{x}_3 + \mathbf{W} \mathbf{h}_2 + \mathbf{b})$$

**Step 4.** Differentiate total loss w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}$ using the **chain rule**. By the multivariate chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}}$$

**Step 5.** Expand each term — for $\mathcal{L}_3$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_3}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_3}{\partial \mathbf{h}_3} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_3}{\partial \mathbf{W}} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_3}{\partial \mathbf{h}_3} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_3}{\partial \mathbf{h}_2} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_2}{\partial \mathbf{W}} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_3}{\partial \mathbf{h}_3} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_3}{\partial \mathbf{h}_2} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_2}{\partial \mathbf{h}_1} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_1}{\partial \mathbf{W}}$$

**Step 6.** Substitute the simplified notation $\delta_t = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_3}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_3}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \delta_3 \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_3}{\partial \mathbf{W}} + \delta_2 \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_2}{\partial \mathbf{W}} + \delta_1 \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_1}{\partial \mathbf{W}}$$

**Step 7.** Combining all time steps:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \delta_t \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_j}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}} \right) \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}}}$$

**Step 8.** The gradient is therefore an **unrolled sum** of contributions from every site where $\mathbf{W}$ was used — this is the famous **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** identity and the cornerstone of why parameter sharing in RNNs requires careful gradient handling (vanishing / exploding gradients).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: CNN Parameter Count (Translation-Shared Filters)

**Setup.** A single convolutional filter of size $f_h \times f_w$ with $c_{\text{in}}$ input channels and $c_{\text{out}}$ output channels.

**Step 1.** Parameters per filter (one kernel):

$$N_{\text{per filter}} = f_h \cdot f_w \cdot c_{\text{in}}$$

**Step 2.** Parameters in the full convolutional layer:

$$N_{\text{conv}} = c_{\text{out}} \cdot f_h \cdot f_w \cdot c_{\text{in}}$$

**Step 3.** Compare with a fully connected layer mapping an image of size $H \times W \times c_{\text{in}}$ to $H \times W \times c_{\text{out}}$:

$$N_{\text{FC}} = (H \cdot W \cdot c_{\text{in}}) \cdot (H \cdot W \cdot c_{\text{out}})$$

**Step 4.** Take a numerical example: $H = W = 64$, $c_{\text{in}} = 3$, $c_{\text{out}} = 16$, filter $3 \times 3$.

$$N_{\text{conv}} = 16 \cdot 3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3 = 432$$

$$N_{\text{FC}} = (64 \cdot 64 \cdot 3) \cdot (64 \cdot 64 \cdot 16) = 12{,}288 \cdot 65{,}536 = 805{,}306{,}368$$

**Step 5.** Reduction factor:

$$\frac{N_{\text{conv}}}{N_{\text{FC}}} = \frac{432}{8.05 \times 10^8} \approx 5.36 \times 10^{-7}$$

This is exactly the **parameter sharing win** in CNNs that makes modern computer vision tractable.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Between Tying & Sharing

| Scenario | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Encoder $\leftrightarrow$ Decoder in autoencoder | **Tying** (hard constraint) | Reconstruction symmetry suggests $f_{\text{dec}} = f_{\text{enc}}^{-1}$. |
| Two sub-networks processing related inputs (Siamese) | **Tying** | Both networks must embed into the same space. |
| Same filter across spatial locations (CNN) | **Sharing** (architectural) | Inductive bias of translation equivariance. |
| Same weight across time (RNN) | **Sharing** | Inductive bias of recurrence. |
| Two networks processing *unrelated* modalities | **Soft tying** (penalty term) | Hard tying is too restrictive; use $\lambda \Vert \mathbf{W}_A - \mathbf{W}_B \Vert_F^2$. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Tied Autoencoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Vector x] --> B[Encoder Linear: W_enc]
    B --> C[Hidden Code h]
    C --> D[Decoder Linear: W_enc Transpose]
    D --> E[Reconstruction x_hat]

    subgraph paramTie[Parameter Tying Constraint]
        B -. equal to .-> D
    end

    classDef tied fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    class B,D tied
```

## 4.2 Siamese Network with Weight Tying

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    I1[Input Image A] --> E1[Twin Sub-Network 1: fW]
    I2[Input Image B] --> E2[Twin Sub-Network 2: fW]
    E1 --> V1[Embedding eA]
    E2 --> V2[Embedding eB]
    V1 --> CMP[Distance Layer]
    V2 --> CMP
    CMP --> OUT[Similarity Score]

    subgraph sharedWeights[Shared Parameters]
        E1 -. identical W .-> E2
    end

    classDef shared fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    class E1,E2 shared
```

## 4.3 RNN — Parameter Sharing Across Time Steps

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1[Input t=1] --> h1[Hidden h1]
    x2[Input t=2] --> h2[Hidden h2]
    x3[Input t=3] --> h3[Hidden h3]
    h1 --> h2
    h2 --> h3
    h3 --> y[Output]

    subgraph sharedW[Recurrent Weight W shared]
        h1 -. W .-> h2
        h2 -. W .-> h3
    end
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Flow — Where Tying/Sharing is Applied

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Input Layer]) --> Mod1[Module A: CNN Backbone]
    Mod1 --> Mod2[Module B: RNN Encoder]
    Mod2 --> Mod3[Module C: Dense Head]

    Mod1 --> S1[Share: spatial filters]
    Mod2 --> S2[Share: recurrent weight W]
    Mod3 --> S3[Tie: with input embedding]

    S1 --> Benefit1[Benefit: translation equivariance]
    S2 --> Benefit2[Benefit: handles variable length]
    S3 --> Benefit3[Benefit: parameter efficiency]
```

## 4.5 Soft Tying (Regularization Penalty) Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LA[Loss Task: L_task] --> ADD[Total Loss]
    LB[Penalty: lambda times W_A minus W_B Frobenius squared] --> ADD
    ADD --> OPT[Optimizer Step]
    W_A[Parameters of Network A] -. gradient flows to .-> OPT
    W_B[Parameters of Network B] -. gradient flows to .-> OPT
```
