topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Undercomplete Autoencoder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 · Undercomplete Autoencoder

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> An **Undercomplete Autoencoder** is a type of artificial neural network used for unsupervised learning, in which the dimensionality of the latent (bottleneck) representation layer is strictly **less than** the dimensionality of the input layer. It consists of two mapping functions — an **Encoder** $\phi: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{F}$ and a **Decoder** $\psi: \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{X}$ — trained jointly to minimize a reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L}(x, \psi(\phi(x)))$ subject to the constraint $\dim(\mathcal{F}) < \dim(\mathcal{X})$.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **journalist tasked with summarizing a 500-page government report into a single 1-page news article**, and then attempting to **re-expand** that 1-page summary back into a 500-page report. The catch: your article (the bottleneck) has *less information capacity* than the original.

- If you succeed at the round-trip, you have captured the **essence** of the report — the *core ideas* that survived compression.
- If you fail, you have either over-summarized (lost critical information) or the report is poorly written (no compressible structure).

> This is precisely what an **Undercomplete Autoencoder** does with high-dimensional data (images, audio, text embeddings). The bottleneck forces the network to learn only the **most salient, compressed features**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology for KTU 2024

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Input Space | $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | Original high-dimensional data (e.g., $d = 784$ for MNIST) |
| Latent Space | $\mathcal{F} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{p}$ | Bottleneck / compressed code, with $p < d$ |
| Encoder | $\phi$ | Maps $x \to h$ (compression function) |
| Decoder | $\psi$ | Maps $h \to \hat{x}$ (reconstruction function) |
| Reconstruction | $\hat{x} = \psi(\phi(x))$ | Approximate output of the autoencoder |
| Reconstruction Loss | $\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x})$ | Measure of dissimilarity between $x$ and $\hat{x}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The strict inequality $\dim(\mathcal{F}) < \dim(\mathcal{X})$ is what makes the autoencoder **"undercomplete"**. If $\dim(\mathcal{F}) \geq \dim(\mathcal{X})$, it becomes an **overcomplete** autoencoder and would trivially learn the identity function (memorization) unless regularized (e.g., sparse or denoising autoencoders).

### 1.3 Why "Undercomplete" Matters in Deep Learning

The architectural constraint is the **primary regularizer**. By forcing the network to pass data through a low-dimensional bottleneck, we compel it to:

1. **Discover the underlying manifold** on which the data lies.
2. **Discard noise and redundant features** (dimensionality reduction).
3. **Learn compact, semantically meaningful representations** useful for downstream tasks (classification, generation, anomaly detection).
4. **Avoid the curse of dimensionality** in feature space.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, undercomplete autoencoders are presented as the **foundational generative-representation model** from which variants (sparse, denoising, variational, contractive) are derived.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network Architecture — Layer-Wise Decomposition

Let the encoder consist of $L_e$ layers and the decoder of $L_d$ layers. The full undercomplete autoencoder is parameterized by the union of weights and biases $\theta = \{W^{(l)}, b^{(l)}\}_{l=1}^{L_e + L_d}$.

**Encoder Forward Pass (Layer by Layer):**

$$h^{(1)} = \sigma_e\left(W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)}\right)$$

$$h^{(2)} = \sigma_e\left(W^{(2)} h^{(1)} + b^{(2)}\right)$$

$$\vdots$$

$$z = h^{(L_e)} = \sigma_e\left(W^{(L_e)} h^{(L_e - 1)} + b^{(L_e)}\right)$$

Here $z \in \mathcal{F}$ is the **latent code** (bottleneck output).

**Decoder Forward Pass (Layer by Layer):**

$$\hat{h}^{(1)} = \sigma_d\left(W'^{(1)} z + b'^{(1)}\right)$$

$$\hat{h}^{(2)} = \sigma_d\left(W'^{(2)} \hat{h}^{(1)} + b'^{(2)}\right)$$

$$\vdots$$

$$\hat{x} = \hat{h}^{(L_d)} = \sigma_d\left(W'^{(L_d)} \hat{h}^{(L_d - 1)} + b'^{(L_d)}\right)$$

- $\sigma_e, \sigma_d$ : encoder/decoder activation functions (commonly ReLU, sigmoid, tanh).
- $W^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_{l-1}}$ : weight matrix at layer $l$.
- $b^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}$ : bias vector at layer $l$.
- $n_{L_e} = p$ : bottleneck size, with the constraint $p < n_0 = d$.

## 2.2 The Bottleneck — Why It Works

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information Bottleneck Principle:** The hidden layer $z$ is forced to be a **lossy compression** of $x$. If $x$ lies on a low-dimensional manifold $\mathcal{M}$ of intrinsic dimension $p$ embedded in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ with $p \ll d$, then a well-trained undercomplete autoencoder learns a mapping that preserves $\mathcal{M}$.

**Mathematical justification:** If $x$ has effective rank $r < d$, then the data covariance matrix $C_x \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ has only $r$ significant eigenvalues. The encoder can project onto the subspace spanned by the top-$p$ eigenvectors (akin to PCA), and the decoder can approximately invert this projection.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a **high-yield, exam-oriented formula sheet** for Undercomplete Autoencoders as per KTU 2024 Scheme PECDT695.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Encoder mapping | $z = \phi(x) = \sigma_e(W_e x + b_e)$ | $\phi: \mathbb{R}^{d} \to \mathbb{R}^{p}$ |
| 2 | Decoder mapping | $\hat{x} = \psi(z) = \sigma_d(W_d z + b_d)$ | $\psi: \mathbb{R}^{p} \to \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 3 | Reconstruction (full) | $\hat{x} = \psi(\phi(x)) = (\psi \circ \phi)(x)$ | Composite function |
| 4 | MSE Loss (real-valued data) | $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Vert x^{(i)} - \hat{x}^{(i)} \Vert_{2}^{2}$ | $\Vert \cdot \Vert_{2}$ is L2 norm |
| 5 | Binary Cross-Entropy Loss (binary data) | $\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left[x^{(i)}_j \log \hat{x}^{(i)}_j + (1 - x^{(i)}_j)\log(1 - \hat{x}^{(i)}_j)\right]$ | Use sigmoid output |
| 6 | Total Parameter Count (single hidden layer AE) | $N_{params} = (d \cdot p + p) + (p \cdot d + d) = 2dp + p + d$ | Encoder + Decoder |
| 7 | Bottleneck Constraint | $p < d$ | Defines "undercomplete" |
| 8 | Optimization Objective | $\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \, \mathcal{L}(x, \psi(\phi(x)))$ | $\theta = \{W_e, b_e, W_d, b_d\}$ |
| 9 | Linear AE Optimality (if $\sigma = $ identity) | $W_e, W_d$ span top-$p$ eigenspace of $C_x$ | Equivalent to PCA subspace |
| 10 | Reconstruction Error Bound (ideal) | $\mathbb{E}\left[\Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert_{2}^{2}\right] = \sum_{i=p+1}^{d}\lambda_i$ | $\lambda_i$ = discarded eigenvalues of $C_x$ |

> **Constraint Reminder:** All table cells use `\Vert` for vector norms to prevent Markdown table-breaking. Exam-takers should write $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ or $\vert \cdot \vert$ on paper.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Application of Undercomplete Autoencoder |
|--------|------------------------------------------|
| **Computer Vision** | Image denoising, feature extraction, anomaly detection in manufacturing |
| **Medical Imaging** | Compressing MRI / CT scans while preserving diagnostic features |
| **Signal Processing** | Speech feature compression, audio denoising |
| **Cybersecurity** | Anomaly / intrusion detection (high reconstruction error = anomaly) |
| **Recommendation Systems** | Collaborative filtering via latent user-item embeddings |
| **Natural Language Processing** | Word embedding compression, document summarization |
| **Industrial IoT** | Sensor data dimensionality reduction for predictive maintenance |

## 2.5 Why an Undercomplete Autoencoder ≠ Just a Compressor

The crucial point for KTU 2024: an undercomplete autoencoder is **not** a generic lossless compressor (like ZIP). The reconstruction is intentionally **lossy**, but the loss is **learned** — designed to preserve features relevant to the data distribution, not arbitrary bytes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Pass Composition

**Goal:** Show that the autoencoder's output $\hat{x}$ is a composition of the encoder and decoder mappings.

**Step 1 — Encoder definition.**
The encoder is a parameterized differentiable function:
$$\phi: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{F}, \quad z = \phi(x; \theta_e)$$
where $\theta_e = \{W^{(1)}, \dots, W^{(L_e)}, b^{(1)}, \dots, b^{(L_e)}\}$.

**Step 2 — Decoder definition.**
The decoder is also a parameterized differentiable function:
$$\psi: \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{X}, \quad \hat{x} = \psi(z; \theta_d)$$

**Step 3 — Composition.**
The full autoencoder is the composition of $\phi$ and $\psi$:
$$\hat{x} = \psi(\phi(x)) = (\psi \circ \phi)(x)$$

> **Interpretation:** This composition is differentiable end-to-end (assuming differentiable activations), so we can apply **backpropagation through time / chain rule** for gradient-based optimization.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gradient of MSE Loss for a Single-Layer Linear Undercomplete Autoencoder

**Setup:** Consider a single-layer linear autoencoder with identity activations and squared-error loss. This is the canonical KTU exam case.

$$z = W_e x, \quad \hat{x} = W_d z = W_d W_e x$$

$$W_e \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times d}, \quad W_d \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times p}, \quad x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$$

**Step 1 — Loss function for $N$ samples.**
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x^{(i)} - W_d W_e x^{(i)} \Vert_{2}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Data-centered assumption.**
Assume the data is zero-centered: $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i} x^{(i)} = 0$. Then define the data matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times N}$ with columns $x^{(i)}$. The sample covariance is:
$$C_x = \frac{1}{N} X X^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$$

**Step 3 — Compact loss form.**
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x^{(i)} - W_d W_e x^{(i)})^{\top}(x^{(i)} - W_d W_e x^{(i)})$$

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \operatorname{tr}\left[(X - W_d W_e X)^{\top}(X - W_d W_e X)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Let $A = W_d W_e \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$.**
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \operatorname{tr}\left[(X - AX)^{\top}(X - AX)\right]$$

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \operatorname{tr}\left[X^{\top}(I_d - A)^{\top}(I_d - A) X\right]$$

**Step 5 — Spectral decomposition of $C_x$.**
Since $C_x$ is real symmetric PSD, we have:
$$C_x = U \Lambda U^{\top}$$
where $U \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ is orthogonal, $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_d)$ with $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_d \geq 0$.

**Step 6 — Optimal solution.**
A classical result (Baldi & Hornik, 1989) shows that the optimal $A^{*} = W_d^{*} W_e^{*}$ spans the subspace of the top-$p$ eigenvectors of $C_x$. In closed form:

$$A^{*} = U_p U_p^{\top}$$

where $U_p \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times p}$ contains the top-$p$ eigenvectors. One simple parameterization is:
$$W_e^{*} = U_p^{\top}, \quad W_d^{*} = U_p$$

**Step 7 — Minimum achievable loss.**
Substituting $A^{*}$ into the loss:
$$\mathcal{L}^{*} = \frac{1}{N} \operatorname{tr}\left[U_{\perp} \Lambda_{\perp} U_{\perp}^{\top}\right] = \sum_{i=p+1}^{d} \lambda_i$$

> **Key Insight:** The minimum reconstruction error equals the sum of the **discarded eigenvalues** — i.e., the variance in directions **not** preserved by the bottleneck.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Backpropagation Through the Bottleneck

**Step 1 — Loss gradient with respect to decoder output.**
$$\delta^{(L)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}} = \frac{2}{N}(\hat{x} - x)$$

**Step 2 — Gradient at bottleneck $z$.**
Applying the chain rule:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = \left(\frac{\partial \hat{x}}{\partial z}\right)^{\top} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}} = W_d^{\top} \cdot \delta^{(L)}$$

**Step 3 — Gradient with respect to encoder weights $W_e$.**
For the encoder output $z = W_e x$:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_e} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} \cdot x^{\top} = (W_d^{\top} \delta^{(L)}) x^{\top}$$

**Step 4 — Gradient with respect to decoder weights $W_d$.**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_d} = \delta^{(L)} \cdot z^{\top}$$

**Step 5 — Update rule (Gradient Descent).**
$$W_e \leftarrow W_e - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_e}, \quad W_d \leftarrow W_d - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_d}$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Undercomplete vs. Other Variants

| Property | Undercomplete AE | Sparse AE | Denoising AE | Variational AE |
|----------|------------------|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| Regularization mechanism | Bottleneck dimension ($p < d$) | Sparsity penalty on $z$ | Stochastic input corruption | KL divergence on $z$ |
| Constraint on $p$ | $p < d$ (strict) | $p \geq d$ allowed | $p \geq d$ allowed | $p$ user-defined |
| Risk of identity learning | Low | Low | Low | Very low (probabilistic) |
| Probabilistic latent space | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Generative capability | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| KTU 2024 emphasis | **Core / Foundational** | Extension topic | Extension topic | Module 4 advanced topic |

## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem:** A single-layer linear undercomplete autoencoder is trained on a 4-dimensional dataset with bottleneck size $p = 2$. The sample covariance matrix $C_x$ has eigenvalues $\lambda_1 = 5.0$, $\lambda_2 = 3.0$, $\lambda_3 = 1.0$, $\lambda_4 = 0.5$. Compute (a) the optimal encoder and decoder matrices (assuming orthonormal eigenvectors), and (b) the minimum achievable MSE.

**Solution:**

**(a)** The top-2 eigenvectors span the optimal 2D subspace. Let $U_2 = [u_1, u_2] \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 2}$. Then:
$$W_e^{*} = U_2^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 4}, \quad W_d^{*} = U_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 2}$$

**(b)** The minimum MSE is the sum of discarded eigenvalues:
$$\mathcal{L}^{*} = \lambda_3 + \lambda_4 = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5$$

> **Exam Tip:** Always justify the choice of $p$ based on the **explained variance ratio** $\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$. For the example above, the 2D code captures $(5.0 + 3.0)/9.5 \approx 84.2\%$ of total variance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture — Undercomplete Autoencoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT LAYER\nx in R^d] --> B[ENCODER HIDDEN 1\nh1 in R^n1]
    B --> C[ENCODER HIDDEN 2\nh2 in R^n2]
    C --> D["BOTTLENECK LAYER\nz in R^p  (p LESS THAN d)"]
    D --> E[DECODER HIDDEN 1\nh3 in R^n2]
    E --> F[DECODER HIDDEN 2\nh4 in R^n1]
    F --> G[OUTPUT LAYER\nxhat in R^d]
    H[LOSS FUNCTION\nL equals x minus xhat squared] -.monitors.-> G
    H -.backprop gradient.-> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The information flow narrows from input dimension $d$ to bottleneck dimension $p$, then re-expands to $d$. The constraint $p < d$ is enforced architecturally.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PhaseA[PHASE 1 FORWARD PASS]
        A1[Raw Input Batch X] --> A2[Encoder Network phi]
        A2 --> A3[Latent Code Z]
        A3 --> A4[Decoder Network psi]
        A4 --> A5[Reconstruction Xhat]
        A5 --> A6[Compute Loss L of X, Xhat]
    end
    subgraph PhaseB[PHASE 2 BACKWARD PASS]
        A6 --> B1[Compute Gradients of L wrt W and b]
        B1 --> B2[Update W and b via Gradient Descent]
    end
    subgraph PhaseC[PHASE 3 EVALUATION]
        B2 --> C1{Monitor Reconstruction Quality}
        C1 --> C2[Track Validation Loss]
        C1 --> C3[Check Bottleneck Saturation]
    end
    A6 -.loss gradient.-> PhaseB
    B2 -.updated parameters.-> A1
```

## 4.3 Data Flow — Information Compression & Reconstruction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[STEP 1 Input x in R^784 e.g. MNIST] --> P2[STEP 2 Encoder Linear plus ReLU]
    P2 --> P3[STEP 3 Bottleneck z in R^32]
    P3 --> P4[STEP 4 Decoder Linear plus Sigmoid]
    P4 --> P5[STEP 5 Output xhat in R^784]
    P5 --> P6[STEP 6 Compare xhat to x via MSE]
    P6 --> P7[STEP 7 Backpropagate Error]
    P7 -.gradient flow.-> P2
    P7 -.gradient flow.-> P4
```

> **Example mapping (KTU 2024 standard illustration):** Input image $x \in \mathbb{R}^{784}$ (28×28 flattened MNIST digit) is compressed to latent vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^{32}$ (compression ratio $\approx 24.5\times$) and reconstructed back to $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{784}$.

## 4.4 Conceptual Mapping — Undercomplete AE vs. PCA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PCA[PCA Linear Approach]
        Q1[Compute Covariance Cx] --> Q2[Eigendecomposition Cx equals U Lambda U transpose]
        Q2 --> Q3[Select top p eigenvectors U sub p]
        Q3 --> Q4[Project x to z equals U sub p transpose x]
    end
    subgraph UCAE[Undercomplete Autoencoder]
        R1[Random Initialize W sub e and W sub d] --> R2[Forward Pass phi and psi]
        R2 --> R3[Compute Reconstruction Loss]
        R3 --> R4[Backprop and Update Weights]
        R4 -.iterate.-> R2
    end
    Q4 -.equivalent optimum.-> R2
```

> **Key takeaway (high-yield for KTU):** A **linear undercomplete autoencoder converges to the PCA solution** when the loss is squared error. Nonlinear activations yield nonlinear generalizations of PCA (e.g., kernel PCA, manifold learning).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an undercomplete autoencoder. What is the significance of the bottleneck layer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **undercomplete autoencoder** is a neural network trained to reconstruct its input via a hidden **bottleneck layer** $z$ whose dimensionality $p$ is strictly less than the input dimensionality $d$ (i.e., $p < d$).

**Significance of the bottleneck (2 marks):**
1. It **regularizes** the network by preventing trivial identity mapping.
2. It forces the network to learn a **compressed, low-dimensional representation** of the input that captures its most essential features.
3. The bottleneck implements an **information-theoretic compression**, learning the data manifold $\mathcal{M}$ embedded in $\mathcal{X}$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the squared error loss function commonly used for training undercomplete autoencoders? State the formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Squared error (MSE) is preferred because:
1. It is **differentiable and convex** for linear models, enabling stable gradient-based optimization.
2. For zero-centered Gaussian-distributed inputs, MSE is the **negative log-likelihood**, making it the maximum-likelihood estimator.
3. It directly measures **Euclidean reconstruction quality**.

**Formula:**
$$\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x^{(i)} - \hat{x}^{(i)} \Vert_{2}^{2}$$

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one full question** from a choice of two. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an **undercomplete autoencoder**. Clearly define the encoder, decoder, bottleneck, and reconstruction loss. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **minimum reconstruction error of a linear undercomplete autoencoder** in terms of the eigenvalues of the data covariance matrix. Show that the optimal encoder/decoder pair spans the subspace of the top-$p$ eigenvectors. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

- The undercomplete autoencoder consists of an **encoder** $\phi: \mathbb{R}^{d} \to \mathbb{R}^{p}$ and a **decoder** $\psi: \mathbb{R}^{p} \to \mathbb{R}^{d}$, with $p < d$.
- The encoder compresses input $x$ into latent code $z = \phi(x) = \sigma_e(W_e x + b_e)$.
- The decoder reconstructs $\hat{x} = \psi(z) = \sigma_d(W_d z + b_d)$.
- The **bottleneck** is the smallest layer (dimension $p$), enforcing compression.
- The **reconstruction loss** $\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x})$ is minimized end-to-end via backpropagation.
- *(Include the architecture diagram from Section 4.1 — 2 marks reserved for the diagram.)*

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**

Let $A = W_d W_e \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ and assume zero-centered data.

The loss is:
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\operatorname{tr}\left[X^{\top}(I_d - A)^{\top}(I_d - A) X\right]$$

Using the spectral decomposition $C_x = \frac{1}{N} X X^{\top} = U \Lambda U^{\top}$ with $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_d)$, $\lambda_1 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_d \geq 0$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \operatorname{tr}\left[U^{\top}(I_d - A)^{\top}(I_d - A) U \Lambda\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i \Vert (I_d - A)u_i \Vert_{2}^{2}$$

Since $A$ has rank at most $p$, it can preserve at most $p$ dimensions. To minimize the weighted sum, $A$ must be the projection onto the subspace of the top-$p$ eigenvectors, giving $A^{*} = U_p U_p^{\top}$.

The minimum loss is:
$$\mathcal{L}^{*} = \sum_{i=p+1}^{d} \lambda_i$$

This represents the **total variance in the discarded directions**.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`
**(a)** Compare and contrast **undercomplete autoencoders**, **sparse autoencoders**, and **denoising autoencoders** in terms of regularization mechanism, architectural constraints, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a dataset with covariance eigenvalues $\{9, 4, 1, 0.5, 0.1\}$. If an undercomplete autoencoder with bottleneck size $p = 3$ is trained, compute: (i) the **explained variance ratio**, and (ii) the **minimum reconstruction error**. Comment on whether $p = 3$ is a good choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Undercomplete AE | Sparse AE | Denoising AE |
|--------|------------------|-----------|--------------|
| Regularization | Bottleneck dimension | Sparsity penalty on $z$ | Stochastic input noise |
| Architectural constraint | $p < d$ | $p \geq d$ allowed | $p \geq d$ allowed |
| Risk of identity mapping | Low (bottleneck prevents) | Low (sparsity prevents) | Low (noise prevents) |
| Input | Clean $x$ | Clean $x$ | Corrupted $\tilde{x}$ |
| Training target | Reconstruct $x$ | Reconstruct $x$ | Reconstruct clean $x$ from $\tilde{x}$ |
| Typical application | Compression, anomaly detection | Feature selection, interpretability | Denoising, robust representations |

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Total variance: $\sum_{i=1}^{5} \lambda_i = 9 + 4 + 1 + 0.5 + 0.1 = 14.6$

**(i) Explained variance ratio (3 marks):**
$$\text{EVR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{3} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} \lambda_i} = \frac{9 + 4 + 1}{14.6} = \frac{14}{14.6} \approx 0.959 = 95.9\%$$

**(ii) Minimum reconstruction error (3 marks):**
$$\mathcal{L}^{*} = \sum_{i=4}^{5} \lambda_i = 0.5 + 0.1 = 0.6$$

**Comment (1 mark):** $p = 3$ is an **excellent choice**, capturing 95.9% of the variance with a low residual error of 0.6 (only 4.1% information loss). The eigenvalue drop from $\lambda_3 = 1$ to $\lambda_4 = 0.5$ confirms a clear "elbow" justifying $p = 3$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint $p < d$:** Students often describe the architecture without explicitly stating the undercomplete constraint. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Conflating reconstruction loss with classification loss:** MSE measures *reconstruction*, not classification accuracy. Examiners specifically look for the term "reconstruction error" or "log-likelihood."
> 3. **Omitting diagrams in 7-mark sub-parts:** KTU evaluators typically reserve 2 marks for a labeled architecture diagram. Always draw and label encoder, decoder, and bottleneck.
> 4. **Skipping the undercomplete vs. overcomplete distinction:** Examiners often award marks for explicitly contrasting undercomplete AE with overcomplete AE and explaining the role of the bottleneck as a regularizer.
> 5. **Incorrect derivation of the optimal subspace:** Students should explicitly show the connection to the eigendecomposition of $C_x$ and the rank-$p$ constraint on $A = W_d W_e$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Undercomplete Autoencoder (KTU 2024 PECDT695 Module 4)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Autoencoder with bottleneck dimension $p$ strictly less than input dimension $d$ ($p < d$).
- [ ] **Two components:** Encoder $\phi: \mathbb{R}^{d} \to \mathbb{R}^{p}$ and Decoder $\psi: \mathbb{R}^{p} \to \mathbb{R}^{d}$.
- [ ] **Composite function:** $\hat{x} = \psi(\phi(x)) = (\psi \circ \phi)(x)$.
- [ ] **Loss function:** MSE for continuous inputs $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Vert x^{(i)} - \hat{x}^{(i)} \Vert_{2}^{2}$; BCE for binary inputs.
- [ ] **Training:** End-to-end backpropagation, gradient descent, parameter update $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}$.
- [ ] **Optimality (linear case):** Equivalent to PCA; encoder/decoder span top-$p$ eigenvectors of $C_x$.
- [ ] **Minimum MSE:** $\mathcal{L}^{*} = \sum_{i=p+1}^{d} \lambda_i$ (discarded eigenvalues).
- [ ] **Regularization mechanism:** Architectural (bottleneck), not explicit penalty.
- [ ] **Risk of identity learning:** Avoided by $p < d$ — overcomplete AE risks trivial identity solution.
- [ ] **Applications:** Dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection, denoising, feature extraction, pre-training.
- [ ] **Nonlinear extension:** Multi-layer nonlinear encoder/decoder learn manifold coordinates (generalization of PCA / kernel PCA).
- [ ] **Variants to know:** Sparse AE, Denoising AE, Contractive AE, Variational AE (VAE).
- [ ] **Hyperparameters to tune:** Bottleneck size $p$, learning rate $\eta$, depth of encoder/decoder, activation functions.
- [ ] **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR):** $\text{EVR}(p) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$ — used to select $p$.

> **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024):** *"Undercomplete = bottleneck $p < d$ + reconstruction loss + learned compressed representation. Linear case reduces to PCA. The bottleneck IS the regularizer."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stochastic Encoder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stochastic Encoder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Stochastic Encoder** is a probabilistic neural network module that maps a high-dimensional input observation $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ into the parameters of a probability distribution defined over a lower-dimensional latent space $\mathcal{Z} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (where typically $d \ll D$). Unlike a deterministic encoder, which compresses an input to a single fixed latent code $\mathbf{z} = f_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$, the stochastic encoder outputs the sufficient statistics (e.g., mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ and covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$) of an approximate posterior distribution $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$, from which a latent code is *sampled*.

In the canonical **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** framework, the stochastic encoder is parameterised by a deep neural network with weights and biases collectively denoted $\phi$, and it implements a recognition model (or inference network) of the form:

$$q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{z} \,\vert\, \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}),\, \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{I}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *stochastic* encoder does not produce a *value* — it produces a *distribution*. The latent vector $\mathbf{z}$ is then *drawn* from this distribution, introducing a deliberate, learnable randomness essential for generative modelling and regularised latent representations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **photographer's contact sheet**:
- A **deterministic encoder** is like printing *one fixed polaroid* of every scene — the same input always gives the same exact slide.
- A **stochastic encoder** is like keeping a *shaky, breathing photographer* who, every time they take a picture of the same scene, holds the camera in *slightly different positions*. The result is a *cloud of similar but not identical* photos clustered around a central tendency.

The "shakiness" is not a bug — it is a **feature**. By sampling from a distribution, the encoder:
1. Forces the latent space to be **continuous and smooth** (nearby latent points decode to similar outputs).
2. Provides a principled mechanism for **regularisation** (preventing overfitting).
3. Enables **generative sampling** — you can pick any point from the prior and decode it into a new, plausible data sample.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** A stochastic encoder turns a real data point into a "range of possible compressed meanings" rather than a single rigid code. The decoder then has to learn to reconstruct the original data from *any* of those possible meanings, which is what forces the latent space to become meaningful and well-organised.

### 1.3 Key Notational Conventions

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}$ | Observed input data point, $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ |
| $\mathbf{z}$ | Latent variable, $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| $p(\mathbf{x})$ | True (unknown) data distribution |
| $p(\mathbf{z})$ | Prior over latent space (typically $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$) |
| $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})$ | Decoder / generative model |
| $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$ | Stochastic encoder / approximate posterior |
| $\phi$ | Encoder network parameters |
| $\theta$ | Decoder network parameters |
| $D$ | Dimensionality of input space |
| $d$ | Dimensionality of latent space |

### 1.4 Why "Stochastic"? The Core Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Deterministic vs. Stochastic Encoder — The Defining Difference**
> - **Deterministic:** $\mathbf{z} = f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$ — a *function* of $\mathbf{x}$. No randomness.
> - **Stochastic:** $\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$ — *sampled* from a learned distribution conditional on $\mathbf{x}$. The same $\mathbf{x}$ can map to *infinitely many* different $\mathbf{z}$ values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Walk-Through: How a Stochastic Encoder Works

The end-to-end pipeline of a VAE-style stochastic encoder proceeds in five canonical stages:

1. **Input Ingestion:** The input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ (e.g., a flattened image of $28 \times 28 = 784$ pixels) is fed into an encoder neural network — usually a stack of fully-connected or convolutional layers.
2. **Parameter Head Production:** Two parallel "heads" branch off the shared encoder trunk. One head outputs a mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, the other outputs a log-variance vector $\log \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (log-variance is used for numerical stability).
3. **Distribution Construction:** An isotropic multivariate Gaussian is assembled, $\mathcal{N}\!\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}),\, \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}))\right)$.
4. **Latent Sampling (with Reparameterisation Trick):** A latent code is drawn as

   $$\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$$

   The Hadamard product $\odot$ multiplies element-wise. This is the *reparameterisation trick* — it keeps the sampling operation **differentiable** so gradient descent works.
5. **Reconstruction Hand-off:** $\mathbf{z}$ is passed to the decoder $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})$ to reconstruct $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$, which is compared against $\mathbf{x}$ to compute a loss.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1–2 (Shared trunk with two heads):** Sharing parameters before the branching point reduces the parameter count and forces the network to learn a unified feature extractor.
- **Step 3 (Gaussian assumption):** The isotropic Gaussian is a *design choice*, not a theorem. It is favoured because (a) it is computationally cheap, (b) it has closed-form KL-divergence with the standard normal prior, and (c) it is smooth and well-behaved under optimisation.
- **Step 4 (Reparameterisation):** Naïvely sampling $\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2})$ blocks backpropagation because the sampling operation is non-differentiable. The reparameterisation moves the *source of randomness* to an external noise variable $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$, leaving a deterministic, differentiable path from $\phi$ to $\mathbf{z}$.
- **Step 5 (Reconstruction):** The decoder must learn to invert *any* plausible latent code, which is what imparts generative capability to the model.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-yield for KTU 2024 examinations on this module.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stochastic encoder output (Gaussian form) | $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{z}\,\vert\,\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}),\boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{I}\right)$ | Probability density over $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 2 | Prior distribution | $p(\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ | Standard normal |
| 3 | Reparameterisation trick | $\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon},\ \boldsymbol{\epsilon}\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$ | $\odot$ is element-wise product |
| 4 | KL divergence (closed form) | $D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) \,\Vert\, p(\mathbf{z})\right] = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{d}\!\left(1 + \log\sigma_{j}^{2} - \mu_{j}^{2} - \sigma_{j}^{2}\right)$ | Sum over $d$ latent dimensions |
| 5 | Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) | $\mathcal{L}(\phi,\theta;\mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})\right] - D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\,\Vert\,p(\mathbf{z})\right]$ | To be **maximised** |
| 6 | Reconstruction term (Gaussian) | $\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z}) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{D}\!\left(\frac{(x_{i}-\hat{x}_{i})^{2}}{\eta^{2}} + \log(2\pi\eta^{2})\right)$ | $\eta$ is output noise |
| 7 | Bernoulli reconstruction (binary $\mathbf{x}$) | $\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z}) = \sum_{i=1}^{D}\!\left[x_{i}\log\hat{x}_{i} + (1-x_{i})\log(1-\hat{x}_{i})\right]$ | Equivalent to binary cross-entropy |
| 8 | Monte Carlo ELBO estimator | $\tilde{\mathcal{L}} \approx \tfrac{1}{L}\sum_{l=1}^{L}\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z}^{(l)}) - D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\,\Vert\,p(\mathbf{z})\right]$ | $L$ = number of samples |
| 9 | Total loss to **minimise** | $\mathcal{J}(\phi,\theta) = -\mathcal{L}(\phi,\theta;\mathbf{x})$ | Negative ELBO |

> **CRITICAL:** Per KTU 2024 valuation norms, students must state *both* the closed-form KL term *and* the reparameterisation trick in any full-mark answer. Omitting either is a common 2-mark deduction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stochastic encoders are the workhorse of:

- **Generative drug discovery** — sampling novel molecular structures from learned latent spaces.
- **Image synthesis & super-resolution** — Denoising VAEs, NVAE for high-fidelity image generation.
- **Anomaly detection** — high reconstruction likelihood gap flags out-of-distribution industrial defects.
- **Representation learning for downstream tasks** — the regularised latent code transfers to classifiers, segmenters, and reinforcement-learning policies.
- **Speech & audio generation** — WaveVAE, Vector-Quantised VAEs (VQ-VAE) build on the stochastic-encoder principle.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Reparameterisation Trick

**Goal:** Compute $\nabla_{\phi}\,\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}[f(\mathbf{z})]$ in a differentiable manner.

**Step 1 — Write the expectation explicitly:**

$$\nabla_{\phi}\,\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}[f(\mathbf{z})] = \nabla_{\phi}\int q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\,f(\mathbf{z})\,d\mathbf{z}$$

**Step 2 — Attempt to push the gradient inside the integral (the naïve approach):**

$$= \int \nabla_{\phi}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\right]\,f(\mathbf{z})\,d\mathbf{z}$$

This integral is generally intractable because $q_{\phi}$ is a complex function of $\phi$.

**Step 3 — Introduce a change of variable that absorbs the parameters into a deterministic transformation.**

Recognise that if $\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}), \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{I})$, then $\mathbf{z}$ can equivalently be written as an affine transformation of a standard normal variable $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$:

$$\mathbf{z} = g_{\phi}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite the expectation as an expectation over $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$, which has no $\phi$-dependence:**

$$\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}[f(\mathbf{z})] = \mathbb{E}_{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})}\!\left[f\!\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})\odot\boldsymbol{\epsilon}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Now the gradient can be moved inside the expectation (the expectation is over $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ alone):**

$$\nabla_{\phi}\,\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}[f(\mathbf{z})] = \mathbb{E}_{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})}\!\left[\nabla_{\phi}\,f\!\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})\odot\boldsymbol{\epsilon}\right)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Approximate the expectation with a Monte Carlo average of $L$ samples:**

$$\nabla_{\phi}\,\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}[f(\mathbf{z})] \approx \frac{1}{L}\sum_{l=1}^{L}\nabla_{\phi}\,f\!\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})\odot\boldsymbol{\epsilon}^{(l)}\right)$$

The gradient $\nabla_{\phi}$ is now taken inside a deterministic computation graph, so standard backpropagation applies. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The randomness is *re-routed* through an external noise variable $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ that does not depend on $\phi$. All $\phi$-dependence now flows through the deterministic, differentiable path $\phi \to (\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi}) \to \mathbf{z}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Form KL Divergence Between Two Diagonal Gaussians

**Goal:** Show that

$$D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2}\mathbf{I})\,\Vert\,\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})\right] = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{d}\!\left(1 + \log\sigma_{j}^{2} - \mu_{j}^{2} - \sigma_{j}^{2}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of KL divergence for two densities $P$ and $Q$:**

$$D_{\text{KL}}(P \,\Vert\, Q) = \int p(\mathbf{z})\log\frac{p(\mathbf{z})}{q(\mathbf{z})}\,d\mathbf{z}$$

**Step 2 — Write the Gaussian densities component-wise. For the $j$-th dimension, the marginals are $\mathcal{N}(\mu_{j}, \sigma_{j}^{2})$ and $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$:**

$$p(z_{j}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{j}^{2}}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z_{j}-\mu_{j})^{2}}{2\sigma_{j}^{2}}\right), \quad q(z_{j}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{z_{j}^{2}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the KL integral. Because the covariances are diagonal, the joint KL decomposes into a sum of marginal KLs:**

$$D_{\text{KL}} = \sum_{j=1}^{d}\int p(z_{j})\log\frac{p(z_{j})}{q(z_{j})}\,dz_{j}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the log-ratio inside the integral:**

$$\log\frac{p(z_{j})}{q(z_{j})} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\log\sigma_{j}^{2} - \frac{(z_{j}-\mu_{j})^{2}}{2\sigma_{j}^{2}} + \frac{z_{j}^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Take expectations under $p(z_{j}) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_{j}, \sigma_{j}^{2})$:**

- $\mathbb{E}[z_{j}] = \mu_{j}$
- $\mathbb{E}[z_{j}^{2}] = \mu_{j}^{2} + \sigma_{j}^{2}$
- $\mathbb{E}[(z_{j}-\mu_{j})^{2}] = \sigma_{j}^{2}$

**Step 6 — Substitute these into the expanded expression:**

$$D_{\text{KL}} = \sum_{j=1}^{d}\!\left[-\tfrac{1}{2}\log\sigma_{j}^{2} - \frac{\sigma_{j}^{2}}{2\sigma_{j}^{2}} + \frac{\mu_{j}^{2} + \sigma_{j}^{2}}{2}\right]$$

**Step 7 — Simplify term by term:**

$$= \sum_{j=1}^{d}\!\left[-\tfrac{1}{2}\log\sigma_{j}^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_{j}^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma_{j}^{2}\right]$$

**Step 8 — Factor out the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ and re-arrange to the standard form:**

$$= -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{d}\!\left(1 + \log\sigma_{j}^{2} - \mu_{j}^{2} - \sigma_{j}^{2}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: From Marginal Log-Likelihood to the ELBO

**Goal:** Show that the marginal log-likelihood $\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ decomposes into the ELBO plus a non-negative residual, proving the ELBO is a valid lower bound.

**Step 1 — Introduce an arbitrary variational distribution $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$ and write:**

$$\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = \log\int p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})\,d\mathbf{z} = \log\int q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\,\frac{p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}\,d\mathbf{z}$$

**Step 2 — Recognise the right-hand side as $\log\,\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\cdot]$:**

$$= \log\,\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}\!\left[\frac{p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply Jensen's inequality ($\log$ is concave, so $\log\,\mathbb{E}[\cdot] \geq \mathbb{E}[\log\cdot]$):**

$$\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}\!\left[\log\frac{p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Expand the joint using the chain rule $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})p(\mathbf{z})$:**

$$\geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z}) + \log p(\mathbf{z}) - \log q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\right]$$

**Step 5 — Group the first term and split the expectation of the log-ratio into the standard ELBO form:**

$$\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) \geq \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}[\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})]}_{\text{reconstruction term}} - \underbrace{D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\,\Vert\,p(\mathbf{z})\right]}_{\text{regularisation term}}$$

**Step 6 — Define the ELBO:**

$$\mathcal{L}(\phi,\theta;\mathbf{x}) \equiv \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})] - D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\,\Vert\,p(\mathbf{z})\right] \leq \log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 7 — The residual gap is the *intractable* true posterior term:**

$$\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{L}(\phi,\theta;\mathbf{x}) + D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\,\Vert\,p_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\right]$$

Since $D_{\text{KL}} \geq 0$, the ELBO is always a lower bound. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Deterministic vs. Stochastic Encoders

| Attribute | Deterministic Autoencoder | Stochastic Encoder (VAE-style) |
|---|---|---|
| Output of encoder | Single point $\mathbf{z} = f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$ | Distribution parameters $(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi})$ |
| Latent code obtained by | Direct evaluation | Sampling, $\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi} + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi}\odot\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ |
| Latent-space regularity | No explicit constraint; holes & discontinuities common | Enforced by KL term pulling $q_{\phi}$ toward $p(\mathbf{z})$ |
| Generative sampling | Not directly possible (no prior model) | Directly sample $\mathbf{z}\sim p(\mathbf{z})$, then decode |
| Loss function | Reconstruction MSE only | Negative ELBO (reconstruction − KL) |
| Training stability | Very stable | Requires reparameterisation trick; can suffer KL vanishing |
| Probability model | Not probabilistic | Fully probabilistic (variational) |
| Typical use | Compression, denoising, representation learning | Generative modelling, density estimation, anomaly detection |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Stochastic Encoder (VAE) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input X dim D"] --> B["Encoder Network Shared Trunk"]
    B --> C["Mean Head"]
    B --> D["Log Variance Head"]
    C --> E["Sample Z via Reparameterisation"]
    D --> E
    Epsilon["Noise Epsilon N 0 I"] --> E
    E --> F["Latent Code Z dim d"]
    F --> G["Decoder Network"]
    G --> H["Reconstruction X hat dim D"]
    I["Prior p of Z N 0 I"] -.KL Term.-> J["Loss Negative ELBO"]
    H --> J
    C -.-> J
    D -.-> J
    J --> K["Backprop Update phi theta"]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Reparameterisation Trick Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M["mu phi of x"] --> Add["Addition Node"]
    S["sigma phi of x"] --> Mul["Elementwise Multiply"]
    Noise["epsilon N 0 I"] --> Mul
    Mul --> Add
    Add --> Zout["Latent z"]
    M -.backprop.-> GradUpdates["Gradient flows to phi"]
    S -.backprop.-> GradUpdates
    Noise -.stop grad.-> GradUpdates
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Recognition Model q phi of z given x"
        A1["Input Layer"] --> A2["Encoder Dense or Conv Stack"]
        A2 --> A3["Bottleneck Features"]
        A3 --> A4["Mean Head FC Layer"]
        A3 --> A5["Log Variance Head FC Layer"]
    end
    subgraph "Stochastic Sampling Layer"
        A4 --> A6["Reparameterisation z = mu plus sigma times epsilon"]
        A5 --> A6
        Ext["External epsilon N 0 I"] --> A6
    end
    subgraph "Generative Model p theta of x given z"
        A6 --> A7["Decoder Dense or Deconv Stack"]
        A7 --> A8["Output Reconstruction x hat"]
    end
    subgraph "Loss Computation"
        A8 --> A9["Reconstruction Loss BCE or MSE"]
        A4 --> A10["KL Divergence Closed Form"]
        A5 --> A10
        A9 --> A11["Total Loss J = minus ELBO"]
        A10 --> A11
    end
    A11 --> A12["Optimizer Adam or RMSprop Update phi and theta"]
```

### 4.4 Training vs. Inference Mode Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Training Mode"
        T1["Sample z using reparameterisation"] --> T2["Compute stochastic gradient of ELBO"]
    end
    subgraph "Inference Mode"
        I1["Use mean mu phi of x as z"] --> I2["Deterministic embedding"]
    end
    T2 --> Outcome["Latent Distribution Learned"]
    I2 --> Outcome
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** During *training*, the stochastic path is used so that gradients flow through the noise. During *inference* (i.e., when extracting a stable embedding for downstream tasks), it is common to use only the mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$ to obtain a deterministic representation.
