## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> Differentiate between a deterministic encoder and a stochastic encoder. State the role of the reparameterisation trick in training a stochastic encoder.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** A **deterministic encoder** maps an input $\mathbf{x}$ to a *single* fixed latent vector $\mathbf{z} = f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$ with no randomness involved.
- **(1 mark)** A **stochastic encoder** maps $\mathbf{x}$ to the *parameters of a probability distribution* $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$, from which a latent code is *sampled*, allowing the same $\mathbf{x}$ to map to many different $\mathbf{z}$.
- **(1 mark)** The **reparameterisation trick** expresses the sample as $\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ with $\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$, moving the source of randomness to an external noise variable so that backpropagation through the sampling operation becomes possible.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Write the expression for the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) in a Variational Autoencoder and identify the role of each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\mathcal{L}(\phi,\theta;\mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})\right] - D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\,\Vert\,p(\mathbf{z})\right]$$

- **(1 mark)** The first term (reconstruction term) measures how well the decoder reconstructs $\mathbf{x}$ from a sampled latent code.
- **(1 mark)** The second term (KL regulariser) penalises the encoder when its approximate posterior $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$ drifts away from the prior $p(\mathbf{z})$.
- **(1 mark)** Maximising the ELBO jointly improves reconstruction quality and keeps the latent space well-organised and continuous, enabling generative sampling.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) and clearly identify the role of the stochastic encoder. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the closed-form expression for the KL divergence term $D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}), \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{I}) \,\Vert\, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})\right]$ used in the VAE objective. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **(2 marks) High-level description:** A VAE is a deep generative model comprising two networks — an encoder $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$ (recognition/inference model) and a decoder $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})$ (generative model). It is trained by maximising the ELBO over parameters $(\phi, \theta)$.

- **(3 marks) Block diagram and stochastic encoder role:** The encoder takes $\mathbf{x}$, passes it through shared dense/conv layers, and branches into a *mean head* and a *log-variance head*. Together they parameterise $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}), \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{I}\right)$. A latent code is drawn using the reparameterisation trick. The decoder takes $\mathbf{z}$ and outputs $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$.

- **(2 marks) Training objective:** Total loss is the negative ELBO. The reconstruction term is a binary cross-entropy (or MSE) between $\mathbf{x}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$, and the KL term acts as a regulariser pulling the encoder distribution toward the standard normal prior. Optimisation is done via stochastic gradient descent (typically Adam) with the reparameterised gradient.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

The full derivation is shown in **Section 3.2** above. Key checkpoints for marking:

- **(2 marks)** Correctly stating the definition of KL divergence and identifying the joint-vs-marginal decomposition.
- **(3 marks)** Expanding the log-ratio of the two Gaussian densities and substituting the expectations $\mathbb{E}[z_{j}] = \mu_{j}$, $\mathbb{E}[z_{j}^{2}] = \mu_{j}^{2} + \sigma_{j}^{2}$, $\mathbb{E}[(z_{j}-\mu_{j})^{2}] = \sigma_{j}^{2}$.
- **(2 marks)** Arriving at the final closed-form answer:

$$D_{\text{KL}} = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{d}\!\left(1 + \log\sigma_{j}^{2} - \mu_{j}^{2} - \sigma_{j}^{2}\right)$$

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Explain the concept of the reparameterisation trick. Why is it essential for training a stochastic encoder, and what problem does it solve? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Starting from the marginal log-likelihood, derive the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) and explain how it relates to the stochastic encoder training objective. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **(2 marks) Definition:** The reparameterisation trick rewrites the sampling step $\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2}_{\phi}\mathbf{I})$ as $\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi} + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi}\odot\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ with $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$.

- **(3 marks) Problem solved:** Direct sampling is non-differentiable, blocking gradient-based optimisation. By routing randomness through the external $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ (which has no $\phi$-dependence), the dependence of $\mathbf{z}$ on $\phi$ becomes a *deterministic* differentiable function, so backpropagation can compute $\nabla_{\phi}\mathcal{L}$.

- **(2 marks) Practical effect:** It makes stochastic encoder training feasible via standard mini-batch gradient descent, while preserving the desired variance of the latent distribution and enabling Monte Carlo estimation of the ELBO.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

- **(3 marks) Starting from $\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$**, introduce the variational distribution $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$ and apply Jensen's inequality as shown in **Section 3.3**, Steps 1–4.

- **(2 marks) Group terms** to isolate the reconstruction term and the KL regulariser:

$$\mathcal{L}(\phi,\theta;\mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})] - D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\,\Vert\,p(\mathbf{z})\right]$$

- **(2 marks) Connect to training objective:** The stochastic encoder $q_{\phi}$ is *trained* by maximising the ELBO. The reconstruction term encourages the encoder-decoder pair to preserve information about $\mathbf{x}$, while the KL term keeps the encoder's output distribution close to the prior, which is what makes the latent space smooth, continuous, and amenable to generative sampling.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 2-Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation (per examiner key patterns):**
> 1. **Writing "the encoder outputs $\mathbf{z}$" instead of "the encoder outputs the parameters of $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$."** This is the single most common conceptual error and loses the first mark immediately in Part A questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the reparameterisation trick in VAE derivations.** Even if the rest of the derivation is correct, omitting the reparameterisation trick typically costs 2 marks in Part B sub-parts.
> 3. **Inverting the KL direction.** The KL term in the ELBO is $D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) \,\Vert\, p(\mathbf{z})\right]$, *not* the reverse. Reversing the order is a definitional error.
> 4. **Sign errors in the loss.** The training objective to *minimise* is the *negative* ELBO, $\mathcal{J} = -\mathcal{L}$. Confusing "maximise ELBO" with "minimise ELBO" is a common slip.
> 5. **Treating the VAE encoder as deterministic in generative contexts.** Generative sampling requires $\mathbf{z}\sim p(\mathbf{z})$, *not* $\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$. Examiners expect students to state this distinction explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stochastic Encoder**

- **Definition:** A stochastic encoder outputs *distribution parameters* $(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}), \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}))$, not a single value of $\mathbf{z}$.
- **Form:** $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}), \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{2}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{I}\right)$.
- **Reparameterisation trick:** $\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$, $\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ — **essential for backprop**.
- **Prior:** $p(\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ (standard normal).
- **ELBO (maximise):** $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})] - D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\,\Vert\,p(\mathbf{z})\right]$.
- **Closed-form KL (must memorise):** $D_{\text{KL}} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{d}\!\left(1 + \log\sigma_{j}^{2} - \mu_{j}^{2} - \sigma_{j}^{2}\right)$.
- **Loss to minimise:** $\mathcal{J} = -\mathcal{L}$.
- **Jensen's inequality** is the bridge from $\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ to the ELBO.
- **The gap** $\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) - \mathcal{L} = D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\,\Vert\,p_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})\right] \geq 0$, so the ELBO is a valid lower bound.
- **Deterministic vs. stochastic** — state clearly which one a vanilla autoencoder uses (deterministic) versus a VAE (stochastic).
- **Inference vs. training** — at inference, often use $\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$; at training, use the reparameterised sample.
- **Generative use:** sample $\mathbf{z}\sim p(\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ and decode — this is *not* an inference step.
- **Log-variance head** (not variance) is standard practice for numerical stability.
- **Assumption of diagonal covariance** simplifies the KL to a closed-form sum; full covariance would require more expensive matrix operations.
- **Engineering applications:** anomaly detection, drug discovery, image/audio synthesis, representation learning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Denoising Encoder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Denoising Encoder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4: Denoising Encoder (Denoising Autoencoder)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Denoising Autoencoder (DAE)** is a specialized variant of the standard autoencoder that is explicitly trained to reconstruct a **clean, uncorrupted output** from a **partially corrupted (noisy) input**. It learns a robust latent representation by minimizing the reconstruction error between the original clean data $\mathbf{x}$ and the decoder's output produced from a corrupted version $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ of the same input.

Mathematically, given a clean input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$, a stochastic corruption process $q(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})$ produces a corrupted input $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$. The DAE then learns the parameters $\theta = \{\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{W}', \mathbf{b}'\}$ that minimize the average reconstruction loss:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}} \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim q(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})} \left[ L\left( \mathbf{x}, \, g_{\theta'}\!\left( f_{\theta}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}) \right) \right) \right]$$

The encoder and decoder mappings are defined as:

$$\mathbf{h} = f_{\theta}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}) = \sigma(\mathbf{W}\tilde{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{b})$$

$$\mathbf{r} = g_{\theta'}(\mathbf{h}) = \sigma(\mathbf{W}'\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}')$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Forensic Sketch Artist**
> 
> Imagine a famous painting that has been vandalized with graffiti, scratches, and dust. A normal autoencoder behaves like an artist who simply *copies* what they see — they reproduce the damaged painting exactly. A **Denoising Autoencoder** behaves like a **restorer** who has memorized the original artist's style: even when given a damaged version, they reconstruct what the painting *should have looked like* before the damage occurred.
> 
> The key insight: by forcing the network to predict the *clean* signal from a *noisy* input, the model cannot learn a trivial identity function. Instead, it must discover the **underlying statistical structure, semantic features, and manifold** of the data — making its representations significantly more robust and meaningful for downstream tasks.

### 1.3 Why Denoising? The Key Limitation of Vanilla Autoencoders

> [!WARNING]
> A standard autoencoder with sufficient capacity (hidden units ≥ input units) can learn the **identity function** — it simply copies input to output. This produces useless representations. DAE overcomes this by injecting **stochastic noise** during training, breaking the symmetry of trivial solutions and forcing the network to learn *meaningful* generalizations.

### 1.4 Types of Corruption Processes $q(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})$

| Corruption Type | Mechanism | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Masking Noise** | A fraction $\nu$ of input dimensions are randomly set to 0 | Sparse high-dimensional data (e.g., bag-of-words) |
| **Gaussian Noise** | Additive noise $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} + \epsilon$, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I})$ | Continuous data (images, sensor readings) |
| **Salt-and-Pepper Noise** | Random pixels set to min/max values | Image data |
| **Dropout Corruption** | Bernoulli masking with rate $p$ | General-purpose feature learning |

**Standard Metric Highlighted:**
The **corruption level** $\nu \in [0,1]$ is typically set between **0.1 and 0.5** (i.e., 10%–50% of dimensions corrupted). Values too high destroy information; values too low fail to regularize.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step

The denoising autoencoder operates in a five-stage pipeline during training:

1. **Sample Clean Input**: Draw $\mathbf{x}$ from the training data distribution $p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})$.
2. **Apply Stochastic Corruption**: Generate $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim q(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})$ using one of the corruption strategies listed above. The corruption is **applied only during training**; at test time, clean input is passed through directly.
3. **Encode**: Map the corrupted input to a latent representation:
$$\mathbf{h} = f_{\theta}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}) = \sigma(\mathbf{W}\tilde{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{b})$$
4. **Decode**: Reconstruct an estimate of the clean input from the latent code:
$$\mathbf{r} = g_{\theta'}(\mathbf{h}) = \sigma(\mathbf{W}'\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}')$$
5. **Compute Loss & Backpropagate**: Measure the discrepancy between the reconstruction $\mathbf{r}$ and the *original clean* $\mathbf{x}$ (not the corrupted version!), then update parameters via gradient descent.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1–2 (Corruption)**: Injects information loss, compelling the encoder to learn a **stochastic mapping** rather than a deterministic copy.
- **Step 3 (Encoding)**: The hidden layer $\mathbf{h}$ is forced to capture the **most salient, invariant features** that survive noise — these are the "manifold directions" of the data distribution.
- **Step 4 (Decoding)**: Acts as a generative prior — the decoder $g$ learns to project latent codes back to the data manifold.
- **Step 5 (Loss vs. Clean)**: The crucial distinction! Loss is computed against $\mathbf{x}$ (clean), not $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ (corrupted). This is what creates the "denoising" behavior.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Component | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Encoder** | $\mathbf{h} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}\tilde{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{b})$ | Maps corrupted input to latent space |
| **Decoder** | $\mathbf{r} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}'\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}')$ | Reconstructs clean input from latent code |
| **Corruption Model** | $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim q(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Stochastic corruption process |
| **MSE Loss** | $L_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mathbf{r}^{(i)} \Vert_2^2$ | For continuous-valued data |
| **Binary Cross-Entropy** | $L_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \log \mathbf{r}^{(i)} + (1-\mathbf{x}^{(i)})\log(1-\mathbf{r}^{(i)}) \right]$ | For binary/normalized inputs |
| **Tied Weights** | $\mathbf{W}' = \mathbf{W}^T$ | Common parameter-sharing constraint |
| **Gradient (Encoder)** | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \delta_h \cdot \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^T$ | Backpropagation signal |
| **Gradient (Decoder)** | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}'} = \delta_r \cdot \mathbf{h}^T$ | Backpropagation signal |
| **Corruption Level** | $\nu \in [0.1, 0.5]$ | Recommended range for masking/dropout |
| **Score Matching Equivalence** | $\Vert J_{f}(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_F^2 + \text{const}$ | DAE approximates contractive regularizer |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Denoising autoencoders are deployed across multiple engineering domains:
> - **Medical Imaging**: Removing noise from MRI/CT scans while preserving diagnostic features
> - **Speech Enhancement**: Cleaning audio signals for hearing aids and voice assistants
> - **Industrial Inspection**: Restoring degraded sensor readings from manufacturing lines
> - **Pre-training**: Stacked DAEs pre-train deep networks layer-by-layer (a precursor to modern self-supervised learning)
> - **Anomaly Detection**: High reconstruction error indicates out-of-distribution samples

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gradient of the DAE Loss Function

We derive the gradient of the reconstruction loss with respect to the encoder weights $\mathbf{W}$, assuming the squared Euclidean loss and tied weights ($\mathbf{W}' = \mathbf{W}^T$).

**Step 1: Define the loss for a single sample.**

$$L(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{r}) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{r} \Vert_2^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - r_j)^2$$

*Explanation:* The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a convention to cancel the exponent during differentiation.

**Step 2: Express the reconstruction explicitly.**

$$\mathbf{r} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{h}) = \sigma(\mathbf{W}^T \sigma(\mathbf{W}\tilde{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{b}) + \mathbf{b}')$$

*Explanation:* We substitute the encoder output $\mathbf{h}$ into the decoder, using the tied-weight assumption $\mathbf{W}' = \mathbf{W}^T$.

**Step 3: Compute the decoder-side error signal $\delta_r$.**

$$\delta_r = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}_r} = (\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{x}) \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}_r)$$

where $\mathbf{z}_r = \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}'$ is the pre-activation of the decoder.

*Explanation:* The chain rule gives the loss derivative w.r.t. the decoder pre-activation as the Hadamard product of the output error and the derivative of the activation.

**Step 4: Propagate the error to the encoder pre-activation $\mathbf{z}_h$.**

$$\delta_h = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}_h} = \left( \mathbf{W}^T \delta_r \right) \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}_h)$$

*Explanation:* The decoder weights transpose the error back to the hidden layer, then the encoder activation derivative is applied.

**Step 5: Compute the gradient w.r.t. encoder weights $\mathbf{W}$.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \delta_h \cdot \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^T$$

*Explanation:* This is the outer product of the hidden-layer error with the (corrupted) input. Notice that the gradient depends on $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ — the corrupted input — but the target remains $\mathbf{x}$, which is what drives the denoising behavior.

**Step 6: Parameter update rule.**

$$\mathbf{W} \leftarrow \mathbf{W} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \mathbf{W} - \eta \cdot \delta_h \cdot \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^T$$

*Explanation:* Standard gradient descent with learning rate $\eta$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalence to Score Matching (Analytical Matrix)

A landmark theoretical result (Vincent, 2011) establishes that **denoising autoencoders implicitly learn the score function** — the gradient of the log-density $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p(\mathbf{x})$ — which connects them to the contractive autoencoder family.

**Step 1: Consider the MSE loss under Gaussian corruption with small noise $\sigma$.**

$$\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} + \sigma \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{I})$$

**Step 2: Expand the expected loss using a Taylor series around $\mathbf{x}$.**

$$L = \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon}\left[ \Vert \mathbf{x} - g(f(\mathbf{x} + \sigma\epsilon)) \Vert^2 \right]$$

**Step 3: For a well-trained network where $g(f(\mathbf{x})) \approx \mathbf{x}$, the optimal denoising function is:**

$$g(f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})) = \mathbf{x} + \sigma^2 \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p(\mathbf{x}) + \mathcal{O}(\sigma^3)$$

*Explanation:* The denoiser's residual is proportional to the **score function** $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p(\mathbf{x})$ — this is the gradient pointing toward higher data density. This means the DAE learns an implicit generative model.

**Step 4: Taking the limit $\sigma \to 0$:**

$$\lim_{\sigma \to 0} \frac{g(f(\mathbf{x} + \sigma\epsilon)) - \mathbf{x}}{\sigma^2} = \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p(\mathbf{x})$$

*Explanation:* In the small-noise limit, the Jacobian of the encoder $J_f(\mathbf{x})$ is shown to satisfy:

$$\Vert J_f(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_F^2 \propto \Vert \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p(\mathbf{x}) \Vert^2 + \text{const}$$

This proves DAE ≈ Contractive Autoencoder (CAE) for small Gaussian noise, justifying the table entry in the formula sheet.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing Loss & Corruption

| Data Type | Recommended Loss | Recommended Corruption | Typical $\nu$ / $\sigma$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grayscale Images (normalized to [0,1]) | Binary Cross-Entropy | Masking + Salt-Pepper | $\nu = 0.3$ |
| RGB Images | MSE | Gaussian + Masking | $\sigma = 0.1$, $\nu = 0.2$ |
| Audio Waveforms | MSE | Gaussian Noise | $\sigma = 0.05$ |
| Tabular Continuous | MSE | Gaussian Noise | $\sigma = 0.1 \cdot \text{std}(x)$ |
| Binary Bag-of-Words | Binary Cross-Entropy | Masking (dropout) | $\nu = 0.3$–$0.5$ |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DAE Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Clean Input Vector x] --> nodeB[Corruption Process q]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Corrupted Input xTilde]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Encoder f theta]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Latent Code h]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Decoder g thetaPrime]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Reconstruction r]
    nodeA --> nodeH[Loss Computation]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Parameter Update theta]
    nodeI --> nodeD
    nodeI --> nodeF
```

*Reading Guide:* The clean input $\mathbf{x}$ is split into two paths — one is corrupted via $q(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})$ to produce $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$, which is fed through the encoder–decoder pipeline. The original clean $\mathbf{x}$ is retained for loss computation, ensuring the network learns to *denoise* rather than copy.

### 4.2 Stacked Denoising Autoencoder (SDA) — Layer-wise Pre-training

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer1
        direction LR
        in1[Input] --> enc1[Encoder 1]
        enc1 --> h1[Latent h1]
        h1 --> dec1[Decoder 1]
        dec1 --> out1[Reconstruction]
    end
    subgraph Layer2
        direction LR
        in2[h1 from Layer 1] --> enc2[Encoder 2]
        enc2 --> h2[Latent h2]
        h2 --> dec2[Decoder 2]
        dec2 --> out2[Reconstruction]
    end
    subgraph Layer3
        direction LR
        in3[h2 from Layer 2] --> enc3[Encoder 3]
        enc3 --> h3[Latent h3]
        h3 --> dec3[Decoder 3]
        dec3 --> out3[Reconstruction]
    end
    Layer1 --> Layer2
    Layer2 --> Layer3
    h3 --> final[Final Deep Representation]
```

*Reading Guide:* In SDA, each layer is trained as a DAE using the previous layer's latent representation as input. The final deep representation is then used for supervised fine-tuning. This was a breakthrough pre-training paradigm for deep networks before the dominance of modern self-supervised methods.

### 4.3 Information Flow — Why DAE Learns Better Features

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Corrupted Input xTilde] --> nodeB[Encoder forces robust features]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Latent h captures data manifold]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Decoder uses h to reconstruct x]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compare to Clean x]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Backprop to reduce gap]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Updated encoder is noise-invariant]
    nodeG --> nodeA
```

*Reading Guide:* The loop illustrates the self-reinforcing nature of DAE training: corruption forces robust features, robust features improve reconstruction, and better reconstruction updates the encoder to be even more noise-invariant.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*Define a denoising autoencoder. How does it differ from a standard autoencoder in terms of training objective?*

**Model Answer:**
A **denoising autoencoder (DAE)** is a neural network trained to reconstruct the **original uncorrupted input** from a **corrupted version** of it. The training objective is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}} \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim q(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})} \left[ L\!\left( \mathbf{x}, \, g(f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})) \right) \right]$$

The **key difference** from a standard autoencoder is that the input passed to the encoder is corrupted $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$, but the **reconstruction target is the clean $\mathbf{x}$**. In a standard autoencoder, both input and target are identical. This forces the DAE to learn **robust, noise-invariant features** rather than a trivial identity mapping, producing more meaningful latent representations suitable for downstream tasks like classification, denoising, and anomaly detection.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*List any three corruption strategies used in denoising autoencoders. What is the recommended range of corruption level $\nu$?*

**Model Answer:**
Three commonly used corruption strategies are:
1. **Masking Noise (Dropout)**: A fraction $\nu$ of input dimensions are randomly set to 0 via Bernoulli sampling.
2. **Gaussian Noise**: Continuous noise $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I})$ is added to the input.
3. **Salt-and-Pepper Noise**: Randomly selected dimensions are set to either the minimum or maximum value (commonly used for image data).

The **recommended corruption level** $\nu$ is typically in the range **[0.1, 0.5]** (10%–50%). Values below 0.1 provide insufficient regularization (model may still learn identity), while values above 0.5 destroy too much information, making reconstruction infeasible.

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

**Choose either Question A or Question B and answer both sub-parts.**

#### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working of a denoising autoencoder. Describe the role of the corruption process $q(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the gradient of the MSE reconstruction loss with respect to the encoder weight matrix $\mathbf{W}$ in a denoising autoencoder, showing all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Architecture and Working:**

A denoising autoencoder consists of two components — an **encoder** $f_\theta$ and a **decoder** $g_{\theta'}$ — with a stochastic **corruption process** applied to the input before encoding.

**Working Steps:**

1. **Input Sampling**: A clean training sample $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is drawn from the data distribution $p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})$.
2. **Corruption**: A corrupted version $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \sim q(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})$ is generated. For masking noise, this means:
$$\tilde{x}_j = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{with probability } \nu \\ x_j & \text{with probability } 1-\nu \end{cases}$$
3. **Encoding**: The corrupted input is mapped to a hidden representation:
$$\mathbf{h} = f_\theta(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}) = \sigma(\mathbf{W}\tilde{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{b})$$
4. **Decoding**: The hidden code is mapped back to a reconstruction:
$$\mathbf{r} = g_{\theta'}(\mathbf{h}) = \sigma(\mathbf{W}'\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}')$$
5. **Loss Computation**: The MSE loss is computed between the **clean** $\mathbf{x}$ and reconstruction $\mathbf{r}$:
$$L = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mathbf{r}^{(i)} \Vert_2^2$$
6. **Backpropagation**: Gradients are backpropagated through the decoder and encoder to update $\theta$ and $\theta'$.

**Role of the Corruption Process:**
The corruption process $q(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})$ is the **defining element** of the DAE. It:
- **Prevents identity learning**: By destroying information, it forces the network to generalize.
- **Acts as a regularizer**: Analogous to dropout, it improves generalization to unseen data.
- **Encourages manifold learning**: The encoder must capture directions along the data manifold that are robust to local perturbations.
- **Enables semantic feature extraction**: The latent $\mathbf{h}$ encodes high-level structure rather than pixel-level details.

**Block Diagram (reproduced for answer):**

```
x → [Corruption q] → x̃ → [Encoder W,b] → h → [Decoder W',b'] → r
                              ↑                                      ↓
                              └────── Loss L(x, r) ←─────────────────┘
```

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Derivation of $\partial L / \partial \mathbf{W}$:**

**Step 1:** Loss for a single sample (using $\frac{1}{2}$ factor):

$$L = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - r_j)^2$$

**Step 2:** Decoder pre-activation and output:

$$\mathbf{z}_r = \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}', \quad \mathbf{r} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}_r)$$

**Step 3:** Gradient of loss w.r.t. decoder pre-activation (chain rule):

$$\delta_r = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}_r} = (\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{x}) \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}_r)$$

**Step 4:** Gradient w.r.t. hidden pre-activation $\mathbf{z}_h = \mathbf{W}\tilde{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{b}$:

$$\delta_h = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}_h} = (\mathbf{W}^T \delta_r) \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}_h)$$

**Step 5:** Gradient w.r.t. encoder weights $\mathbf{W}$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \delta_h \cdot \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^T$$

**Step 6:** Update rule:

$$\mathbf{W} \leftarrow \mathbf{W} - \eta \cdot \delta_h \cdot \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^T$$

**Key Observation:** The gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}}$ depends on the **corrupted** input $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ (because the forward pass used $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$), but the target is the **clean** $\mathbf{x}$ (from the loss). This asymmetry is precisely what drives the denoising behavior — the network must use information in $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ to predict something *different* from it.

---

#### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **Stacked Denoising Autoencoder (SDA)**. How does layer-wise unsupervised pre-training using DAEs benefit deep network training? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that for small Gaussian noise, a denoising autoencoder approximates the **Contractive Autoencoder (CAE)** objective, establishing the connection to the score function $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p(\mathbf{x})$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Stacked Denoising Autoencoder (SDA):**

An SDA is a deep architecture formed by **stacking multiple DAE layers**, where the latent representation of layer $k$ serves as the input for training layer $k+1$.

**Layer-wise Training Procedure:**

1. **Train Layer 1** as a DAE: input $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$, learn $\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = f^{(1)}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(0)})$.
2. **Train Layer 2** as a DAE: input $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = \mathbf{h}^{(1)}$, learn $\mathbf{h}^{(2)} = f^{(2)}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(1)})$.
3. Continue for $L$ layers.
4. **Supervised Fine-tuning**: Unroll all encoders, attach a softmax classifier, and fine-tune end-to-end using labeled data.

**Benefits of Layer-wise Pre-training:**

- **Better Initialization**: Avoids poor local minima in deep networks.
- **Greedy Feature Hierarchy**: Each layer learns progressively abstract features (edges → textures → parts → objects).
- **Regularization Effect**: DAE corruption acts as implicit regularization, reducing overfitting.
- **Enables Training of Deep Networks**: Historically critical before the advent of ReLU, BatchNorm, and residual connections.
- **Semi-supervised Learning**: Pre-training uses only unlabeled data, allowing labeled data to be reserved for fine-tuning.

**Mathematical Formulation for Layer $k$:**

$$\mathbf{h}^{(k)} = \sigma\!\left( \mathbf{W}^{(k)} \tilde{\mathbf{h}}^{(k-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(k)} \right), \quad \tilde{\mathbf{h}}^{(k-1)} \sim q(\cdot \mid \mathbf{h}^{(k-1)})$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**DAE–CAE Equivalence under Small Gaussian Noise:**

**Step 1:** Define Gaussian corruption: $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} + \sigma \epsilon$, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{I})$.

**Step 2:** DAE minimizes expected MSE:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} = \mathbb{E}_\epsilon \left[ \Vert \mathbf{x} - g(f(\mathbf{x} + \sigma\epsilon)) \Vert^2 \right]$$

**Step 3:** Expanding $g(f(\mathbf{x} + \sigma\epsilon))$ via first-order Taylor around $\mathbf{x}$:

$$g(f(\mathbf{x} + \sigma\epsilon)) \approx g(f(\mathbf{x})) + \sigma \cdot J_{g \circ f}(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \epsilon$$

Since $g(f(\mathbf{x})) \approx \mathbf{x}$ for a well-trained network:

$$g(f(\mathbf{x} + \sigma\epsilon)) \approx \mathbf{x} + \sigma \cdot J_{g \circ f}(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \epsilon$$

**Step 4:** Substituting and using $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$, $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon \epsilon^T] = \mathbf{I}$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} \approx \sigma^2 \cdot \mathbb{E}_\epsilon \left[ \Vert J_{g \circ f}(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \epsilon \Vert^2 \right] = \sigma^2 \cdot \Vert J_{g \circ f}(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_F^2$$

**Step 5:** For tied weights and symmetric encoder–decoder, $J_{g \circ f}(\mathbf{x}) \approx J_f(\mathbf{x})$ (up to constant scaling). Thus:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} \approx \sigma^2 \cdot \Vert J_f(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_F^2 + \text{const}$$

**Step 6:** Comparing with the **CAE objective**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CAE}} = L(\mathbf{x}, g(f(\mathbf{x}))) + \lambda \Vert J_f(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_F^2$$

we see that **DAE implicitly penalizes the Frobenius norm of the encoder Jacobian** — exactly the contractive penalty.

**Step 7:** The connection to the **score function** emerges because the optimal small-noise denoiser satisfies:

$$g(f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})) = \mathbf{x} + \sigma^2 \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p(\mathbf{x}) + \mathcal{O}(\sigma^3)$$

Therefore:

$$\Vert J_f(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_F^2 \propto \Vert \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p(\mathbf{x}) \Vert^2 + \text{const}$$

**Conclusion:** The DAE, in the small-noise limit, learns a representation whose Jacobian encodes the **score function of the data distribution** — equivalent to the contractive regularizer. This is a foundational result in the theoretical analysis of deep representation learning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing input and target**: Students often write the loss as $L(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}, g(f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})))$ — this is **wrong**! The loss is always against the *clean* $\mathbf{x}$, not the corrupted $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$. (Loss: **2 marks**)
> 2. **Omitting the corruption process**: Defining a DAE without mentioning $q(\tilde{\mathbf{x}} \mid \mathbf{x})$ is incomplete. (Loss: **1 mark**)
> 3. **Forgetting backpropagation chain**: In the gradient derivation, students frequently jump from $L$ directly to $\partial L / \partial \mathbf{W}$, skipping $\delta_r$ and $\delta_h$. (Loss: **2–3 marks**)
> 4. **Confusing DAE with VAE**: DAE uses deterministic encoder/decoder with stochastic input corruption; VAE uses stochastic encoder/decoder with KL regularization. Do not interchange! (Loss: **2 marks**)
> 5. **Skipping the SDA motivation**: Part B(a) requires the *rationale* for stacking — just drawing a diagram is insufficient. (Loss: **1–2 marks**)

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **DAE Definition**: Autoencoder trained to reconstruct **clean** $\mathbf{x}$ from **corrupted** $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$.
- ✅ **Corruption Operators**: Masking, Gaussian, Salt-Pepper, Dropout — applied only during training.
- ✅ **Corruption Level**: $\nu \in [0.1, 0.5]$ is the sweet spot; never 0, never > 0.7.
- ✅ **Loss Target**: Always the **clean** $\mathbf{x}$, never the corrupted $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$.
- ✅ **MSE vs. BCE**: MSE for continuous data; BCE for binary/normalized data.
- ✅ **Why It Works**: Forces the encoder to learn **noise-invariant, semantically meaningful** features along the data manifold.
- ✅ **Stacked DAE (SDA)**: Layer-wise greedy pre-training + supervised fine-tuning; historically crucial for deep learning.
- ✅ **DAE ≈ CAE**: For small Gaussian noise, DAE's gradient approximates the contractive penalty (Frobenius norm of encoder Jacobian).
- ✅ **Score Function Connection**: DAE implicitly learns $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p(\mathbf{x})$ in the small-$\sigma$ limit.
- ✅ **No Identity Mapping**: Unlike vanilla AEs, DAEs **cannot** trivially copy input because information has been destroyed.
- ✅ **Modern Relevance**: Foundation of **self-supervised learning** (e.g., masked autoencoders like MAE in computer vision).
- ✅ **Exam Tip**: Always include the corruption diagram and the loss equation; these are the most heavily weighted components.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Autoencoders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications of Autoencoders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Autoencoder (AE):** A *self-supervised* neural network trained to reconstruct its input $\mathbf{x}$ at the output layer by passing the data through a **bottleneck** (latent) layer $\mathbf{z}$, such that $\mathbf{z}$ captures only the most salient, compressed representation of the input. Formally, an autoencoder is the composition of two mappings — an **encoder** $f_{\phi}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Z}$ and a **decoder** $g_{\theta}: \mathcal{Z} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ — trained to minimize a reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})))$.

Mathematically:

$$
\mathbf{z} = f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_e \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_e)
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = g_{\theta}(\mathbf{z}) = \sigma'(\mathbf{W}_d \mathbf{z} + \mathbf{b}_d)
$$

The parameters $(\phi, \theta)$ are jointly optimized to minimize:

$$
\phi^*, \theta^* = \arg\min_{\phi,\theta} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\bigl(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})) \bigr)
$$

## 1.2 The KTU 2024 Schema — Application Taxonomy

Under the **PECDT695 / Deep Learning / Module 4** syllabus, the examination focus is on **how the structural variants of autoencoders are deployed in real-world tasks**. The following taxonomy is the *official framework* for KTU answers:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Variants and Their Principal Applications**
>
> | Variant | Latent Constraint | Primary KTU Application |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Vanilla / Undercomplete AE** | $\dim(\mathbf{z}) < \dim(\mathbf{x})$ | Dimensionality Reduction, Data Compression |
> | **Denoising Autoencoder (DAE)** | Stochastic corruption + reconstruction of clean $\mathbf{x}$ | **Image/Acoustic Denoising** |
> | **Sparse Autoencoder (SAE)** | Sparsity penalty $\Omega(\mathbf{z})$ | Feature Learning, Anomaly Detection |
> | **Convolutional Autoencoder (CAE)** | Convolutional encoder–decoder | **Super-Resolution, Inpainting, Colorization** |
> | **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** | Posterior $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) \approx \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}^2)$ | **Generative Modeling, Image Synthesis** |
> | **Contractive Autoencoder (CAE)** | $\|\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})\|_F^2$ penalty | Robust Feature Extraction |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Photographer & The Sketchpad"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a student-artist (the *encoder*) who is shown a high-resolution **photograph** of a face. Instead of copying the photograph pixel-by-pixel (which requires millions of numbers), the artist draws a **rough pencil sketch** of just the essential features — eye shape, nose length, jawline, expression. This sketch — small, abstract, but information-rich — is the **latent code $\mathbf{z}$**.
>
> Later, a second artist (the *decoder*) looks at the sketch and *re-draws a full photograph* from memory. If the reconstruction is faithful, the latent sketch has successfully captured the essence of the original face. **Applications of autoencoders are simply the clever re-use of this encoder–decoder pair** — sometimes we throw away the decoder and use only the latent code (compression), sometimes we corrupt the input before encoding (denoising), and sometimes we sample new sketches to generate never-before-seen faces (generation).

## 1.4 Why Autoencoders Matter for KTU Engineering

Autoencoders occupy a **central role in modern Deep Learning pipelines** because they simultaneously solve:

1. **Representation Learning** — replace hand-crafted features.
2. **Generative Modeling** — sample from a learned distribution.
3. **Signal Restoration** — recover clean signals from corrupted ones.
4. **Anomaly Detection** — flag inputs with high reconstruction error.

These four pillars map directly to the **Course Outcomes (CO4 / CO5)** of the 2024 scheme, which expect students to "design and evaluate deep generative and reconstructive architectures for engineering data."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Generic Autoencoder

An autoencoder is a *composition* of three functional blocks:

1. **Encoder** $f_{\phi}$ — maps $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ to $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_z}$ (typically $d_z \ll d_x$ for an *undercomplete* AE).
2. **Latent / Bottleneck Layer** — the compressed code $\mathbf{z}$.
3. **Decoder** $g_{\theta}$ — maps $\mathbf{z}$ back to $\hat{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$.

The **loss landscape** is shaped entirely by the choice of $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \hat{\mathbf{x}})$ and by any *regularizer* imposed on $\mathbf{z}$. This single design lever generates the entire zoo of AE variants.

## 2.2 Loss Functions — The Foundation of Every Application

### 2.2.1 Mean Squared Error (MSE) — used for real-valued images, audio

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}}(\mathbf{x}, \hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)} \right\|_2^2
$$

### 2.2.2 Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) — used when inputs are in $[0,1]$ (e.g., MNIST)

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}}(\mathbf{x}, \hat{\mathbf{x}}) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{d_x} \left[ x^{(i)}_j \log \hat{x}^{(i)}_j + (1 - x^{(i)}_j) \log(1 - \hat{x}^{(i)}_j) \right]
$$

### 2.2.3 Reconstruction Loss + Regularizer (Generic AE objective)

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{AE}} = \underbrace{\mathcal{L}\bigl(\mathbf{x}, g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}))\bigr)}_{\text{fit term}} + \lambda \cdot \underbrace{\Omega(\mathbf{z})}_{\text{regularizer}}
$$

The choice of $\Omega(\mathbf{z})$ defines the variant:

| Regularizer $\Omega(\mathbf{z})$ | Resulting AE Variant | Resulting Application |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda \|\mathbf{z}\|_2^2$ | Weight-decay AE | Compression |
| $\lambda \sum_j \mathrm{KL}(\rho \,\|\, \hat{\rho}_j)$ | **Sparse AE** | Feature selection, Anomaly detection |
| $\lambda \|\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})\|_F^2$ | **Contractive AE** | Robust feature extraction |
| $\mathrm{KL}\bigl(q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) \,\|\, p(\mathbf{z})\bigr)$ | **Variational AE** | Generation, Synthesis |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master-Reference Table for Autoencoder Applications (Module 4)**

| # | Application | AE Variant Used | Key Equation / Loss | Latent Dimension | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Image Denoising** | Denoising AE | $\mathcal{L} = \| \mathbf{x} - g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})) \|_2^2$ | $d_z = d_x$ | Clean $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ |
| 2 | **Super-Resolution** | Convolutional AE | $\mathcal{L} = \| \mathbf{x}^{HR} - g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}^{LR})) \|_2^2$ | $d_z = d_x^{LR}$ | High-Res Image |
| 3 | **Image Colorization** | Convolutional AE | $\mathcal{L} = \| \mathbf{x}^{RGB} - g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}^{L})) \|_2^2$ | $d_z = d_x^{L}$ | RGB Image |
| 4 | **Image Generation** | Variational AE | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_{q}[\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})] - \mathrm{KL}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) \| p(\mathbf{z}))$ | $d_z \ll d_x$ | New $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}$ |
| 5 | **Anomaly Detection** | Vanilla / Sparse AE | $\text{score}(\mathbf{x}) = \| \mathbf{x} - g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})) \|_2^2$ | $d_z \ll d_x$ | Anomaly Score |
| 6 | **Dimensionality Reduction** | Undercomplete AE | Same as Vanilla AE | $d_z \ll d_x$ | 2-D / 3-D embedding |
| 7 | **Recommendation** | Variational / Multi-VAE | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{(u,i)} \log \hat{r}_{ui}$ | Latent user/item | Predicted ratings |
| 8 | **Image Inpainting** | Convolutional AE | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}}$ on masked region | Convolutional latent | Completed image |

## 2.4 Detailed Analysis of the Top 4 KTU-Focused Applications

### 2.4.1 Image Denoising — Denoising Autoencoder (DAE)

A DAE is trained on **corrupted–clean pairs** $(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{x})$. The encoder learns to **map a noisy input back to the manifold of clean data**, thereby implicitly learning the *data distribution* $p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})$.

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I})
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - g_{\theta}\bigl(f_{\phi}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)})\bigr) \right\|_2^2
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Medical imaging (MRI/CT denoising with reduced radiation dose), low-light photography, audio signal restoration in hearing aids, seismic data cleanup.

### 2.4.2 Super-Resolution — Convolutional Autoencoder

Maps a low-resolution image $\mathbf{x}^{LR} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ to a high-resolution $\mathbf{x}^{HR} \in \mathbb{R}^{sH \times sW \times C}$ (where $s$ is the upscaling factor). Uses strided convolutions in the encoder and transposed convolutions in the decoder.

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{SR}} = \| \mathbf{x}^{HR} - g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}^{LR})) \|_2^2 + \alpha \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{perceptual}}
$$

The perceptual loss uses a pre-trained VGG network to compare high-level features, giving sharper outputs.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Satellite imaging, surveillance footage enhancement, medical ultrasound upscaling, restoring archival film footage.

### 2.4.3 Image Generation — Variational Autoencoder (VAE)

The VAE replaces the deterministic latent with a **probabilistic** latent. The encoder outputs parameters $(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}^2)$ of a Gaussian posterior $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}\bigl(\mathbf{z}; \boldsymbol{\mu}_\phi(\mathbf{x}), \boldsymbol{\sigma}^2_\phi(\mathbf{x})\bigr)$. The objective is the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)**:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}\bigl[ \log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z}) \bigr]}_{\text{Reconstruction term}} - \underbrace{\mathrm{KL}\bigl( q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) \,\|\, p(\mathbf{z}) \bigr)}_{\text{Regularization term}}
$$

with $p(\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$. Sampling uses the **reparameterization trick**:

$$
\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Synthetic medical data augmentation (CT/MRI scans for rare diseases), drug molecule generation, fashion design, voice synthesis, data augmentation when real samples are scarce.

### 2.4.4 Anomaly Detection

Train on **normal data only**. At inference, an anomaly is any sample whose reconstruction error exceeds a threshold $\tau$.

$$
\text{Anomaly Score}(\mathbf{x}) = \| \mathbf{x} - g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(\mathbf{x})) \|_2^2
$$

$$
\text{Label}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{cases} \text{Normal} & \text{if } \text{Anomaly Score}(\mathbf{x}) < \tau \\ \text{Anomaly} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Credit card fraud detection, manufacturing defect identification, network intrusion detection, predictive maintenance on industrial IoT sensors.

## 2.5 The Three Pillars of Practical Success in Autoencoder Applications

1. **Bottleneck Sizing** — Too small $\rightarrow$ underfitting; too large $\rightarrow$ identity function (no learning). Rule of thumb: $d_z \approx d_x / 4$ to $d_x / 10$.
2. **Loss Selection** — MSE for continuous, BCE for binary-normalized, perceptual + adversarial for photorealistic generation.
3. **Regularization** — Sparsity, contractive, or variational penalties ensure $\mathbf{z}$ is **meaningful** rather than just a compressed copy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides the exhaustive mathematical derivations that examiners expect for full marks on a 14-mark question. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation of the VAE Training Objective (ELBO)

### Step 1 — Start from the log-likelihood of one data point

For a single sample $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$, we want to maximize $\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$. Introduce an arbitrary distribution $q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$:

$$
\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = \log \int p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}) \, d\mathbf{z}
$$

### Step 2 — Multiply and divide by $q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$

$$
\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = \log \int q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) \, \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}^{(i)})} \, d\mathbf{z}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Jensen's Inequality

Because $\log$ is concave, for any distribution $q$:

$$
\log \mathbb{E}_{q}\!\left[ \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}^{(i)})} \right] \geq \mathbb{E}_{q}\!\left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}^{(i)})} \right]
$$

Therefore:

$$
\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}^{(i)})}\!\left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}^{(i)})} \right] \equiv \text{ELBO}^{(i)}
$$

### Step 4 — Expand the joint into conditional × prior

Since $p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}) = p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\vert\mathbf{z}) \, p(\mathbf{z})$:

$$
\text{ELBO}^{(i)} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}\!\left[ \log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\vert\mathbf{z}) \right] - \mathrm{KL}\!\left( q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) \,\|\, p(\mathbf{z}) \right)
$$

### Step 5 — Reparameterization trick for differentiable sampling

To backpropagate through the stochastic node $\mathbf{z}$:

$$
\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_\phi(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_\phi(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})
$$

This separates the stochasticity ($\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$) from the network parameters ($\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}$), enabling gradient flow.

### Step 6 — Closed-form KL for diagonal Gaussians

When $q_\phi = \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}^2)$ and $p(\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$:

$$
\mathrm{KL}(q_\phi \| p) = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{d_z} \left( 1 + \log \sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2 \right)
$$

### Step 7 — Monte Carlo estimate of the reconstruction term

Using a single sample $\mathbf{z}^{(l)} \sim q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}\!\left[ \log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\vert\mathbf{z}) \right] \approx \frac{1}{L} \sum_{l=1}^{L} \log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\vert\mathbf{z}^{(l)})
$$

With $L=1$ (standard practice for large datasets) and Gaussian decoder:

$$
\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\vert\mathbf{z}) = -\frac{1}{2} \left\| \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - g_\theta(\mathbf{z}) \right\|_2^2 + \text{const}
$$

### Step 8 — Final VAE loss to minimize

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}}^{(i)} = \frac{1}{2} \left\| \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - g_\theta(\mathbf{z}^{(l)}) \right\|_2^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{d_z} \left( \mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - \log \sigma_j^2 - 1 \right)
$$

This is the **Negative ELBO** — the loss actually minimized by gradient descent.

## 3.2 Derivation of Sparse Autoencoder Sparsity Penalty

### Step 1 — Define average activation of hidden unit $j$

$$
\hat{\rho}_j = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} h_j(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), \quad \text{where } h_j(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_e^{(j)} \mathbf{x} + b_e^{(j)})
$$

### Step 2 — Force $\hat{\rho}_j \approx \rho$ (a small number, e.g., 0.05)

The KL divergence between two Bernoulli random variables with means $\rho$ and $\hat{\rho}_j$:

$$
\Omega(\mathbf{z}) = \sum_{j=1}^{d_z} \mathrm{KL}\bigl(\rho \,\|\, \hat{\rho}_j\bigr) = \sum_{j=1}^{d_z} \left[ \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1-\rho) \log \frac{1-\rho}{1-\hat{\rho}_j} \right]
$$

### Step 3 — Total sparse AE loss

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{SAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - g_\theta(f_\phi(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})) \right\|_2^2 + \beta \sum_{j=1}^{d_z} \mathrm{KL}(\rho \| \hat{\rho}_j)
$$

This forces most hidden units to be inactive for any given input, producing **distributed, disentangled features**.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Anomaly Detection Threshold

Suppose we train a Vanilla AE on 1000 normal sensor readings. After training, the reconstruction errors on the **training set** are:

$$
\{0.012, 0.018, 0.009, 0.022, 0.015, \ldots\}
$$

### Step 3.3.1 Compute mean and standard deviation

$$
\mu_{\text{err}} = \frac{1}{1000} \sum_{i=1}^{1000} \text{err}_i = 0.015
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{err}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{999} \sum_{i=1}^{1000} (\text{err}_i - \mu_{\text{err}})^2} = 0.004
$$

### Step 3.3.2 Set the anomaly threshold (3-sigma rule)

$$
\tau = \mu_{\text{err}} + 3 \sigma_{\text{err}} = 0.015 + 3 \times 0.004 = 0.027
$$

Any new sample with $\|\mathbf{x} - g_\theta(f_\phi(\mathbf{x}))\|_2^2 > 0.027$ is flagged as an **anomaly**.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Autoencoder for Which Application?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Quick-Reference Matrix — Use this in any KTU essay answer.**

| Application | Input Domain | Best Variant | Loss Function | Latent Type | Typical $d_z$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Image Denoising | RGB / Gray image | Denoising AE | MSE | Deterministic | Same as input |
| Super-Resolution | LR image | Convolutional AE | MSE + Perceptual | Deterministic | Same as LR feature map |
| Image Colorization | Grayscale image | Convolutional AE | MSE on $L$ channel | Deterministic | Same as $L$ feature map |
| Image Generation | Random $\mathbf{z}$ | Variational AE | ELBO (negative) | Stochastic Gaussian | 32 – 256 |
| Anomaly Detection | Tabular / Sensor | Vanilla / Sparse | MSE | Deterministic | $d_x / 4$ to $d_x / 10$ |
| Recommendation | User–Item matrix | Mult-VAE | Negative log-likelihood | Stochastic | 32 – 128 |
| Dimensionality Reduction | Tabular | Undercomplete AE | MSE | Deterministic | 2 or 3 for visualization |
| Image Inpainting | Masked image | Convolutional AE | MSE on masked region | Deterministic | Same as feature map |
| Time-Series Forecasting | Sequential signal | LSTM-AE | MSE | Deterministic | 16 – 64 |
| Semantic Hashing | Text / Image | Deep Hashing AE | BCE + quantization | Binary {-1,+1} | 32 – 128 bits |

## 3.5 Detailed Loss-Function Flowchart for AE Applications

$$
\text{Application} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Choose Variant} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Choose Loss} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Choose Regularizer}
$$

Concretely:

$$
\text{Anomaly Detection} \;\to\; \text{Vanilla AE} \;\to\; \mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} \;\to\; \text{None (rely on undercomplete bottleneck)}
$$

$$
\text{Generation} \;\to\; \text{VAE} \;\to\; \mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} \;\to\; \mathrm{KL}\bigl(q_\phi \| p\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{Denoising} \;\to\; \text{DAE} \;\to\; \mathcal{L}\bigl(\mathbf{x}, g_\theta(f_\phi(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}))\bigr) \;\to\; \text{None}
$$

$$
\text{Super-Resolution} \;\to\; \text{Conv AE} \;\to\; \mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} + \alpha \mathcal{L}_{\text{VGG}} \;\to\; \text{None}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are compiled under the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** and **Label Formatting Restriction** — purely alphanumeric node IDs, raw uppercase labels inside double quotes, no markdown formatting inside labels.

## 4.1 Master Architecture — Generic Autoencoder

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["INPUT X DIM DX"] --> nodeB["ENCODER LAYER 1 DENSE OR CONV"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["ENCODER LAYER 2 DENSE OR CONV"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["BOTTLENECK LATENT Z DIM DZ"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["DECODER LAYER 1 DENSE OR TRANSPOSE CONV"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["DECODER LAYER 2 DENSE OR TRANSPOSE CONV"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["OUTPUT X HAT DIM DX"]
    nodeG -.-> nodeH["LOSS FUNCTION L X X HAT"]
    nodeC -.-> nodeH
    nodeH -.-> nodeB
    nodeH -.-> nodeE
```

## 4.2 Denoising Autoencoder — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    rawX["CLEAN IMAGE X"] --> corrupt["CORRUPTION OPERATOR NOISE OR MASK"]
    corrupt --> tildeX["CORRUPTED INPUT TILDE X"]
    tildeX --> enc1["ENCODER F PHI"]
    enc1 --> zLat["LATENT Z DENOISED REPRESENTATION"]
    zLat --> dec1["DECODER G THETA"]
    dec1 --> xHat["RECONSTRUCTION X HAT"]
    xHat --> loss["MSE LOSS BETWEEN X HAT AND CLEAN X"]
    rawX --> loss
    loss --> upd["GRADIENT UPDATE PHI AND THETA"]
    upd -.-> enc1
    upd -.-> dec1
```

## 4.3 Variational Autoencoder — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    xIn["INPUT X"] --> encLin["ENCODER SHARED BACKBONE"]
    encLin --> muHead["MU HEAD LINEAR"]
    encLin --> sigmaHead["LOG SIGMA HEAD LINEAR"]
    muHead --> muNode["MU PHI X"]
    sigmaHead --> sigmaNode["SIGMA PHI X"]
    eps["EPSILON STANDARD GAUSSIAN NOISE"] --> reparam["REPARAMETERIZATION Z MU PLUS SIGMA TIMES EPSILON"]
    muNode --> reparam
    sigmaNode --> reparam
    reparam --> zSample["LATENT Z SAMPLED"]
    zSample --> decNode["DECODER G THETA"]
    decNode --> xRecon["RECONSTRUCTION X HAT"]
    xRecon --> recLoss["RECONSTRUCTION TERM E Q LOG P X GIVEN Z"]
    muNode --> klLoss["KL DIVERGENCE TERM KL Q PHI Z GIVEN X PARALLEL TO P Z"]
    sigmaNode --> klLoss
    recLoss --> elbo["ELBO COMBINED LOSS"]
    klLoss --> elbo
    elbo --> backprop["BACKPROPAGATION THROUGH REPARAMETERIZATION"]
    backprop -.-> encLin
    backprop -.-> decNode
```

## 4.4 Super-Resolution Convolutional Autoencoder — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    lrIn["LOW RESOLUTION INPUT X LR"] --> conv1["CONV2D STRIDE 2 DOWNSAMPLE FEATURES"]
    conv1 --> conv2["CONV2D STRIDE 2 DEEP FEATURES"]
    conv2 --> bott["BOTTLENECK LATENT FEATURE MAP"]
    bott --> tconv1["CONV2D TRANSPOSE STRIDE 2 UPSAMPLE"]
    tconv1 --> tconv2["CONV2D TRANSPOSE STRIDE 2 FINAL UPSAMPLE"]
    tconv2 --> hrOut["HIGH RESOLUTION OUTPUT X HR"]
    hrOut --> mseL["PIXEL WISE MSE LOSS"]
    hrTarget["GROUND TRUTH X HR"] --> mseL
    mseL -.-> conv1
    mseL -.-> tconv1
```

## 4.5 Anomaly Detection Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    trainX["TRAINING DATA NORMAL ONLY"] --> trainEnc["TRAIN VANILLA OR SPARSE AE"]
    trainEnc --> trained["TRAINED MODEL F PHI G THETA"]
    trained --> inf["INFERENCE NEW INPUT X"]
    inf --> recErr["RECONSTRUCTION ERROR EQUALS X MINUS G F X NORM SQUARED"]
    recErr --> thr["COMPARE WITH THRESHOLD TAU"]
    thr --> normal["NORMAL IF BELOW TAU"]
    thr --> anomaly["ANOMALY IF ABOVE TAU"]
```

## 4.6 Application-to-Variant Decision Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["KTU APPLICATION TASK"]
    start --> q1["NEED NEW SAMPLES GENERATION"]
    start --> q2["NEED CLEAN SIGNAL FROM NOISY INPUT"]
    start --> q3["NEED UPSCALED OR RESTORED IMAGE"]
    start --> q4["NEED OUTLIER OR FRAUD FLAGS"]
    start --> q5["NEED LOW DIM VISUALIZATION"]

    q1 --> vae["VAE VARIATIONAL AUTOENCODER"]
    q2 --> dae["DAE DENOISING AUTOENCODER"]
    q3 --> scae["CONVOLUTIONAL AE"]
    q4 --> svae["VANILLA OR SPARSE AE"]
    q5 --> ucae["UNDERCOMPLETE VANILLA AE"]

    vae --> outELBO["ELBO LOSS"]
    dae --> outMSE["MSE ON CLEAN TARGET"]
    scae --> outPERC["MSE PLUS PERCEPTUAL VGG LOSS"]
    svae --> outMSE2["MSE WITH POSSIBLE SPARSITY PENALTY"]
    ucae --> outMSE3["STANDARD MSE"]
```

## 4.7 Encoder-Decoder Symmetry — Detailed Architecture View

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ENCODER
        e1["INPUT LAYER DX NEURONS"]
        e2["HIDDEN LAYER 1 H1 NEURONS RELU"]
        e3["HIDDEN LAYER 2 H2 NEURONS RELU"]
        e4["BOTTLENECK DZ NEURONS"]
    end
    subgraph DECODER
        d1["BOTTLENECK DZ NEURONS"]
        d2["HIDDEN LAYER 3 H2 NEURONS RELU"]
        d3["HIDDEN LAYER 4 H1 NEURONS RELU"]
        d4["OUTPUT LAYER DX NEURONS SIGMOID"]
    end
    e1 --> e2
    e2 --> e3
    e3 --> e4

    d1 --> d2
    d2 --> d3
    d3 --> d4
```
