<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Explain in brief the role of an autoencoder in image denoising. Mention the variant used and the loss function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Denoising Autoencoder (DAE)** is trained to reconstruct the **clean** image $\mathbf{x}$ from a **corrupted** version $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$, where $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ is typically Gaussian noise.

The encoder $f_\phi$ maps $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ to a latent representation $\mathbf{z}$ that captures the underlying clean structure, while the decoder $g_\theta$ reconstructs the clean image $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ from $\mathbf{z}$.

The loss function used is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - g_\theta\!\left(f_\phi(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)})\right) \right\|_2^2
$$

**Application context (1 mark):** Used in medical imaging (low-dose CT denoising), low-light photography, and audio restoration.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series A]`

**List any three applications of autoencoders and specify the variant used in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Application | Variant |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Image Denoising | Denoising Autoencoder (DAE) |
| 2 | Image Generation / Synthesis | Variational Autoencoder (VAE) |
| 3 | Anomaly / Fraud Detection | Vanilla Undercomplete Autoencoder |

(Each correctly matched entry carries **1 mark**.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme regulations**, every Module-End ESE question carries **internal choice**. Two full alternative 14-mark questions (A and B) are provided below.

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Module 4 / 14 Marks]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)**. Derive its training objective (ELBO) and explain the reparameterization trick. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe how a VAE is used for **image generation**. Compare VAE with a standard autoencoder in terms of latent space structure and sampling capability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Architecture (2 marks):** A VAE consists of:

- An **encoder** $f_\phi$ that outputs two vectors: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_\phi(\mathbf{x})$ and $\log \boldsymbol{\sigma}^2_\phi(\mathbf{x})$, the parameters of a Gaussian posterior $q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$.
- A **latent space** $\mathbf{z}$ sampled from $\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}^2)$.
- A **decoder** $g_\theta$ that generates $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ from $\mathbf{z}$.

(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.3.)

**ELBO Derivation (4 marks):**

Start with the log-marginal likelihood:

$$
\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \log \int p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) \, d\mathbf{z}
$$

Introduce $q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$:

$$
\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \log \int q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})} d\mathbf{z}
$$

Apply Jensen's inequality:

$$
\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})}\!\left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})} \right]
$$

Expand using $p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = p_\theta(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z})$:

$$
\text{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}\!\left[ \log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z}) \right] - \mathrm{KL}\!\left( q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) \,\|\, p(\mathbf{z}) \right)
$$

where $p(\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$.

**Reparameterization Trick (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\sigma}_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})
$$

This separates randomness from parameters, allowing gradient backpropagation through the sampling step.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**VAE for Image Generation (3 marks):**

1. Train the VAE on a dataset of images (e.g., CelebA faces).
2. After training, the latent space is regularized to approximate $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$.
3. To generate a new image, **sample** $\mathbf{z}_{\text{new}} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$.
4. Pass through decoder: $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} = g_\theta(\mathbf{z}_{\text{new}})$.

**Comparison with Standard Autoencoder (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Standard AE | VAE |
|---|---|---|
| Latent Representation | Deterministic point $\mathbf{z} = f_\phi(\mathbf{x})$ | Stochastic distribution $q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x})$ |
| Latent Space Structure | Discontinuous, irregular gaps | **Continuous, smooth**, suitable for sampling |
| Regularization | None (or weight decay) | KL-divergence to $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ |
| Generative Capability | **Cannot generate new samples** | **Yes**, by sampling $\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ |
| Loss | MSE or BCE | ELBO (negative) |
| Training Stability | Easy | Requires reparameterization for stable gradients |

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Module 4 / 14 Marks]`**

**(a)** Explain **Image Denoising** and **Super-Resolution** as two major applications of Convolutional Autoencoders. Discuss the architecture and loss function used in each case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe how an autoencoder can be used for **anomaly detection** in industrial IoT sensor data. Derive the thresholding criterion and explain the role of the reconstruction error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Image Denoising with Convolutional DAE (3.5 marks):**

A **Convolutional Denoising Autoencoder (C-DAE)** takes a noisy image $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ (e.g., a CT scan corrupted by Poisson noise) and outputs a clean reconstruction $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$.

**Architecture:**
- **Encoder:** stack of `Conv2D(stride=2) → BatchNorm → ReLU` blocks that downsample spatial dimensions while increasing channel depth.
- **Bottleneck:** compressed feature map with high semantic content.
- **Decoder:** stack of `Conv2DTranspose(stride=2) → BatchNorm → ReLU` blocks that upsample to the original resolution.
- **Output layer:** `Conv2D(3, kernel=1) → Sigmoid` for RGB reconstruction.

**Loss Function:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - g_\theta\!\left(f_\phi(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)})\right) \right\|_2^2
$$

where $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)} = \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}^{(i)}$ with $\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I})$.

**Super-Resolution with Convolutional AE (3.5 marks):**

**Architecture:**
- Input: low-resolution $\mathbf{x}^{LR} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$.
- Encoder: convolutional feature extractor.
- Decoder: a series of **sub-pixel convolution** (or transposed convolution) layers that upsample by factor $s$.
- Output: high-resolution $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{HR} \in \mathbb{R}^{sH \times sW \times C}$.

**Loss Function:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{SR}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \mathbf{x}^{HR,(i)} - g_\theta\!\left(f_\phi(\mathbf{x}^{LR,(i)})\right) \right\|_2^2 + \alpha \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{VGG}}\!\left( \mathbf{x}^{HR,(i)}, \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{HR,(i)} \right)
$$

The **VGG perceptual loss** compares deep feature activations from a pre-trained VGG-16 network, producing visually sharper outputs than pure pixel-wise MSE.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Anomaly Detection Pipeline (4 marks):**

1. **Training phase:** Collect **only normal** operating data $\mathcal{D}_{\text{normal}} = \{\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ from the IoT sensors (e.g., vibration, temperature, current).
2. Train an undercomplete Vanilla Autoencoder by minimizing:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{AE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - g_\theta(f_\phi(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})) \right\|_2^2
$$

The bottleneck $d_z \ll d_x$ forces the network to learn the **manifold of normal behavior**.

3. **Inference phase:** For a new sensor reading $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}$, compute the **reconstruction error**:

$$
e(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}) = \left\| \mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} - g_\theta(f_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}})) \right\|_2^2
$$

**Thresholding Criterion (3 marks):**

Compute mean and standard deviation of reconstruction errors on the **validation set** (normal data only):

$$
\mu_e = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} e(\mathbf{x}_{\text{val}}^{(j)}), \qquad \sigma_e = \sqrt{\frac{1}{M-1} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \left( e(\mathbf{x}_{\text{val}}^{(j)}) - \mu_e \right)^2}
$$

Set the anomaly threshold using the **3-sigma rule**:

$$
\tau = \mu_e + k \cdot \sigma_e, \quad \text{typically } k \in \{2, 3\}
$$

**Decision rule:**

$$
\text{Label}(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}) = \begin{cases} \text{Normal} & \text{if } e(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}) \leq \tau \\ \textbf{Anomaly} & \text{if } e(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}) > \tau \end{cases}
$$

**Why this works:** Anomalies deviate from the learned normal manifold. The autoencoder, having never seen such deviations, **cannot reconstruct them faithfully**, producing a high reconstruction error.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing AE with PCA (2-mark penalty):** AEs are **non-linear**; PCA is **linear**. Always emphasize the non-linear activations (ReLU, Sigmoid).
> 2. **Forgetting the reparameterization trick in VAE answers (1–2 marks lost):** Examiners explicitly test whether you know *why* direct sampling breaks backpropagation.
> 3. **Writing "MSE loss" for VAE (1 mark lost):** VAE uses **ELBO** (or its negative), which contains both a reconstruction term AND a KL term. Writing only MSE is incomplete.
> 4. **Skipping the threshold formula in anomaly detection (1 mark lost):** Always derive $\tau = \mu_e + k\sigma_e$ explicitly with the $k$-sigma justification.
> 5. **No diagram in 14-mark answers (1–2 marks lost):** Always include a labelled block diagram of the encoder–decoder pipeline.
> 6. **Confusing DAE corruption with input noise in training (1 mark lost):** The input is corrupted, but the **target is the clean** image $\mathbf{x}$. Writing the loss on $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ is incorrect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Applications of Autoencoders**

- An **autoencoder** reconstructs its input through a **bottleneck** latent $\mathbf{z}$, forcing the network to learn compressed representations.
- The **fundamental components** are: encoder $f_\phi$, latent code $\mathbf{z}$, decoder $g_\theta$, and reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \hat{\mathbf{x}})$.
- **Vanilla / Undercomplete AE** is used for **dimensionality reduction and compression** when $d_z < d_x$.
- **Denoising AE (DAE)** is trained with **corrupted input $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$** and **clean target $\mathbf{x}$**; applied in medical imaging, low-light photography.
- **Convolutional AE** uses **Conv2D** in encoder and **Conv2DTranspose** in decoder; used for super-resolution, colorization, and inpainting.
- **Super-Resolution** loss = pixel MSE + perceptual (VGG) loss for sharp outputs.
- **Variational AE (VAE)** replaces the deterministic latent with a Gaussian distribution $q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}^2)$ trained via the **ELBO**.
- **ELBO** = reconstruction term − KL divergence to prior $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$.
- **Reparameterization trick** = $\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\sigma} \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ with $\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ — enables backpropagation through sampling.
- **VAE generates new images** by sampling $\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ and decoding, unlike a standard AE which cannot.
- **Sparse AE** uses KL-divergence sparsity penalty $\Omega(\mathbf{z}) = \sum_j \mathrm{KL}(\rho \| \hat{\rho}_j)$ to learn disentangled features.
- **Anomaly detection** uses reconstruction error $\|\mathbf{x} - g_\theta(f_\phi(\mathbf{x}))\|_2^2$ with threshold $\tau = \mu_e + k\sigma_e$.
- **Image colorization** trains on grayscale → RGB reconstruction.
- **Image inpainting** trains with masked inputs and computes MSE only on the masked region.
- **Recommendation systems** use **Multi-VAE** with multinomial likelihood on user-item interactions.
- **Recurrent AE (LSTM-AE)** handles sequential data — time-series forecasting and ECG anomaly detection.
- **Contractive AE** adds $\|\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f_\phi(\mathbf{x})\|_F^2$ penalty for local robustness.
- **Standard loss functions**: MSE (continuous), BCE (binary-normalized), ELBO (probabilistic).
- **Engineering applications** span medical imaging, satellite imagery, finance, IoT, NLP, and pharmaceutical research.
- **Always include a labelled diagram** in 14-mark KTU answers showing encoder → latent → decoder flow.
- **Always cite the specific variant** (DAE / VAE / Sparse AE / Convolutional AE) when discussing an application.
- **Threshold $k$** in anomaly detection is typically 2 or 3 — mention the trade-off (higher $k$ → fewer false positives, more false negatives).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generative Models: Generative Adversarial Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Generative Models: Generative Adversarial Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** are a class of deep generative models introduced by Ian Goodfellow et al. (2014) that learn to produce synthetic samples indistinguishable from real data by framing the learning task as a **two-player zero-sum minimax game** between two neural networks trained simultaneously.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme notation for **PECDT695 - Deep Learning**, a GAN consists of two differentiable functions (typically parameterized as deep neural networks):

| Symbol | Role | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **G** | **Generator** — maps noise $z \sim p_z(z)$ to a fake sample $G(z)$ | $G(z; \theta_g)$ |
| **D** | **Discriminator** — estimates the probability that a sample came from the real data rather than from G | $D(x; \theta_d)$ |
| $p_{data}(x)$ | True (real) data distribution | Unknown target |
| $p_z(z)$ | Prior noise distribution (usually $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ or $\mathcal{U}(-1, 1)$) | Known and sampleable |
| $p_g(x)$ | Implicit generator distribution induced by $G$ | $p_g(x) = p_z(z) \cdot \vert \det(J_G)\vert^{-1}$ |

The networks are trained with the **adversarial loss** in Equation (1).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Forger and the Detective

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine a **counterfeit currency forger** (Generator **G**) and a **detective** (Discriminator **D**):
> - The **forger** prints fake currency from random sketches (noise $z$) and tries to pass it as real money.
> - The **detective** is shown both real and fake bills and must decide which is genuine.
> - Initially, the forger's work is clumsy and the detective easily catches fakes. But the forger learns from every failure. As the detective gets sharper, the forger must improve — and vice versa.
> - At **equilibrium (Nash equilibrium)**, the forger's fakes are so perfect that the detective can do no better than a **coin flip (50% accuracy)**.
> - At that point, the forger has effectively learned $p_{data}(x)$ without ever seeing the explicit density.

This **adversarial pressure** is what allows GANs to produce remarkably sharp, photorealistic images — sharper than VAEs and likelihood-based generative models.

---

## 1.3 Key Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 4 High-Yield Terms to Memorize**
> - **Adversarial training** — simultaneous SGD on two networks with opposing objectives.
> - **Value function $V(D, G)$** — the scalar objective whose saddle point is sought.
> - **Nash equilibrium** — the fixed point where neither $G$ nor $D$ can improve unilaterally.
> - **Mode collapse** — failure mode where $G$ produces limited varieties of samples.
> - **Vanishing gradients** — failure mode that occurs when $D$ becomes too strong early in training.
> - **Saturating vs. non-saturating loss** — two formulations to handle the log$(1 - D(G(z)))$ saturation problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Adversarial Value Function

The original Goodfellow GAN is defined by the **minimax game** in Equation (1):

$$
\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]
$$

This compact equation encodes the entire GAN training philosophy. The expanded form (after applying the change of variable $x = G(z)$) is:

$$
V(D, G) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log D(x)\, dx + \int_{z} p_z(z) \log\bigl(1 - D(G(z))\bigr)\, dz
$$

$$
= \int_{x} \Bigl[ p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log\bigl(1 - D(x)\bigr) \Bigr] dx
$$

---

## 2.2 The Discriminator's Optimization (Inner Max)

For a **fixed generator $G$**, the optimal discriminator is obtained by maximizing $V(D, G)$ with respect to $D(x)$ pointwise. The integrand for any $x$ is:

$$
f(D) = p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))
$$

Setting the derivative $\partial f / \partial D = 0$:

$$
\frac{p_{data}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}
$$

At equilibrium, when $p_g = p_{data}$, we get $D^{*}(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ everywhere — the discriminator is completely fooled.

---

## 2.3 The Generator's Optimization (Outer Min)

Substituting $D^{*}(x)$ back into $V$:

$$
\max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}} \!\left[ \log \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g} \!\left[ \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} \right]
$$

This is the **negative log-4 plus twice the Jensen–Shannon Divergence (JSD)** between $p_{data}$ and $p_g$:

$$
\max_{D} V(D, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot D_{JS}\!\left(p_{data} \,\|\, p_g\right)
$$

Since $G$ minimizes this and the JSD is non-negative, the **global optimum is $p_g = p_{data}$**, giving $C^{*} = -\log 4$.

---

## 2.4 The Non-Saturating Heuristic

Early in training, $D$ easily rejects fake samples, so $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ and the gradient of $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ with respect to $\theta_g$ vanishes (saturates). The standard fix used in practice is the **non-saturating GAN (NS-GAN) loss** for $G$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_G^{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}\bigl[\log D(G(z))\bigr]
$$

This provides strong gradients in the early phase and yields the **same fixed point** as the minimax loss.

---

## 2.5 Other Key Formulas (Wasserstein, LSGAN)

| Loss Family | Discriminator Loss $\mathcal{L}_D$ | Generator Loss $\mathcal{L}_G$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Vanilla GAN (minimax)** | $-[\log D(x) + \log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ |
| **Non-Saturating GAN** | Same as above | $-\log D(G(z))$ |
| **Least-Squares GAN (LSGAN)** | $\tfrac{1}{2}(D(x)-1)^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}D(G(z))^2$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}(D(G(z)) - 1)^2$ |
| **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** | $\mathbb{E}[\,D(G(z))\,] - \mathbb{E}[\,D(x)\,]$ | $-\mathbb{E}[\,D(G(z))\,]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why WGAN matters for KTU:** The **Wasserstein-1 distance** $W(p_{data}, p_g) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(p_{data},p_g)} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim \gamma}[\,\|x-y\|\,]$ provides a **continuous, everywhere-differentiable** training signal even when supports of $p_{data}$ and $p_g$ are disjoint — fixing the vanishing-gradient problem of vanilla GANs.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for the KTU University Exam.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Where it appears |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GAN minimax value** | $\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$ | Definition / 14-mark derivations |
| 2 | **Optimal discriminator** | $D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$ | Proof of equilibrium |
| 3 | **Value at optimum** | $C^{*} = -\log 4 \approx -1.386$ | Sanity check |
| 4 | **JSD equivalence** | $\max_D V = -\log 4 + 2\,D_{JS}(p_{data}\Vert p_g)$ | Links GANs to information theory |
| 5 | **KL divergence** | $D_{KL}(P\Vert Q) = \int P(x) \log\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\,dx$ | Used in derivation |
| 6 | **Jensen–Shannon Divergence** | $D_{JS}(P\Vert Q) = \tfrac{1}{2}D_{KL}(P\Vert M) + \tfrac{1}{2}D_{KL}(Q\Vert M)$, $M=\tfrac{1}{2}(P+Q)$ | Information-theoretic interpretation |
| 7 | **Wasserstein-1 distance** | $W(p_{data}, p_g) = \inf_{\gamma} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\gamma}[\Vert x - y \Vert]$ | WGAN definition |
| 8 | **Lipschitz constraint (WGAN)** | $\Vert D \Vert_L \leq 1$ | Enforced by weight clipping / gradient penalty |
| 9 | **Non-saturating $G$ loss** | $\mathcal{L}_G^{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_z[\log D(G(z))]$ | Practical training |
| 10 | **Mode collapse indicator** | $\text{Var}_{z}[G(z)] \to 0$ while $\text{Var}_{x \sim p_{data}}[x] > 0$ | Failure diagnosis |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

GANs are the workhorse of modern **synthetic data generation** in:
- **Medical imaging** — synthesizing MRI / CT slices to augment scarce datasets.
- **Autonomous driving simulation** — generating photorealistic driving scenes.
- **Drug discovery** — proposing novel molecular structures.
- **Super-resolution and image inpainting** — recovering missing high-frequency content.
- **Voice cloning and text-to-image synthesis** — modern foundation-model pipelines (Stable Diffusion, DALL·E use diffusion + GAN hybrids).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Discriminator for Fixed Generator

**Given:** $G$ is fixed, $p_g(x)$ is the induced distribution. We maximize $V(D, G)$:

$$
V(D, G) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log D(x)\, dx + \int_{x} p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\, dx
$$

**Step 1:** For each $x$, take the derivative of the integrand $f(D) = p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))$ with respect to $D(x)$:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial D} = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)}
$$

**Step 2:** Set the derivative to zero and solve for $D(x)$:

$$
\frac{p_{data}(x)}{D(x)} = \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; p_{data}(x)\,(1 - D(x)) = p_g(x)\,D(x)
$$

$$
p_{data}(x) = D(x)\,(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}
$$

**Step 3:** Verify this is a **maximum** by second derivative:

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial D^{2}} = -\frac{p_{data}(x)}{D^{2}(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{(1 - D(x))^{2}} < 0 \quad \text{(always negative, since } D, p_{data}, p_g \geq 0\text{)}
$$

Hence $D^{*}(x)$ is indeed a global maximum for each $x$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Global Optimum Value $C^{*} = -\log 4$

**Step 1:** Substitute $D^{*}(x)$ back into $V$:

$$
\max_D V(D, G) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}\,dx + \int_{x} p_g(x) \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}\,dx
$$

**Step 2:** Add and subtract $\log 2$ inside each log to introduce the mixture $M = \tfrac{1}{2}(p_{data} + p_g)$:

$$
= \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log \frac{p_{data}(x)}{2 M(x)}\,dx + \int_{x} p_g(x) \log \frac{p_g(x)}{2 M(x)}\,dx
$$

**Step 3:** Split each log using $\log(a/b) = \log a - \log b$:

$$
= \int_{x} p_{data}(x)\bigl[\log p_{data}(x) - \log 2 - \log M(x)\bigr] dx + \int_{x} p_g(x)\bigl[\log p_g(x) - \log 2 - \log M(x)\bigr] dx
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the **KL divergences** $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \int P \log(P/Q)$:

$$
= D_{KL}\!\left(p_{data}\,\Vert\, M\right) + D_{KL}\!\left(p_{g}\,\Vert\, M\right) - 2\log 2 \int_{x}(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x))\,dx
$$

**Step 5:** Since both $p_{data}$ and $p_g$ are valid probability densities, $\int (p_{data} + p_g) = 2$, so the last term is $-2\log 2 \cdot 2 = -4\log 2$.

**Step 6:** Using the JSD definition $D_{JS}(P\Vert Q) = \tfrac{1}{2}D_{KL}(P\Vert M) + \tfrac{1}{2}D_{KL}(Q\Vert M)$:

$$
\max_D V(D, G) = 2 D_{JS}\!\left(p_{data}\,\Vert\, p_g\right) - 4\log 2
$$

$$
= 2 D_{JS}\!\left(p_{data}\,\Vert\, p_g\right) - \log 4
$$

> **Key Result:** $\quad \max_D V(D, G) = -\log 4 + 2\,D_{JS}(p_{data} \Vert p_g)$

**Step 7:** Since $D_{JS} \geq 0$ with equality iff $p_g = p_{data}$, the minimum over $G$ is:

$$
\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = -\log 4 \quad \text{achieved at} \quad p_g = p_{data}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This proves two KTU-favorite facts in one go: **(i) the global optimum has $D^{*}=\tfrac{1}{2}$ everywhere**, and **(ii) the optimal generator distribution equals the true data distribution** — the formal justification that GANs are **consistent** estimators.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Why the Non-Saturating Loss Works

The vanilla $G$ loss $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ saturates when $D(G(z)) \to 0$ (fake samples easily detected), giving near-zero gradient. Cross-entropy minimization pushes the loss to be a function of $D(G(z))$ directly:

$$
\mathcal{L}_G^{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log D(G(z))]
$$

Differentiate with respect to $\theta_g$:

$$
\nabla_{\theta_g} \mathcal{L}_G^{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_{z}\!\left[ \frac{1}{D(G(z))} \nabla_{\theta_g} D(G(z)) \right]
$$

When $D(G(z)) \to 0$ (early training), the factor $1/D(G(z))$ explodes — providing the strong gradient that the vanilla loss lacks. The fixed point $D(G(z)) = 1$ is identical, so the two losses share the same Nash equilibrium.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes of GANs

| Failure Mode | Symptom | Root Cause | Standard Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode collapse** | $G$ outputs nearly identical samples | $G$ finds a single mode that fools $D$ | Minibatch discrimination, unrolled GANs, WGAN |
| **Vanishing gradient (saturating loss)** | $D$ too strong → $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ | $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ saturates | Use non-saturating loss or WGAN |
| **Oscillation / non-convergence** | Loss does not plateau | Adversarial dynamics, no convexity | Smaller learning rates, two-timescale update rule (TTUR) |
| **Discriminator overfit** | $D(x) \to 1$ for all real $x$ | Limited data | Label smoothing, dropout in $D$ |
| **Helmet / texture sticking** | $G$ copies patches from training images | Insufficient inductive bias | Spectral normalization, self-attention, augmentation |

---

## 3.5 Conditional GAN — Extending the Framework

When labels $y$ are available, the value function is extended to:

$$
\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x \mid y)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z, y \sim p_y}[\log(1 - D(G(z \mid y) \mid y))]
$$

This is the foundation of **pix2pix, image-to-image translation, and class-conditional generation**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level GAN Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    noiseP["Noise Prior p_z(z)"] --> genIn["Random vector z"]
    genIn --> generator["Generator Network G(z)"]
    realData["Real Data x ~ p_data(x)"] --> disIn["Inputs to Discriminator"]
    fakeOut["Fake sample G(z)"] --> disIn
    generator --> fakeOut
    disIn --> discriminator["Discriminator Network D(x)"]
    discriminator --> dOut["Probability D(x): real or fake"]
    dOut --> loss1["Adversarial Loss V(D,G)"]
    loss1 --> updateG["Update G via backprop"]
    loss1 --> updateD["Update D via backprop"]
    updateG --> generator
    updateD --> discriminator
```

## 4.2 Training Loop Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA["Step 1: Sample minibatch of m noise samples z_i from p_z(z)"] --> stepB
    startB["Step 2: Sample minibatch of m real samples x_i from p_data"] --> stepB
    stepB["Step 3: Generate m fake samples G(z_i)"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 4: Update Discriminator D by ascending stochastic gradient of V(D,G) with respect to theta_d"]
    stepC --> stepD["Step 5: Update Generator G by descending stochastic gradient of V(D,G) with respect to theta_g"]
    stepD --> check{"Step 6: Converged? D output near 0.5 and stable"}
    check -- No --> startA
    check -- Yes --> endNode["Step 7: Trained Generator ready for sampling"]
```

## 4.3 Generator-Discriminator Adversarial Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Game["Two-Player Zero-Sum Game"]
        direction LR
        G1["Generator G tries to minimize V"] -- produces fakes --> D1["Discriminator D tries to maximize V"]
        D1 -- real vs fake labels --> G1
    end
    Game --> equil["Nash Equilibrium: p_g equals p_data, D(x) equals 0.5 for all x"]
```

## 4.4 Loss Landscape — Vanilla vs. Non-Saturating

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0["D(G(z)) output"] --> B1["Near 0: fake easily detected"]
    A0 --> B2["Near 0.5: equilibrium"]
    A0 --> B3["Near 1: indistinguishable from real"]
    B1 --> C1["Vanilla loss log(1 - D(G(z))) saturates to 0, gradient vanishes"]
    B1 --> C2["Non-saturating loss -log D(G(z)) gradient is large and informative"]
    B3 --> C3["Both losses give strong gradient signal at optimum"]
    B2 --> C4["Both losses near 0 at equilibrium, D outputs 0.5"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] State the GAN minimax objective. Identify the role of the Generator and the Discriminator.

**Model Answer:**

> The **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is defined by the following two-player zero-sum game:
> $$\min_{G}\,\max_{D}\,V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$
>
> - The **Discriminator $D$** is a binary classifier that outputs the probability that a sample $x$ came from the real data distribution $p_{data}$ rather than from the generator. It is trained to **maximize** $V$ — i.e., correctly distinguish real from fake.
> - The **Generator $G$** maps a noise vector $z$ (sampled from a known prior $p_z$, typically Gaussian) to a synthetic sample $G(z)$ that mimics real data. It is trained to **minimize** $V$ — i.e., produce samples that the discriminator misclassifies as real.
>
> At the **Nash equilibrium**, $p_g = p_{data}$ and $D(x) = 0.5$ for every $x$, meaning the discriminator is maximally uncertain and the generator has learned the true data distribution.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Differentiate between a Generative Adversarial Network and a Variational Autoencoder. Mention one limitation of GANs.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **GAN** | **VAE** |
|---|---|---|
| **Training paradigm** | Adversarial (two networks in a minimax game) | Variational inference with KL-regularized ELBO |
| **Loss function** | Adversarial $\log D + \log(1 - D(G(z)))$ | Reconstruction + KL divergence: $\mathcal{L} = -\mathbb{E}_{q(z\mid x)}[\log p(x \mid z)] + D_{KL}(q \Vert p)$ |
| **Sample sharpness** | Very sharp, photorealistic | Tends to be blurry |
| **Explicit density $p_g(x)$** | **No** (implicit) | **Yes** (variational lower bound) |
| **Mode coverage** | Prone to mode collapse | Better mode coverage due to KL term |
| **Inference (latent → data)** | Direct: $x = G(z)$ | Direct: $x = G(z)$ |
| **Inference (data → latent)** | **No** (needs encoder or inversion) | **Yes** (encoder $q(z \mid x)$) |

> **One key limitation of GANs:** They suffer from **mode collapse** — the generator may learn to produce only a narrow subset of the data distribution, fooling the discriminator with a few "safe" samples. Training is also notoriously **unstable** (oscillations, vanishing gradients) because the loss is non-convex in the joint $(D, G)$ parameter space.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the optimal discriminator $D^{*}(x)$ for a fixed generator $G$, and show that the GAN minimax game reduces to minimizing the Jensen–Shannon Divergence between $p_{data}$ and $p_g$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **vanishing gradient problem** of vanilla GANs and how the **non-saturating loss** and **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** address it. Discuss the Lipschitz constraint and gradient penalty. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

> **Step 1 — Set up $V(D, G)$ for fixed $G$:**  
> Treat the integrals over $x$ pointwise, maximizing each integrand independently:
> $$V(D, G) = \int_{x}\!\bigl[p_{data}(x)\log D(x) + p_g(x)\log(1 - D(x))\bigr]dx$$
>
> **Step 2 — Differentiate the integrand $f(D) = p_{data}\log D + p_g\log(1 - D)$:**
> $$\frac{\partial f}{\partial D} = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0$$
>
> **Step 3 — Solve for $D(x)$:**
> $$D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Substitute $D^{*}$ back into $V$** and rewrite using $M = \tfrac{1}{2}(p_{data}+p_g)$:
> $$\max_D V = D_{KL}(p_{data}\Vert M) + D_{KL}(p_g \Vert M) - \log 4$$
>
> **Step 5 — Recognize JSD:** $D_{JS}(P \Vert Q) = \tfrac{1}{2}D_{KL}(P\Vert M) + \tfrac{1}{2}D_{KL}(Q\Vert M)$:
> $$\max_D V(D, G) = -\log 4 + 2\,D_{JS}(p_{data} \Vert p_g)$$
>
> **Step 6 — Generator's objective:** Since $-\log 4$ is constant in $G$, minimizing $V$ over $G$ is equivalent to:
> $$\min_G\, D_{JS}(p_{data} \Vert p_g)$$
> Hence, at the global optimum $p_g = p_{data}$ and $D^{*}(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

> **Step 1 — Why vanilla GAN gradients vanish:**  
> The generator loss is $\mathcal{L}_G = \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$. When $D$ becomes strong early in training, $D(G(z)) \to 0$ for fakes, so $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ saturates at $\log 1 = 0$ with **vanishing derivative**:
> $$\frac{d}{d\theta_g}\log(1 - D(G(z))) = -\frac{1}{1 - D(G(z))}\cdot \nabla_{\theta_g} D(G(z)) \to 0 \;\;\text{as}\;\; D(G(z)) \to 0$$
>
> **Step 2 — Non-saturating loss fix:**  
> Replace with $\mathcal{L}_G^{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_z[\log D(G(z))]$. Now:
> $$\frac{d}{d\theta_g}\bigl[-\log D(G(z))\bigr] = -\frac{1}{D(G(z))}\nabla_{\theta_g}D(G(z))$$
> When $D(G(z)) \to 0$, the prefactor $1/D(G(z))$ **explodes** — strong gradient signal. Same fixed point: $D(G(z)) \to 1$ is still the optimum.
>
> **Step 3 — WGAN: replace JSD with Wasserstein-1:**  
> The Wasserstein-1 distance $W(p_{data}, p_g)$ is **continuous everywhere** and has **non-vanishing gradients** even when $p_{data}$ and $p_g$ have disjoint supports (where JSD is constant and JSD gradient is zero).
>
> **Step 4 — Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality:**  
> $$W(p_{data}, p_g) = \sup_{\Vert f \Vert_L \leq 1}\;\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[f(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g}[f(x)]$$
> The discriminator $D = f$ is required to be **1-Lipschitz**: $\Vert D \Vert_L \leq 1$.
>
> **Step 5 — Enforcing the Lipschitz constraint:**  
> - **Weight clipping:** Clamp $D$'s weights to $[-c, c]$ after each step (Arjovsky et al., 2017).
> - **Gradient penalty (WGAN-GP):** Add a penalty term $\lambda \mathbb{E}_{\hat{x}}\bigl[(\Vert \nabla_{\hat{x}} D(\hat{x}) \Vert_2 - 1)^{2}\bigr]$ where $\hat{x} = \epsilon x + (1-\epsilon) G(z)$.
>
> **Step 6 — WGAN losses:**
> $$\mathcal{L}_D = \mathbb{E}_{z}[D(G(z))] - \mathbb{E}_{x}[D(x)] + \lambda \cdot \text{GP}$$
> $$\mathcal{L}_G = -\mathbb{E}_{z}[D(G(z))]$$
> Unlike vanilla GAN, the WGAN loss correlates with sample quality — a smaller $W$ value means better generation.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023] **(14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the architecture and training procedure of a Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN). List the architectural guidelines specified by Radford et al. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **mode collapse**, **Conditional GANs**, and the **evaluation metrics** (Inception Score and Fréchet Inception Distance) used for GANs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

> **Architecture (DCGAN, Radford et al., 2015):**
> - **Generator $G$:** A stack of transposed convolutions (fractionally-strided convolutions) that upsample a noise vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^{100}$ into a $64 \times 64 \times 3$ image. No fully-connected layers in the main body; only at the input/output projections.
> - **Discriminator $D$:** A standard CNN classifier (strided convolutions) that downsamples the input image to a single scalar probability.
>
> **Architectural guidelines (the "DCGAN rules"):**
> 1. Replace **pooling layers** with **strided convolutions** in $D$ and **fractionally-strided convolutions** in $G$.
> 2. Use **batch normalization** in both $G$ and $D$ (except the last layer of $G$ and first layer of $D$).
> 3. Remove **fully-connected hidden layers**; use global average pooling at the discriminator output.
> 4. Use **ReLU** activation in $G$ for all layers except the output (which uses **Tanh**).
> 5. Use **LeakyReLU** activation in $D$ for all layers.
>
> **Training procedure (Algorithm 1 of Goodfellow 2014, adapted for DCGAN):**
> 1. For each minibatch of size $m$: sample $m$ noise samples $\{z^{(1)}, \dots, z^{(m)}\}$ and $m$ real samples $\{x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(m)}\}$.
> 2. **Update $D$** by ascending the stochastic gradient of:
>    $$\nabla_{\theta_d} \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\!\bigl[\log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))\bigr]$$
> 3. **Update $G$** by descending the stochastic gradient of:
>    $$\nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))$$
>    *(or the non-saturating version $-\log D(G(z^{(i)}))$)*
> 4. Repeat for a fixed number of iterations or until $D$'s output converges to $\approx 0.5$.
>
> **Why these guidelines help:** Strided convolutions let the network learn its own downsampling/upsampling. Batch normalization stabilizes adversarial training. LeakyReLU prevents the discriminator from saturating at zero gradient. Tanh output keeps pixel values in $[-1, 1]$ matching real data normalization.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

> **Mode Collapse:**  
> A pathological failure where the generator collapses to producing a **small number of similar samples**, even though the data distribution has high diversity. It occurs because $G$ finds a few "easy" modes that consistently fool $D$. **Fixes:** minibatch discrimination (allowing $D$ to compare samples within a batch), unrolled GANs (let $G$ see $D$'s future updates), WGAN, and experience replay for $D$.
>
> **Conditional GAN (cGAN):**  
> Both $G$ and $D$ receive an auxiliary conditioning variable $y$ (e.g., a class label, text embedding, or another image):
> $$\min_G \max_D\, V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}[\log D(x \mid y)] + \mathbb{E}_{z,y}[\log(1 - D(G(z \mid y) \mid y))]$$
> Applications: **pix2pix** (image-to-image), **StackGAN** (text-to-image), class-conditional generation, and style transfer.
>
> **Evaluation Metrics:**
> - **Inception Score (IS):** $\text{IS} = \exp\bigl(\mathbb{E}_{x}\bigl[D_{KL}\bigl(p(y \mid x) \,\Vert\, p(y)\bigr)\bigr]\bigr)$  
>   High IS = generated images have confident, diverse class predictions. **Limitation:** insensitive to intra-class mode collapse.
> - **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID):**  
>   Compute the Inception-v3 feature statistics $(\mu_r, \Sigma_r)$ for real images and $(\mu_g, \Sigma_g)$ for generated images. Then:
>   $$\text{FID} = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^{2} + \text{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$$
>   Lower FID = generated distribution closer to real distribution in feature space. **More robust than IS** because it compares full feature distributions and is sensitive to mode collapse.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $D^{*}(x)$ with $D(x)$ at the end of training.** Examiners explicitly check whether you can state: at *equilibrium*, $D^{*}(x) = 0.5$ and $p_g = p_{data}$. Missing this costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up JSD and KL.** The JSD derivation **requires** the mixture $M = \tfrac{1}{2}(p_{data}+p_g)$ and **two** KL terms. Writing only one KL will be marked wrong.
> 3. **Forgetting the Lipschitz constraint in WGAN.** A common 7-mark question. State it explicitly: $\Vert f \Vert_L \leq 1$.
> 4. **Writing $C^{*} = -1.386$ without derivation.** The symbolic result $C^{*} = -\log 4$ is preferred. Mark allocation is for the derivation, not the number.
> 5. **Skipping the second-derivative check** in the optimal discriminator derivation. Always verify the critical point is a *maximum*, not a minimum — a 1-mark sub-step examiners expect.
> 6. **Using $p_g = p_{data}$ without stating the support condition.** A subtle point: $D^{*} = 0.5$ everywhere requires the supports to overlap. WGAN is preferred when they are disjoint.

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PECDT695 Module 4 — GANs**

- ⭐ **Two networks, one game:** $G$ maps $z \to x$ to fool $D$; $D$ classifies real vs. fake. The objective is the **minimax value function**:
  $$\min_G \max_D\, V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$
- ⭐ **Optimal discriminator:** $D^{*}(x) = \dfrac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$
- ⭐ **Equilibrium:** $p_g = p_{data}$ and $D(x) = 0.5$ for all $x$, giving $V^{*} = -\log 4$.
- ⭐ **JSD equivalence:** $\max_D V = -\log 4 + 2\,D_{JS}(p_{data} \Vert p_g)$ — links GANs to information theory.
- ⭐ **Non-saturating loss** fixes early-training gradient vanishing: $\mathcal{L}_G^{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_z[\log D(G(z))]$.
- ⭐ **Wasserstein GAN** uses $W(p_{data}, p_g)$ with **1-Lipschitz** $D$ (enforced by **weight clipping** or **gradient penalty**) — fixes vanishing gradients on disjoint supports.
- ⭐ **Mode collapse** is the most common failure: $G$ produces low-diversity outputs. Diagnose with batch-to-batch variance of generated samples.
- ⭐ **DCGAN architectural rules:** strided/fractionally-strided convolutions, batch norm, ReLU/Tanh in $G$, LeakyReLU in $D$, no fully-connected hidden layers.
- ⭐ **Conditional GAN** adds $y$ to both $G$ and $D$: enables class-conditional generation, image-to-image translation, and text-to-image.
- ⭐ **Evaluation:** **Inception Score (IS)** — sharp + diverse predictions; **FID** — distance between Inception feature covariances. FID is the gold standard.
- ⭐ **Training loop:** alternate one SGD step on $D$, then one on $G$. Use **TTUR** (two-timescale update rule) — higher learning rate for $D$ than $G$ — for stability.
- ⭐ **Convergence criterion:** $D(x) \to 0.5$ for both real and fake samples, $G$ loss plateaus, and qualitative samples look realistic.
- ⭐ **Variants to know for KTU:** Vanilla GAN, DCGAN, Conditional GAN, LSGAN, WGAN, WGAN-GP, InfoGAN.
- ⭐ **Equations to memorize verbatim:** the minimax value, the optimal $D^{*}$, the JSD identity, the WGAN Kantorovich dual, and the FID formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: BERT
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study: BERT

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)** is a pre-trained deep language representation model introduced by Google Research in **2018** (Devlin et al., paper: "Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"). It is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers.

### Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

BERT is a multi-layer **Transformer Encoder** architecture that is **pre-trained** on a large unlabeled text corpus using two unsupervised tasks:
1. **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)** — predicting randomly masked tokens using bidirectional context.
2. **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)** — predicting whether one sentence follows another in the original text.

After pre-training, BERT can be **fine-tuned** with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of downstream NLP tasks such as Question Answering, Sentiment Classification, Named Entity Recognition, and Textual Entailment.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Omniscient Reader"**
> Imagine a brilliant student (BERT) who has read an entire library of millions of books before your exam. Unlike older models that read a sentence only left-to-right (like reading with one eye covered) or right-to-left, this student can look at **both sides** of any word simultaneously to understand its meaning in context.
>
> When you ask, *"The animal didn't cross the street because **it** was too tired,"* — a left-to-only model might be confused about what "it" refers to. But BERT sees **both directions** and instantly knows "it" = "the animal."

### Key Architectural Specifications

| Variant | Layers ($L$) | Hidden Size ($H$) | Attention Heads ($A$) | Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BERT-base** | **12** | **768** | **12** | ~110 Million |
| **BERT-large** | **24** | **1024** | **16** | ~340 Million |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> BERT-base uses **12** encoder layers, **768** hidden units, **12** attention heads, and ~**110M** parameters. BERT-large uses **24**, **1024**, **16**, and ~**340M** respectively.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BERT Architecture — Operational Breakdown

BERT is constructed purely from the **Encoder stack** of the original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017). The architecture consists of:

- **Input Layer** → Token, Segment, and Positional Embeddings summed.
- **Stack of $L$ identical Encoder blocks**, each containing:
  1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** sub-layer.
  2. **Add & Norm** (residual connection + layer normalization).
  3. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)**.
  4. **Add & Norm** (second residual + normalization).
- **Output Layer** → Sequence of contextualized token representations of dimension $H$.

## 2.2 Input Representation — The "Why" and "How"

Each input token's embedding is the **element-wise sum** of three embeddings:

$$
\text{Input Embedding} = \text{Token Embedding} + \text{Segment Embedding} + \text{Position Embedding}
$$

- **Token Embedding** — Uses **WordPiece** tokenizer with a vocabulary of ~**30,000** tokens. Special tokens: `[CLS]` (classification, always first) and `[SEP]` (sentence separator).
- **Segment Embedding** — Distinguishes Sentence A from Sentence B (values $0$ or $1$).
- **Position Embedding** — Learned positional vectors (max sequence length **512**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sum three embeddings?** Each captures a distinct signal: *what* the word is (token), *which sentence* it belongs to (segment), and *where* it is located (position).

## 2.3 Pre-Training Objective 1 — Masked Language Modeling (MLM)

- **15%** of the input WordPiece tokens are randomly chosen for prediction.
- Of those chosen tokens:
  - **80%** are replaced with `[MASK]`.
  - **10%** are replaced with a random token.
  - **10%** are kept unchanged.

The final hidden vectors corresponding to the masked positions are fed into a softmax output layer over the vocabulary to predict the original token.

$$
P(\text{token} \mid \text{context}) = \text{softmax}(W_O \cdot h_i + b_O)
$$

where $h_i$ is the final hidden state of the masked token $i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 80/10/10 split?** Replacing all masked tokens with `[MASK]` would create a mismatch between pre-training and fine-tuning (where `[MASK]` never appears). The random/replaced strategy forces BERT to maintain a **distribution-aware contextual representation** of every token.

## 2.4 Pre-Training Objective 2 — Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)

- For each pre-training example, two sentences A and B are sampled.
- **50%** of the time, B is the *actual* next sentence (label `IsNext`).
- **50%** of the time, B is a *random* sentence from the corpus (label `NotNext`).
- The `[CLS]` token's final hidden state $C \in \mathbb{R}^H$ is passed through a classification layer:

$$
P(\text{IsNext} \mid C) = \text{softmax}(W_N \cdot C)
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for BERT — commit them to memory for ESE answers.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Self-Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | $d_k$ = key dimension |
| 2 | Scaled Dot-Product scaling | $\text{scale factor} = \sqrt{d_k}$ | Prevents softmax saturation |
| 3 | Multi-Head Attention | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h)W^O$ | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$ |
| 4 | Feed-Forward Network | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ | Applied position-wise |
| 5 | Layer Normalization | $\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \odot \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Across feature dimension |
| 6 | MLM Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} \log P(w_i \mid x_{\setminus \mathcal{M}})$ | Sum over masked positions |
| 7 | NSP Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{NSP}} = -\log P(y \mid C)$ | Binary cross-entropy on `[CLS]` |
| 8 | Total Pre-training Loss | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{NSP}}$ | Joint optimization |
| 9 | Input Embedding Sum | $E_{\text{input}} = E_{\text{token}} + E_{\text{segment}} + E_{\text{position}}$ | Element-wise addition |
| 10 | Number of parameters (approx.) | BERT-base $\approx 110$M; BERT-large $\approx 340$M | Exam-favorite fact |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` when writing absolute values inside tables — the pipe symbol `\vert` is reserved by markdown. Example: $\vert x \vert$ inside a table cell.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

BERT revolutionized practical NLP deployment in:
- **Google Search** — Query understanding (replaced older keyword systems).
- **Healthcare** — Clinical text mining, biomedical NER (BioBERT, ClinicalBERT).
- **Customer Support** — Sentiment classification, intent detection.
- **Legal Tech** — Contract analysis, legal question answering.
- **Code Intelligence** — GitHub Copilot lineage uses Transformer-style encoders.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Self-Attention Output for a BERT Token

We derive the contextualized output vector for a single query position.

### Step 1 — Project inputs into Q, K, V

For an input sequence packed into matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ where $n$ = sequence length, $d$ = embedding dimension:

$$
Q = X W^Q, \qquad K = X W^K, \qquad V = X W^V
$$

where $W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$ and $W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v}$ are learnable projection matrices.

### Step 2 — Compute raw similarity scores

For each query position $i$, compute the dot product with every key:

$$
\text{score}(i, j) = Q_i \cdot K_j^{\top}
$$

where $Q_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k}$ is the $i$-th row of $Q$ and $K_j \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k}$ is the $j$-th row of $K$.

### Step 3 — Scale the scores

Divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to keep gradients stable (large dot products push softmax into saturation):

$$
\text{scaled}(i, j) = \frac{Q_i K_j^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply softmax to obtain attention weights

$$
\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q_i K_j^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q_i K_k^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)}
$$

The weights satisfy $\sum_{j} \alpha_{ij} = 1$ — a valid probability distribution.

### Step 5 — Weighted sum over values

$$
\text{head}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{ij} \, V_j
$$

### Step 6 — Combine into Multi-Head Attention

Run $h$ parallel heads, concatenate their outputs along the feature dimension, then project:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) &= \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \text{head}_2, \dots, \text{head}_h) \, W^O \\
\text{head}_i &= \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q,\; K W_i^K,\; V W_i^V)
\end{aligned}
$$

For BERT-base, $h = 12$, $d_k = d_v = d/h = 768 / 12 = 64$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Masked Language Modeling Loss

Suppose the set of masked positions is $\mathcal{M} = \{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_k\}$ and the true tokens at those positions are $w_{m_1}, \dots, w_{m_k}$.

### Step 1 — Pass the final hidden vector through a projection layer

$$
z_i = W_O \, h_i + b_O, \qquad i \in \mathcal{M}
$$

where $W_O \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times \vert V \vert}$, $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size (~30,522 for BERT-base).

### Step 2 — Convert to a probability distribution via softmax

$$
\hat{p}_{i,c} = \frac{\exp(z_{i,c})}{\sum_{c'=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(z_{i,c'})}
$$

### Step 3 — Take the negative log-likelihood

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} \log \hat{p}_{i,\, w_i}
$$

### Step 4 — Joint pre-training loss

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{NSP}}
$$

This scalar loss is back-propagated through **all $L$ encoder layers** to update every parameter.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pre-training Hyperparameters

> [!NOTE]
> These are **canonical** values used in the original BERT paper — examiners love to test them.

| Hyperparameter | BERT-base | BERT-large |
|---|---|---|
| Layers ($L$) | **12** | **24** |
| Hidden size ($H$) | **768** | **1024** |
| Attention heads ($A$) | **12** | **16** |
| Parameters | ~110 M | ~340 M |
| Max sequence length | **512** | **512** |
| Vocabulary size ($\vert V \vert$) | ~30,522 | ~30,522 |
| Batch size | 256 | 256 |
| Optimizer | Adam (lr = 1e-4) | Adam (lr = 1e-4) |
| Training steps | 1,000,000 | 1,000,000 |
| Mask probability | 15% | 15% |
| NSP 50/50 split | Yes | Yes |

## 3.4 Numerical Example — BERT-base Attention Head Dimension

Given $H = 768$ and $A = 12$:

$$
d_k = d_v = \frac{H}{A} = \frac{768}{12} = 64
$$

Total parameters in one attention head's projections:

$$
\underbrace{768 \times 64}_{W^Q} + \underbrace{768 \times 64}_{W^K} + \underbrace{768 \times 64}_{W^V} = 3 \times 49{,}152 = 147{,}456
$$

Adding the output projection $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{768 \times 768}$:

$$
\text{Per-head total} \approx 147{,}456 + 768^2 = 147{,}456 + 589{,}824 = 737{,}280
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exact parameter count is approximately **737,280** for the multi-head attention block in a single BERT-base layer. This kind of numeric drill-down is a frequent **14-mark question** topic.

## 3.5 Why BERT is Bidirectional — Architectural Argument

Traditional left-to-right language models (e.g., GPT, ELMo's forward LSTM) factorize probability as:

$$
P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{t=1}^{n} P(w_t \mid w_1, \dots, w_{t-1})
$$

BERT instead trains a **denoising autoencoder** objective that predicts $w_i$ from *both* directions simultaneously, allowing every layer to use left AND right context:

$$
P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1}, w_{i+1}, \dots, w_n) \quad \text{(bidirectional)}
$$

This is the fundamental conceptual advance that gives BERT its superior contextual understanding.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BERT Overall Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[WordPiece Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Token IDs with CLS and SEP]
    C --> D[Token Embedding Lookup]
    C --> E[Segment Embedding Lookup]
    C --> F[Positional Embedding Lookup]
    D --> G[Element Wise Sum]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Encoder Block 1]
    H --> I[Encoder Block 2]
    I --> J[Encoder Block Dots]
    J --> K[Encoder Block L]
    K --> L[Contextualized Output Vectors]
    L --> M[MLM Prediction Head]
    L --> N[NSP Prediction Head on CLS]
    M --> O[Pre-training Loss]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Backprop Update All Weights]
    P --> Q[Fine-tuned BERT Model]
    Q --> R[Task A QA]
    Q --> S[Task B Classification]
    Q --> T[Task C NER]
```

## 4.2 Single BERT Encoder Block — Internal Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Hidden States H] --> B[Multi-Head Self Attention]
    B --> C[Add and Norm 1]
    A --> C
    C --> D[Feed Forward Network FFN]
    D --> E[Add and Norm 2]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Output Hidden States H plus 1]
```

## 4.3 Fine-Tuning Architecture for Downstream Tasks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pre-trained BERT Weights] --> B[Add Task-Specific Layer]
    B --> C{Choose Task}
    C --> D[Sequence Classification]
    C --> E[Token Classification NER]
    C --> F[Question Answering]
    C --> G[Multiple Choice]
    D --> H[Softmax over Classes]
    E --> I[Per-Token Softmax]
    F --> J[Start End Span Prediction]
    G --> K[Argmax over Choices]
    H --> L[Fine-tuned Predictions]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.4 Embedding Composition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Token] --> B[Token Embedding E token]
    C[Segment ID 0 or 1] --> D[Segment Embedding E segment]
    E[Position Index 0 to 511] --> F[Position Embedding E position]
    B --> G[Element Wise Sum]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Final Input Vector]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `A`, `B`, `nodeA`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone IDs. Labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only — no markdown formatting inside double quotes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define BERT. List its two pre-training objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)** is a pre-trained language representation model that uses a multi-layer Transformer encoder architecture to produce deep bidirectional contextual embeddings of text. (1 mark)

The **two pre-training objectives** are: (2 marks)

1. **Masked Language Modeling (MLM):** Randomly masking **15%** of the input tokens and training the model to predict the original token based on bidirectional context. Of the masked tokens, 80% are replaced with `[MASK]`, 10% with random tokens, and 10% are left unchanged.
2. **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP):** Given a pair of sentences (A, B), the model predicts whether B is the actual next sentence following A in the corpus, using the `[CLS]` token's representation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is the significance of the `[CLS]` token in BERT?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `[CLS]` (classification) token is a special token **prepended** to every input sequence during pre-training and fine-tuning. (1 mark)

**Significance:** (2 marks)

1. Its final hidden state $C \in \mathbb{R}^H$ serves as the **pooled aggregate representation** of the entire input sequence.
2. During pre-training, it is used by the **NSP head** to predict whether sentence B follows sentence A.
3. During fine-tuning for **sentence-level classification** tasks (e.g., sentiment analysis), a classification layer is added on top of $C$ to produce the final class label.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget that `[CLS]` is **only** useful for sequence-level (sentence-level) tasks. For token-level tasks like NER or QA, **every** token's output is used, not just `[CLS]`. Mentioning this distinction can earn bonus marks.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Each Part-B question offers two alternatives. Answer **either** (a) OR (b) wholly. We provide both alternatives below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Explain the BERT architecture in detail. Discuss its input representation, encoder block components, and the Masked Language Modeling (MLM) pre-training objective with the 80/10/10 strategy.**

#### (a) BERT Architecture and Input Representation (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

BERT consists of a **stack of Transformer encoder blocks**. For BERT-base, there are **L = 12** encoder layers, each with hidden size **H = 768** and **A = 12** attention heads. (1 mark)

**Input Representation** (3 marks):
Each input token's final embedding is the element-wise sum of three components:
- **Token Embeddings** from a **WordPiece tokenizer** with vocabulary size ~30,522. Special tokens `[CLS]` and `[SEP]` are inserted to mark sequence start and sentence boundaries.
- **Segment Embeddings** to distinguish sentence pairs — values 0 for sentence A and 1 for sentence B.
- **Position Embeddings** — learned vectors of length up to 512 to encode token order.

**Encoder Block Components** (3 marks):
Each of the $L$ encoder layers contains:
1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** — computes attention in parallel across $A$ heads, each with dimension $d_k = H/A = 64$.
2. **Add & Norm** — residual connection followed by layer normalization.
3. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** — two linear transformations with a ReLU/GELU activation in between: $\text{FFN}(x) = W_2 \cdot \text{GELU}(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2$.
4. **Add & Norm** — second residual + normalization.

#### (b) MLM Pre-Training Objective and 80/10/10 Strategy (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

In **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)**, the model is trained to predict **randomly masked** tokens from their bidirectional context, simulating a `fill-in-the-blank` task. (1 mark)

**Masking Procedure** (3 marks):
- **15%** of the WordPiece tokens in each sequence are selected for prediction.
- Of these selected tokens, the **80/10/10 strategy** is applied:
  - **80%** are replaced with the `[MASK]` token.
  - **10%** are replaced with a **random** token from the vocabulary.
  - **10%** are kept **unchanged** (original token retained).

**Why the 80/10/10 split?** (2 marks)
If 100% of masked tokens were replaced with `[MASK]`, the model would learn that the masked token is *always* `[MASK]`. During fine-tuning, `[MASK]` tokens do not appear, causing a **train-test mismatch**. The 10% random and 10% unchanged tokens force BERT to maintain rich contextual representations for *every* token, not just `[MASK]` positions.

**Loss Function:**
$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} \log \frac{\exp(W_O h_i + b_O)_{w_i}}{\sum_{c} \exp(W_O h_i + b_O)_c}
$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ is the set of masked positions. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]

**Discuss the Self-Attention and Multi-Head Attention mechanisms used in BERT. Derive the attention output for a single token, and explain how BERT is fine-tuned for downstream tasks like Question Answering.**

#### (a) Self-Attention and Multi-Head Attention Derivation (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Linear Projections** (1 mark): For input $X$, learn matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V$ and compute:
$$
Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V
$$

**Step 2 — Scaled Dot-Product Attention** (3 marks):
$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$
The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling prevents softmax saturation when dot-product magnitudes grow large.

**Step 3 — Multi-Head Combination** (2 marks):
$$
\text{MHA}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) W^O
$$
where each $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V)$. For BERT-base, $h = 12$ and $d_k = 64$.

**Step 4 — Output for a single token $i$** (1 mark):
$$
\text{head}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{ij} V_j, \quad \alpha_{ij} = \text{softmax}_j\!\left(\frac{Q_i K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)
$$

#### (b) Fine-Tuning BERT for Question Answering (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Add a QA-specific head** (2 marks): BERT's pre-trained encoder is kept, and two new trainable vectors $W_S, W_E \in \mathbb{R}^H$ are introduced. The final hidden vector of every token $T_i$ is scored for being the **start** or **end** of the answer span:

$$
P_{\text{start}}(i) = \frac{\exp(W_S \cdot T_i)}{\sum_j \exp(W_S \cdot T_j)}, \quad P_{\text{end}}(i) = \frac{\exp(W_E \cdot T_i)}{\sum_j \exp(W_E \cdot T_j)}
$$

**Step 2 — Input format** (2 marks): The input is constructed as `[CLS] Question [SEP] Context [SEP]`. The segment embedding distinguishes question tokens from context tokens.

**Step 3 — Training objective** (2 marks): The cross-entropy loss for both start and end positions is minimized jointly. All BERT weights plus $W_S, W_E$ are updated via back-propagation (typically with a small learning rate like $2 \times 10^{-5}$).

**Step 4 — Inference** (1 mark): The span $(i^*, j^*)$ with maximum $P_{\text{start}}(i) \cdot P_{\text{end}}(j)$ subject to $i \le j$ is selected as the predicted answer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse BERT with GPT.** BERT is **encoder-only** (bidirectional); GPT is **decoder-only** (unidirectional left-to-right). Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this mix-up.
> 2. **Always specify the 80/10/10 split** when explaining MLM — vague answers like "tokens are masked" lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mention WordPiece tokenizer** explicitly in input representation answers; generic "tokenization" loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Show the formula $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{NSP}}$** for full pre-training loss — this is a high-frequency 2-mark direct question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for BERT**

- [x] **BERT = Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers**, introduced by Google in **2018** (Devlin et al.).
- [x] BERT uses only the **Encoder stack** of the original Transformer — it is **NOT** an encoder-decoder model.
- [x] Two model sizes: **BERT-base (12 layers, 768 hidden, 12 heads, ~110M params)** and **BERT-large (24, 1024, 16, ~340M)**.
- [x] Input embedding = **Token + Segment + Position** (element-wise sum).
- [x] Special tokens: **`[CLS]`** (classification start) and **`[SEP]`** (sentence separator).
- [x] Tokenization: **WordPiece**, vocabulary size ~**30,522**.
- [x] Pre-training task 1 — **MLM (Masked Language Modeling)**: 15% tokens masked; **80/10/10 split** among `[MASK]` / random / unchanged.
- [x] Pre-training task 2 — **NSP (Next Sentence Prediction)**: 50/50 binary classification on `[CLS]`.
- [x] Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula: $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$.
- [x] Multi-Head Attention = $h$ parallel heads concatenated and projected by $W^O$.
- [x] Per-head dimension in BERT-base: $d_k = H/A = 768/12 = 64$.
- [x] Each encoder layer = Multi-Head Self-Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm.
- [x] FFN: two linear layers with **GELU** activation in BERT (ReLU in original Transformer).
- [x] Layer Normalization (not Batch Norm) is used in Transformer/BERT.
- [x] BERT is **bidirectional** — sees left AND right context simultaneously (unlike GPT).
- [x] Fine-tuning adds **task-specific output layer(s)**; all (or most) BERT weights are updated with small learning rate (~2e-5).
- [x] Downstream tasks: Sequence Classification, Token Classification (NER), Question Answering (SQuAD), Multiple Choice, etc.
- [x] **BERT ≠ GPT** — most critical distinction: BERT = encoder-only, bidirectional; GPT = decoder-only, unidirectional.
- [x] Total pre-training loss: $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{NSP}}$.
- [x] Max sequence length: **512** tokens.
- [x] Common exam pitfalls: confusing BERT/GPT, omitting 80/10/10 split, forgetting WordPiece, mixing encoder/decoder roles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Social Media Sentiment Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT695 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Social Media Sentiment Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Social Media Sentiment Analysis (SMA)**, also known as **Opinion Mining**, is a specialized subdomain of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** and **Affective Computing** that employs deep learning architectures to systematically identify, extract, quantify, and classify the affective states, opinions, attitudes, and emotional polarity embedded within user-generated content on digital social platforms (Twitter/X, Facebook, Instagram, Reddit, YouTube, LinkedIn, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Sentiment Analysis is the computational study of opinions, sentiments, and emotions expressed in text. In the deep learning context, it involves training neural networks (RNN, LSTM, Bi-LSTM, Transformer-based models) on labeled corpora to automatically learn hierarchical linguistic representations for polarity classification (positive, negative, neutral, or fine-grained emotions like joy, anger, sadness, fear, surprise, disgust).

The standard classification granularity in KTU-aligned problems includes:

- **Binary Polarity Classification:** Positive vs. Negative
- **3-Class Polarity:** Positive, Negative, Neutral
- **Fine-Grained (5-Star):** Very Positive → Very Negative
- **Emotion Classification:** Joy, Anger, Sadness, Fear, Surprise, Disgust (Ekman's 6 basic emotions)
- **Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA):** Sentiment tied to specific aspects/features of an entity

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍽️ The Restaurant Review Analogy:** Imagine you own a restaurant and receive 50,000 reviews on Google, Zomato, and Twitter. Reading each one manually would take months. Now imagine a highly intelligent assistant who reads ALL 50,000 reviews in seconds, understands slang ("the biryani was 🔥🔥", "worst ambienc tbh"), detects sarcasm ("oh great, another 2-hour wait, just loved that 🙄"), and then tells you: *"Sir, 72% of customers are happy with the food, 45% complained about waiting time, and there's a spike in negative sentiment every weekend."* That intelligent assistant is **Social Media Sentiment Analysis powered by Deep Learning.**

### 1.3 Why Deep Learning for Social Media?

Traditional lexicon-based methods (VADER, SentiWordNet) and classical ML (Naive Bayes, SVM) struggle because social media text contains:

- **Noisy Tokens:** Emojis, hashtags, mentions, URLs
- **Shorthand & Slang:** "lit", "goat", "mid", "bussin"
- **Sarcasm & Irony:** "Thanks for the wonderful service 👎"
- **Code-mixing:** "Yeh product bahut mast hai bhai"
- **Context Dependence:** Same word, different meaning in different contexts
- **Class Imbalance:** Negative tweets often overshadow positive ones during crises

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Deep learning models automatically learn **contextual embeddings** (e.g., BERT) that capture word meaning based on surrounding context, making them vastly superior to bag-of-words or static embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) for sentiment tasks.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU-Exam Frequently Asked)

| Metric | Formula (for Binary) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $(TP+TN)/(TP+TN+FP+FN)$ | Balanced datasets |
| **Precision** | $TP/(TP+FP)$ | When false positives are costly |
| **Recall** | $TP/(TP+FN)$ | When false negatives are costly |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Imbalanced classes (most common in SMA) |
| **Macro-F1** | Average of per-class F1 | Equal weight to all classes |
| **ROC-AUC** | Area under TPR vs FPR | Threshold-independent evaluation |

Where **TP** = True Positive, **TN** = True Negative, **FP** = False Positive, **FN** = False Negative.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sentiment Analysis Pipeline (KTU-Favorite Diagram Topic)

The end-to-end SMA pipeline consists of **six sequential stages**:

1. **Data Acquisition (Crawling/API):** Twitter API v2, Reddit API, web scraping (BeautifulSoup, Selenium), or use pre-built datasets like **Sentiment140**, **SemEval-2014**, **IMDB**, **GoEmotions**.
2. **Text Preprocessing & Normalization:**
   - Lowercasing, punctuation removal
   - Tokenization (WordPiece for BERT, BPE for GPT)
   - Stop-word removal (context-dependent: "not" should NOT be removed)
   - Stemming / Lemmatization
   - Handling emojis (convert to text or use emoji embeddings)
   - Hashtag segmentation ("#ThisIsGreat" → "this is great")
   - Slang/abbreviation expansion ("u" → "you", "gr8" → "great")
3. **Feature Representation (Embeddings):**
   - Static: Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText
   - Contextual: BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT, ALBERT
4. **Model Architecture:** RNN / LSTM / Bi-LSTM / GRU / Transformer
5. **Training & Optimization:** Backpropagation through time (BPTT), Adam optimizer, cross-entropy loss
6. **Evaluation & Deployment:** F1-score, confusion matrix, real-time inference via REST API

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations

### 2.2.1 Embedding Layer

Each token $w_i$ in vocabulary $V$ is mapped to a dense vector $\mathbf{e}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ where $d$ is the embedding dimension (typically $d = 100, 200, 300$ for static; $d = 768$ for BERT-base).

$$
\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{e}_T] \in \mathbb{R}^{T \times d}
$$

Here $T$ is the sequence length (number of tokens in a tweet).

### 2.2.2 LSTM Cell Equations (KTU-Most-Tested)

The **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** network is the workhorse for SMA because it captures long-range dependencies. Each LSTM cell has three gates:

$$
\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f) \quad \text{(Forget Gate)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i) \quad \text{(Input Gate)}
$$

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C) \quad \text{(Candidate Cell State)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t \quad \text{(Cell State Update)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o) \quad \text{(Output Gate)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t) \quad \text{(Hidden State Output)}
$$

Where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function, $\tanh$ is the hyperbolic tangent, and $\odot$ denotes element-wise (Hadamard) product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LSTM for Sentiment?** Standard RNNs suffer from **vanishing gradient** problem. LSTM's gating mechanism (forget/input/output) allows the network to remember important sentiment-bearing words (e.g., "not", "never", "love") over long contexts, which is critical when a tweet's sentiment depends on a negation word 20 tokens earlier.

### 2.2.3 Bi-LSTM (Bidirectional LSTM)

For social media, context flows in **both directions**. A Bi-LSTM processes the sequence forward ($\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t$) and backward ($\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t$):

$$
\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t = \text{LSTM}_{fwd}(\mathbf{x}_t, \overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t-1})
$$

$$
\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t = \text{LSTM}_{bwd}(\mathbf{x}_t, \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t-1})
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = [\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t \oplus \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{2u}
$$

where $\oplus$ is vector concatenation and $u$ is the number of hidden units per direction.

### 2.2.4 Output Layer — Softmax Classifier

For multi-class sentiment (e.g., 3 classes: positive, negative, neutral):

$$
\mathbf{z} = W_y \mathbf{h}_T + \mathbf{b}_y \in \mathbb{R}^{C}
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{z}) = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_k}}, \quad c = 1, 2, \ldots, C
$$

For **binary** sentiment (positive vs. negative), a single sigmoid neuron is used:

$$
P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{h}_T + b)
$$

### 2.2.5 Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})
$$

where $N$ is the batch size, $C$ is the number of classes, $y_{i,c}$ is the ground-truth one-hot label, and $\hat{y}_{i,c}$ is the predicted probability.

### 2.2.6 Attention Mechanism (Bonus — Important for KTU)

The attention mechanism allows the model to focus on sentiment-relevant words. The attention score $\alpha_t$ for hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ is computed as:

$$
u_t = \tanh(W_a \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_a)
$$

$$
\alpha_t = \frac{\exp(u_t^\top \mathbf{u}_s)}{\sum_{k=1}^{T} \exp(u_k^\top \mathbf{u}_s)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{c} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t \mathbf{h}_t
$$

where $\mathbf{u}_s$ is a learnable context vector and $\mathbf{c}$ is the final context vector fed to the classifier.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | Gate activations, binary output |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$ | Cell state squashing |
| **Softmax** | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class probability |
| **Cross-Entropy** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Loss for classification |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Balanced metric |
| **LSTM Forget Gate** | $\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(W_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)$ | Controls memory retention |
| **Embedding Lookup** | $\mathbf{e}_t = E[w_t]$ | Maps token to dense vector |
| **Attention Score** | $\alpha_t = \frac{\exp(u_t)}{\sum_k \exp(u_k)}$ | Word importance weighting |
| **Gradient Clipping** | $\mathbf{g} = \min(1, \theta/\vert \mathbf{g} \vert) \cdot \mathbf{g}$ | Prevents exploding gradients |
| **Dropout** | $\mathbf{h}_{drop} = \mathbf{h} \odot \mathbf{m}, \; m_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(1-p)$ | Regularization |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Brand Monitoring:** Coca-Cola, Nike track sentiment to gauge campaign success
> - **Political Analysis:** Predicting election outcomes from Twitter sentiment
> - **Financial Markets:** Sentiment-driven stock prediction (FinBERT models)
> - **Crisis Management:** Detecting mental health distress signals (suicide prevention)
> - **Customer Service:** Auto-routing negative tweets to priority queues
> - **Product Development:** Mining feature-level complaints from reviews
