# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Forward Pass Through a Single LSTM Cell for Sentiment

### Problem Setup
Consider the tweet: **"The movie was not good"** (Negative sentiment).
After tokenization: `["The", "movie", "was", "not", "good"]`, $T = 5$.
Embedding dimension $d = 4$, hidden units $u = 3$.

Let the embedding matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times 4}$ produce:

$$
\mathbf{x}_1 = [0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4]^\top \quad \text{(The)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}_2 = [0.5, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3]^\top \quad \text{(movie)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}_3 = [0.0, 0.4, 0.1, 0.2]^\top \quad \text{(was)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}_4 = [0.3, 0.0, 0.5, 0.1]^\top \quad \text{(not)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}_5 = [0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1]^\top \quad \text{(good)}
$$

### Step 1: Initialization
At $t=0$, initialize hidden and cell state to zeros:

$$
\mathbf{h}_0 = [0, 0, 0]^\top, \quad \mathbf{C}_0 = [0, 0, 0]^\top
$$

### Step 2: Gate Computations at $t=1$ (token "The")
Concatenate input and previous hidden:

$$
[\mathbf{h}_0, \mathbf{x}_1] = [0,0,0,0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4]^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{7}
$$

Compute the forget gate (assuming weight vector $\mathbf{w}_f = [0.1, 0.2, 0.1, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1, 0.1]$ and bias $b_f = 0$):

$$
z_f = \mathbf{w}_f^\top [\mathbf{h}_0, \mathbf{x}_1] = 0.1(0.1) + 0.2(0.2) + 0.1(0.3) + 0.3(0.4) = 0.19
$$

$$
\mathbf{f}_1 = \sigma(0.19) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.19}} \approx 0.5474
$$

Apply sigmoid to all gates, then compute the new cell state and hidden state. This process repeats for $t = 2, 3, 4, 5$.

### Step 3: Final Output at $t=5$ (token "good")
After 5 timesteps, suppose the final hidden state is:

$$
\mathbf{h}_5 = [0.62, -0.15, 0.43]^\top
$$

### Step 4: Softmax Classification
For 3-class sentiment, assume the output weight matrix $W_y \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ and bias $\mathbf{b}_y$ produce logits:

$$
\mathbf{z} = W_y \mathbf{h}_5 + \mathbf{b}_y = [1.2, -0.3, 0.5]^\top
$$

Apply softmax:

$$
\hat{y}_1 = \frac{e^{1.2}}{e^{1.2} + e^{-0.3} + e^{0.5}} = \frac{3.320}{3.320 + 0.741 + 1.649} = \frac{3.320}{5.710} \approx 0.5814
$$

$$
\hat{y}_2 = \frac{e^{-0.3}}{5.710} \approx 0.1297
$$

$$
\hat{y}_3 = \frac{e^{0.5}}{5.710} \approx 0.2888
$$

### Step 5: Cross-Entropy Loss
Ground truth is "Negative" → one-hot: $\mathbf{y} = [0, 1, 0]^\top$

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{3} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c) = -\log(0.1297) \approx 2.043
$$

This loss is backpropagated through time (BPTT) to update all weights.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) Gradient

The gradient of the loss with respect to hidden state at time $t$ depends on all future time steps:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}\bigg\vert_{direct} + \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BPTT is hard:** For long tweets ($T > 100$), the gradient chain grows exponentially. LSTM's additive cell state update (vs. RNN's multiplicative) is what solves the vanishing gradient issue.

## 3.3 Gradient Clipping Derivation

To prevent exploding gradients, clip the gradient norm to threshold $\theta$:

$$
\text{If } \vert \mathbf{g} \vert > \theta: \quad \mathbf{g} \leftarrow \frac{\theta}{\vert \mathbf{g} \vert} \cdot \mathbf{g}
$$

$$
\text{Else: } \quad \mathbf{g} \leftarrow \mathbf{g}
$$

This ensures the gradient magnitude never exceeds $\theta$ (commonly $\theta = 5$).

## 3.4 Complete Forward Pass for Bi-LSTM + Attention

$$
\mathbf{x}_t \xrightarrow{\text{Embedding}} \mathbf{e}_t \xrightarrow{\text{Bi-LSTM}} \mathbf{h}_t = [\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t]
$$

$$
u_t = \tanh(W_a \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_a)
$$

$$
\alpha_t = \frac{\exp(u_t^\top \mathbf{u}_s)}{\sum_{k=1}^{T} \exp(u_k^\top \mathbf{u}_s)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{c} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t \mathbf{h}_t
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \text{softmax}(W_y \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{b}_y)
$$

This is the standard architecture used in KTU-level research problems on SMA.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> **Q:** Define Social Media Sentiment Analysis. List any four challenges specific to sentiment analysis on social media text.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Social Media Sentiment Analysis is a Natural Language Processing task that uses deep learning models to automatically classify the polarity (positive, negative, neutral) of user-generated content on social media platforms.

**Four Challenges:**
1. **Use of Sarcasm and Irony:** "Oh fantastic, my flight got cancelled again" expresses negative sentiment through positive words.
2. **Code-mixed and Multilingual Text:** Users often mix languages (Hinglish, Spanglish), making tokenization difficult.
3. **Emojis and Emoticons:** 😍 conveys love; standard NLP pipelines strip these out, losing sentiment signal.
4. **Class Imbalance:** During crises or product launches, negative tweets often dominate, skewing model predictions.

*(Examiner expects 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for challenges OR 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for challenges. Be precise.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**

> **Q:** Why are LSTM networks preferred over vanilla RNNs for sentiment analysis? Mention any two reasons.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Solving Vanishing Gradient Problem:** Vanilla RNNs suffer from exponentially decaying gradients over long sequences, making them unable to learn long-range dependencies (e.g., "not good" where the negation is 2-3 words before the sentiment word). LSTMs use additive cell state updates with gating mechanisms that preserve gradient flow.

2. **Selective Memory via Gates:** LSTM has three gates (forget, input, output) that learn to retain sentiment-relevant information (e.g., "love", "hate", "never", "not") and discard irrelevant noise (e.g., stop words, filler words), which is critical for accurate polarity classification.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete pipeline of a deep learning-based social media sentiment analysis system. List and briefly describe each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The deep learning-based SMA pipeline consists of **six stages**:

**Stage 1 — Data Acquisition:** Collect tweets/reviews using Twitter API v2, Reddit API, or web scrapers. Use labeled datasets like Sentiment140 (1.6M tweets) or GoEmotions (58K Reddit comments with 27 emotions).

**Stage 2 — Text Preprocessing:**
- Remove URLs, mentions (@user), and HTML tags
- Convert emojis to text (e.g., 😊 → "happy_face")
- Tokenize using WordPiece (BERT) or BPE
- Handle negation markers ("not", "no", "never") with special tokens

**Stage 3 — Embedding:** Convert tokens to dense vectors. Use BERT contextual embeddings (768-dim) for state-of-the-art results, or GloVe (300-dim) for faster training.

**Stage 4 — Model Architecture:** Feed embeddings into Bi-LSTM with attention, or fine-tune BERT directly. Bi-LSTM captures bidirectional context.

**Stage 5 — Training:** Use cross-entropy loss, Adam optimizer (lr=1e-3 to 1e-5), dropout (0.3-0.5), gradient clipping (θ=5), early stopping.

**Stage 6 — Evaluation & Deployment:** Compute F1-score, accuracy, confusion matrix. Deploy as Flask/FastAPI REST endpoint for real-time inference.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the visual.)*

---

> **Q (b)** Derive the equations for an LSTM cell. Explain how the forget gate helps in retaining long-term sentiment context in tweets. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The LSTM cell computes the following at each time step $t$:

$$
\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(W_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)
$$

$$
\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma(W_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i)
$$

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh(W_C [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C)
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t
$$

$$
\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma(W_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o)
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)
$$

**Role of Forget Gate in Sentiment Retention:**

The forget gate $\mathbf{f}_t \in [0,1]^u$ acts as a learnable mask over the previous cell state. When the model encounters a sentiment-bearing word like "not" or "love" early in the tweet, the forget gate learns to output values close to 1, preserving that information in $\mathbf{C}_t$ across many time steps. Conversely, for neutral tokens like "the", "a", "is", the gate outputs near 0, flushing them from memory.

For example, in the tweet "I really did not enjoy the movie at all":
- At $t=$"not", the input gate strongly writes the negation signal into $\mathbf{C}_t$
- The forget gate at subsequent tokens ("enjoy", "the", "movie", "at", "all") outputs ~1, preserving the negation
- This allows the final hidden state $\mathbf{h}_T$ to correctly represent the negative sentiment despite the presence of the word "enjoy" (which is positive in isolation)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**

> **Q (a)** Explain the architecture of a Bi-LSTM model with attention mechanism for sentiment classification. How does attention improve over vanilla Bi-LSTM? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Bi-LSTM with Attention Architecture:**

The input tweet is first tokenized into $T$ tokens, embedded via an embedding layer, then processed in **both forward and backward directions** by separate LSTM networks:

$$
\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t = \text{LSTM}_{fwd}(\mathbf{x}_t, \overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t-1})
$$

$$
\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t = \text{LSTM}_{bwd}(\mathbf{x}_t, \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t-1})
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = [\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{2u}
$$

The concatenated hidden states $\mathbf{h}_t$ for $t = 1, \ldots, T$ are then passed through the attention layer:

$$
u_t = \tanh(W_a \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_a)
$$

$$
\alpha_t = \frac{\exp(u_t^\top \mathbf{u}_s)}{\sum_{k=1}^{T} \exp(u_k^\top \mathbf{u}_s)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{c} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t \mathbf{h}_t
$$

The context vector $\mathbf{c}$ is fed to a dense layer followed by softmax for final classification.

**How Attention Improves Over Vanilla Bi-LSTM:**

In a vanilla Bi-LSTM, only the **last hidden state** $\mathbf{h}_T$ is used for classification, which creates an **information bottleneck** — for long tweets, earlier sentiment-bearing words are "forgotten." Attention solves this by computing a **weighted sum of all hidden states**, where weights $\alpha_t$ are learned to highlight the most sentiment-relevant words. For example, in "The battery life is amazing, but the camera is terrible", attention will assign high weights to "amazing" and "terrible", allowing the model to handle mixed/aspect-based sentiments effectively.

---

> **Q (b)** A sentiment classifier predicts 150 True Positives, 30 False Positives, 20 False Negatives, and 800 True Negatives. Calculate Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Comment on the model's performance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $TP = 150$, $FP = 30$, $FN = 20$, $TN = 800$
- Total $N = 150 + 30 + 20 + 800 = 1000$

**Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{150 + 800}{1000} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95 = 95\%
$$

**Precision:**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{150}{150 + 30} = \frac{150}{180} \approx 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

**Recall:**

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{150}{150 + 20} = \frac{150}{170} \approx 0.8824 = 88.24\%
$$

**F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8333 \times 0.8824}{0.8333 + 0.8824} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7353}{1.7157} \approx 0.8571
$$

So $F_1 \approx 85.71\%$.

**Comments on Performance:**
- The model achieves a high accuracy of **95%** because the dataset is imbalanced (800 TN out of 1000). Accuracy alone is misleading here.
- Precision (83.33%) and Recall (88.24%) give a more realistic picture.
- F1-Score of **85.71%** indicates a good balance between precision and recall, suitable for production deployment.
- However, since the negative class dominates, we should also compute **Macro-F1** by computing per-class metrics for both positive and negative classes to ensure the model performs well on the minority (positive) class.

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Softmax step:** Many students write the LSTM equations correctly but skip the final classification head (softmax/cross-entropy). Always end with a softmax output.
> 2. **Confusing Softmax with Sigmoid:** Use **softmax for multi-class** (3+ classes), **sigmoid for binary**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 3. **Not explaining the role of gates:** Writing LSTM equations without explaining *why* forget/input/output gates are needed (vanishing gradient, selective memory) loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing precision/recall formula:** Mixing up $TP/(TP+FP)$ and $TP/(TP+FN)$ is a 1-mark penalty in calculations.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** "With neat block diagram" questions REQUIRE a diagram. Without it, expect a 2-mark penalty.
> 6. **Static vs. Contextual embeddings:** Calling BERT a "static embedding" loses 1 mark — BERT is **contextual**; Word2Vec/GloVe are static.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 Quick-Fire Revision Points (Must-Memorize for KTU):**
>
> - **SMA Definition:** NLP + Deep Learning → classify polarity of social media text
> - **Three-class output:** Positive / Negative / Neutral
> - **Pipeline:** Data → Preprocess → Embed → Model → Train → Evaluate → Deploy
> - **LSTM has 3 gates:** Forget ($\mathbf{f}_t$), Input ($\mathbf{i}_t$), Output ($\mathbf{o}_t$)
> - **Cell state equation:** $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$
> - **Hidden state equation:** $\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$
> - **Bi-LSTM output:** Concatenation $\mathbf{h}_t = [\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t]$
> - **Softmax:** $\hat{y}_c = e^{z_c} / \sum_k e^{z_k}$ (multi-class)
> - **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(x) = 1/(1+e^{-x})$ (binary or gate)
> - **Cross-Entropy Loss:** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$
> - **F1-Score:** $F_1 = 2PR/(P+R)$ — best metric for imbalanced sentiment data
> - **Attention score:** $\alpha_t = \exp(u_t) / \sum_k \exp(u_k)$ — weights sentiment-relevant words
> - **Challenges:** Sarcasm, code-mixing, emojis, class imbalance, short text
> - **State-of-the-art models:** BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT fine-tuned on GoEmotions/Sentiment140
> - **Datasets to remember:** Sentiment140, IMDB, SemEval-2014, GoEmotions
> - **Hyperparameters:** Embedding dim $d=300$ (GloVe) or $768$ (BERT), LSTM units $u=128$–$256$, dropout $p=0.3$–$0.5$, learning rate $1e-3$ (LSTM) or $2e-5$ (BERT)
